APPENDIX  C —  COMPLAINT  FOR
DECLARATORY,  INJUNCTIVE,
AND  OTHER  RELIEF
DATED  JULY  17, 2000
AND  FILED  JULY  19, 2000

IN  THE  UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
FOR  THE  WESTERN  DISTRICT  OF  WASHINGTON
AT  SEATTLE

No.  C00-1205P

PARENTS  INVOLVED  IN  COMMUNITY  SCHOOLS,
a  Washington  nonprofit  corporation,

Plaintiff,

V.

SEATTLE  SCHOOL  DISTRICT  NO.  1, a  political
subdivision  of the  State  of Washington;  JOSEPH
OLCHEFSKE,  in  his  official  capacity  as  superintendent;
BARBARA  SCHAAD-LAMPHERE,  in  her  official
capacity  as  President  of the  Board  of Directors  of Seattle
Public  Schools;  and  DONALD  NIELSON,  in  his  official
capacity  as  Vice  President  of the  Board  of Directors  of
Seattle  Public  Schools;  STEVEN  BROWN;  JAN
KUMASAKA;  MICHAEL  PRESTON;  BARBARA
PETERSON;  and  NANCY  WALDMAN,  in  their  official
capacities  as  members  of the  Board  of Directors,

Defendants.

29a

Appendix  C

COMPLAINT  FOR  DECLARATORY,  INJUNCTIVE,
AND  OTHER  RELIEF

I.  NATURE  OF  THE  CASE

This  suit  is  brought  by  Parents  Involved  in
1.
Community  Schools  (“Parents”  or  “PICS”)  challenging  the
defendants’  impermissible  use  of  race  in  deciding  which
students  may  attend  specific  high schools  in the Seattle  Public
School  system.  PICS  is  comprised  of  parents  from
neighborhoods  around  the  city whose  children  have  been  or
will  likely  be  denied  admission  to  the  high  schools  of their
choice  because  of their  race.  Many of these  parents  have  been
dedicated  supporters  of  Seattle  public  schools,  including
some  who  have  volunteered  hundreds  of hours  every  year  in
support  of  its  projects  over  the  past  10  years.  The  school
district’s  policy  of making  many  of  its  student-admission
decisions  based  on  race  violates  both  federal  and  state  law.
This  suit  seeks  to  stop  that  unlawful  and  discriminatory
practice.  PICS  requests  (1)  a  preliminary  injunction
prohibiting  defendants  from  using  race  as  a factor  in student
admissions  in  high  schools  pending  the  resolution  of  this
lawsuit;  (2)  a  declaratory  judgment  that  defendants’  race-
based  student  admissions  plan  for  high  schools  violates
federal  and  state  law  and  (3)  an  injunction  permanently
prohibiting  defendants  from  using  race  as  a  factor  in student
admissions  plans  in  high  schools.

Il.  PARTIES

2.
Parents  Involved  in  Community  Schools  ts  a
Washington  nonprofit  corporation  whose  stated  purpose  ts

30a

Appendix  C

to promote  the well-being  of community  public  schools  and,
to  that  end,  to  seek  school  placement  criteria  that  comply
with  the  laws  requiring  equal  treatment.

3.
PICS’  Board  of  Directors  and  members  include
parents  whose  children  were  denied  or  will  likely  be denied
admission  to the high schools  of their choice  because  of their
race.  Some  of these  children  were  denied  admission  to  their
first-choice  schools,  some  to  their  first  and  second-choice
schools,  and  some  to  their  first,  second,  and  third-choice
schools.  Some  members  of  PICS  with  children  in  middle
schools  in  the  Seattle  Public  School  system  fear  that  their
children  will  also  be denied  admission  to the  high  schools  of
their  choice  when  they apply  for those  schools  in the  future.
All  of these  parents  and their  children  have  been  and  will  be
forced  to compete  for seats  at certain  high schools  in a system
that  uses  race  as  a deciding  factor  in many  of its admissions
decisions.

4.
Seattle  Schoo!  District  No.  | (“District”)  is a political
subdivision  of the State  of Washington  that owns  and operates
public  schools  in Seattle,  including  high schools.  The  District,
at  the  direction  of  its  superintendent,  implements  policies,
including  admissions  policies,  established  by  its  board  of
directors.  The  District  is a recipient  of federal  funds.

Joseph  Olchefske  is  the  Superintendent  of  Seattle
5.
Public  Schools  and  as  such  is responsible  for  implementing
policies,  including  admissions  policies,  established  by  the
District’s  board  of directors  (the “Board”).

3la

Appendix  C

Barbara  Schaad-Lamphere  is  the  president  of  the

6.
Board;  Donald  Nielson  is its  Vice  President.

7.
Steve  Brown,  Jan  Kumasaka,  Michael  Preston,
Barbara  Peterson,  and  Nancy  Waldman  are  members  of the
Board.

lil.  JURISDICTION  AND  VENUE

8.
This  action  arises  under  the  Fourteenth  Amendment
to  the  United  States  Constitution,  under  42  U.S.C.  §§  1983
and  2000d  ef seg.,  and  under  RCW  49.60.400.  The  court  has
jurisdiction  of  the  federal  law  claims  under  28  U.S.C.  §§
1331  and  1343  and jurisdiction  of the  state  law  claim  under
28  U.S.C.  § 1367.

9.
Venue  is proper  in this  Court  under  28  U.S.C.  § 1391
and  the  court  has  personal  jurisdiction  over  defendants
because  the  actions  giving  rise  to  the  claims  were  committed
in  the  Western  District  of Washington.

IV.  ADDITIONAL  FACTS  AND  LEGAL  CLAIMS

A.

Defendants’  Race-Based  Admissions  System

10.
The  District  operates  10  public  high  schools  in
Seattle.  It  requires  all  students  who  attend  its  schools  to
identify  their  race  on  a  form  filed  with  the  District.

The  District  and  other  defendants  have  determined
11.
that  the  public  high  school  population  in  Seattle  is

32a

Appendix  C

approximately  40%  white  and  60%  “minority”  or  nonwhite.

12.  | Defendants  have  decided  that  the  racial  balance  of
each  of its  10  high  schools  should  approximate  that  of the
public  high  school  population  and  have  adopted  policies
designed  to  achieve  a  racial  balance  of approximately  40%
white  and  60%  minority  in each  school.

To  this  end,  when  deciding  who  will  be  admitted  to
13.
which  schools,  the  District  considers  whether  admission  of
a  particular  student  to  a  particular  schoo!  will  further  or
impede  achievement  of the  desired  racial  balance.

B.  The  Parents’  Efforts  to  Enroll  Their  Children  in

Schools  of Their  Choice

14.  _ Earlier  this  year,  several  members  of PICS  sought  to
enroll  their  children  in specific  high schools  for the upcoming
2000-2001  school  year.  The  applications  were  submitted  on
time  and  indicated  preferences  among a list of schools.  Their
choices  were  based  on  variety  of factors,  including  proximity
to  home,  educational  programs  offered,  and  extracurricular
activities  offered.  Several  children  of  PICS  members,  and
many  other  children,  were  denied  admission  to their  schools
of choice  on  the  basis  of race.  If the  children  had  been  of a
different  race  (that  is,  if the  white  children  had  been  of  a
minority  race  or  if the minority  children  had been  white)  they
would  not  have  been  denied  admission  to the schools  of their
choice,  but  instead  would  have  been  admitted  to  one  of the
schools  they  had  selected.  However,  as  a  result  of  the
District’s  race-based  admissions  policy,  these  children  were
required  to  attend  schools  that  were  farther  away  from  their

33a

Appendix  C

homes  than the schools  they had selected  or that did not  offer
the  educational  or  extracurricular  activities  available  at  the
schools  they  selected.  The  parents  of children  affected  by
these  race-based  admissions  decisions  are  referred  to  herein
as  “aggrieved  parents.”

15.
When  they  learned  of  the  admissions  decisions
affecting  their  children,  several  of  the  aggrieved  parents
petitioned  the  District,  through  its  appeals  processes,  to
change  the  decisions.  These  appeals  were  denied.

Several  of the aggrieved  parents  also attended  School
16.
Board  meetings  and  explained  their  concerns.  They  have
repeatedly  asked,  both  at  meetings  and  in  writing,  that  the
defendants  change  their  policies.  Defendants  denied  or
ignored  these  requests.

Receiving  no  response  to  their  requests,  several  of
17.
the aggrieved  parents  formed  PICS  and  made a final  attempt
to persuade  defendants  to abandon  their  race-based  policies.
When a final  attempt  failed,  PICS  decided  to  file  this  suit.

C.  The  Legal  Bases  of the  Claims

18.
Theacts  of defendants  described  above  were  committed
under  color  of law.  Defendants  have  discriminated  and  will
likely  continue  to discriminate  against  members  of PICS  and
their  children  on  the  basis  of race.  Defendants’  acts  have
denied  and  will  likely  continue  to  deny  members  of PICS  and
their  children  the  equal  protection  of the  laws.  Defendants’
acts  violate  the  Fourteenth  Amendment  of the  United  States
Constitution  and  are  actionable  under  42  U.S.C.  § 1983.

34a

Appendix  C

19.
Defendant  Seattle  Public  Schools  is  a  recipient  of
public  funds,  and  its discrimination  against  the  members  of
PICS  and  their  children  on  the  basis  of their  race,  color,  or
ethnicity  violates  Title  VI  of the  Civil  Rights  Act  of  1964,
42  U.S.C.  § 2000d,  et seq.

20.
The  acts  of defendants  described  above  violate  RCW
49.60.400,  which  specifically  prohibits  school  districts  in
Washington  from  discriminating  against  or  granting
preferential  treatment  to any  individual  or  group  on  the basis
of race,  color,  ethnicity,  or  national  origin.

21.
 =Plaintiff  is  entitled  to  injunctive  relief  against
defendanis  as  there  is no  plain,  adequate,  or  speedy  remedy
at  law  to  prevent  continuation  of the  acts  complained  of
herein  and  because  the  harm  they  have  suffered  and  will
otherwise  continue  to  suffer  is irreparable.

V.  RELIEF  SOUGHT

Wherefore,  plaintiff  prays  for  the  followmg  relief:

A.
A preliminary  injunction  prohibiting  defendants  from
discriminating  on  the basis  of race  in high school  admissions
pending  the  outcome  of this  suit;

A declaratory  judgment  by the court  that  defendants’
B.
policy of considering  race  in its admissions  decisions  violates
the Fourteenth  Amendment  to the United  States  Constitution,
Title  VI of the Civil  Rights  Act of 1964,  and  RCW  49.60.400;

35a

Appendix  C

wa
A permanent  injunction  prohibiting  defendants  from
discriminating  on  the  basis  of  race  in  their  high  school
admissions  decisions;

Attorneys’  fees and costs  pursuant  to 42 U.S.C.  § 1988

D.
and  RCW  49.60;

,

E.

Any  other  relief  that  is appropriate  and just.

DATED  this  17th  day of July,  2000.

Davis  Wright  Tremaine  LLP
Attorneys  for  Plaintiff  PICS

By  s/  Daniel  B.  Ritter
Daniel  B.  Ritter
WSBA  # 2234

WSBA # 23173

36a

APPENDIX  D —  DECLARATION  OF  MORGAN
LEWIS  IN  SUPPORT  OF  DEFENDANTS’  MOTION
FOR  PARTIAL  SUMMARY  JUDGMENT
DATED  DECEMBER  21, 2000

UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
WESTERN  DISTRICT  OF  WASHINGTON
AT  SEATTLE

NO.  C00-1205R

NOTE  ON  MOTION  CALENDAR:
Friday,  February  2, 2001

PARENTS  INVOLVED  IN  COMMUNITY  SCHOOLS,  a
Washington  nonprofit  corporation,

Plaintiff,

VS.

SEATTLE  SCHOOL  DISTRICT  NO.  1, a political
subdivision  of the  State  of Washington;  JOSEPH
OLCHEFSKE,  in  his  official  capacity  as  superintendent;
BARBARA  SCHAAD-LAMPHERE,  1n  her  official
capacity  as  President  of the  Board  of Directors  of Seattle
Public  Schools;  and  DONALD  NIELSON,  in his  official
capacity  as  Vice  President  of the  Board  of Directors  of
Seattle  Public  Schools;  STEVEN  BROWN,  JAN
KUMASAKA;  MICHAEL  PRESTON,  BARBARA
PETERSON;  and  NANCY  WALDMAN,  in their  official
capacities  as  members  of the  Board  of Directors,

Defendants.

37a

Appendix  D

DECLARATION  OF  MORGAN  LEWIS  IN  SUPPORT
OF  DEFENDANTS’  MOTION  FOR  PARTIAL
SUMMARY  JUDGMENT

1, Morgan  Lewis,  declare:

1.  lam  the  Manager  of Enrollment  Planning, Technical
Support  and  Demographics  for  Seattle  School  District  No.
|.  My  office  is located  at  4141  —  4" Ave.  S.,  Seattle,  WA
98134.  I received  my  master’s  degree  in economics  from  the
University  of  Washington,  which  included  specialized
training  in statistics,  projections,  demographic  analysis,  and
forecasting.  | am  competent  to testify  in this  action  and  make
this  declaration  on  personal  knowledge  and  the  official
records  and  files  of the  Seattle  Schoo!  District.

2.

Seattle  schools  currently  have  an  enrollment  of
59.9%  racial  minority  students  and  40.1%  white  students.
By comparison,  in  1979,  Seattle’s  public  school  population
was  about  62%  white.  The  private  schools  in  Seattle  have
80%  white  students  and  20%  racial  minority  students.
Meanwhile,  the  city’s  overall  racial  demographic  is  about
75%  white  and  25%  people  of color,  and  its  population  of
schoo]  age  children  is about  50%  white.

3.  The  Seattle  School  District  operates  ten  regular  high
schools.  (Some  high  school  education  is  also  given  at
alternative  or  special  schools.)  The  Student  Assignment  Plan
adopted  by  the  Board  of  Directors  of  the  Seattle  School
District  permits  high  school  students  to  attend  any  of these
high  schools,  provided  that  there  are  seats  available.  For  the
2000-01  school  year,  demand  was  particularly  strong  for five

—_——

38a

Appendix  D

of  those  ten  high  schools:  82%  of al!  entering  9" graders
selected  one  of these  five  schools  as  their  first  choice.  This
choice  pattern  resulted  in  “oversubscription”  at  Ballard,
Nathan  Hale,  Roosevelt,  Franklin  and Garfield  High  Schools;
i.e.,  more  students  selected  those  schools  than  there  were
seats  available.  Where  a  high  school  is oversubscribed,  the
Student  Assignment  Plan  allocates  seats  in  those  schools
based  on a set of “tie-breakers”  which  are  sequentially  applied
in the  following  order:  (1) whether  the  student  has a sibling
already  attending  the  school;  (2)  if a  school  is out  of racial
balance,  whether  the student’s  assignment  will  serve  to bring
the school  into balance;  (3) closest  distance  from  the student's
residence  to  the  school;  and  (4) a  lottery.  Under  the  Student
Assignment  Policy  in  effect  for  the  2000-01  school  year,
“racial  imbalance”  was  defined  as  having  a school  population
whose  percentage  of minorities  or  whites  was  10%  or  more
different  from  the  district-wide  racial  demographic  of 59  %
minority/41%  white.  For next  year’s  assignments  (2001-02),
the  Board  has  changed  the  definition  of racial  imbalance  to
15%  or greater  deviation  from  the district-wide  demographic.

4.

Ballard,  Nathan  Hale  and  Roosevelt  High  Schools
are  all  located  in the  north  end  of Seattle,  in regions  of the
city where  most  of the residents  are  white.  In the fall  of 1999,
Ballard  High School  moved  to a new  facility.  Prior to moving
into  the new  building,  Ballard  had not  been  an  oversubscnbed
school  before  the  1999-2000  school  year.  Franklin  is located
in  the  Beacon  Hill  area,  where  the  residential  racial
demographics  are  predominantly  minority.  Garfield  is located
in central  Seattle,  and  draws  students  from  a racially  diverse
set  of  neighborhoods,  including  Capitol  Hill,  the  Central

39a

Appendix  D

District,  Mount  Baker,  the  Rainier  Valley,  Seward  Park,
Madison  Park, Queen  Anne,  Windermere,  downtown  Seattle,
and  Alki.

5.  Of  these  five  oversubscribed  high  schools,  only
Garfield  was  racially  balanced  in  1999-2000  under  the  plan
in  effect  for  the  1999-2000  school  year.  Garfield  has
historically  been  out  of balance,  with  a  high  concentration
of  minority  students.  However,  due  the  popularity  of  the
school  and  the  operation  of  the  integration  tie-breaker,
Garfield  came  into  racial  balance  in  1999.

6.

In three  of the  other  four  oversubscribed  schools—
Nathan  Hale,  Ballard,  and  Roosevelt—there  were  more  than
51%  white  students  enrolled  in  1999-2000.  At Franklin,  there
were  more  than  69%  students  of color enrolled  in  1999-2000.
Accordingly,  students  of color  who  selected  Nathan  Hale,
Ballard  or  Roosevelt  were  assigned,  regardless  of distance
from  those  schools,  while  white  students  who  selected
Franklin  were  also  assigned  regardless  of distance.  Based
on  current  enrollments,  and using the revised  15% band  rather
than  the  10%  band  used  in  the  1999-2000  school  year,
Roosevelt  would  be  considered  integration  neutral  for  next
year’s (2001-02)  assignments,  but the other  three  high schools
would  remain  out  of balance.

7.

For  the  2000-01  school  year,  the  application  of the
integration  tie-breaker  resulted  in  ussignment  of  89  more
white  students  who  chose  Franklin  than  would  have  been
enrolled  there  in  the  absence  of the  integration  tie-breaker.
At  Roosevelt,  the  integration  tie-breaker  resulted  in  the
enrollment  of  82  more  minority  students  than  would  have

40a

Appendix  D

been  enrolled  there  in  the  absence  of the  integration  tie-
breaker.  107  more  minority  students  who  chose  Ballard  were
enrolled  as  a result  of the  integration  tie-breaker.  At Nathan
Hale,  27 more  minority  students  were  enrolled  as  a result  of
the  integration  tie-breaker.

8.  A  part  of  my  responsibility  at  the  Seattle  School
District  is to  stay  informed  of the  housing  and  demographic
patterns  in the  Seattle  area.  Seattle  has  had  and  continues  to
have  racially  concentrated  neighborhoods.  These  racial
concentrations  of the  Seattle  neighborhoods  are  reflected  in
Exhibit  A, a chart  map  that  I created,  which  shows  the  effect
of the  integration  tie-breaker  by  indicating  the  location  of
the ten  high schools,  the racial  make-up of the neighborhoods
of  students  assigned  to  the  oversubscribed  high  schools
because  of the  integration  tie-breaker,  and  the  schools  to
which  they  were  assigned.

9.  As  a  result  of the  operation  of the  integration  tie-
breaker  for  2000-01,  the  entering  9th  grade  class  at  each  of
the  oversubscribed  out  of balance  schools  is  at  least  10
percent  closer  to  the  district's  overall  demographic  profile
than  it  would  have  been  otherwise.  The  following  table
depicts  the effect  of the integration  tiebreaker  on  the entering
freshman  class  at  each  school:

4la

10.

Roughly  half of the approximately  300  high school
students  whose  assignments  were  determined  by  the
integration  tie-breaker  moved  from  racially  concentrated
residential  neighborhoods  to  schools  in  other  areas  of  the
city,  where  the  racial  make-up  of  the  residential
neighborhoods  is different  from  where  they live.  For the most
part,  these  were  minority  students,  residing  in South  Seattle,
the  Central  Area,  and  Rainier  Valley,  who  were  assigned  to
Roosevelt,  Ballard,  and  Hale.  Under  a neighborhood  schools
policy,  or  an  assignment  policy  otherwise  driven  by
proximity,  these  students  would  not  have  had  the opportunity
to  attend  these  in-demand  north  end  schools.

11.

After  the  application  of  the  various  tiebreakers,
80.3%  of  entering  9" graders  were  assigned  to  their  first
choice  school.  The  total  number  of 9" grade  students  assigned
to  a  school  other  than  their  first  choice  is  largely  the  result
of the  popularity  of five  of the  ten  high  schools,  and  not  the
integration  tiebreaker;  i.e.,  if the  integration  tie-breaker  had
not  been  in effect,  the percentage  of 9" graders  receiving  their
first  choice  of schools  would  have  increased  only  slightly,  to
80.4%.

42a

Appendix  D

12.

Overall,  the  assignment  policy  resulted  in  82.4%
of students  receiving  their  first  choice  school.  Most  of the
remaining  students  received  their  second  or  third  choice
school.  For  all  grade  levels,  the  assignment  plan  resulted  in
17.6%  of students  not  receiving  their  first  choice  school.  If
the  integration  tiebreaker  had  not  been  used,  16.9%  of the
students  still  would  not  have  received  their  first  choice
school.

13.

Due  to  the  racial  composition  of  Seattle's
residential  neighborhoods,  the goal established  by the school
board  of attaining  a  level  of diversity  within  the  schools  that
approximates  the  district’s  overall  demographics  cannot  be
accomplished  within  the reasonably  foreseeable  future  unless
some  students  are  assigned  to schools  other  than  their  nearest
or  next  nearest  schools.

14.

The  Seattle  School  District  provides  transportation
for  elementary  students  who  select  schools  within  a  cluster
of schools  in the area  defined  by the student's  residence.  For
secondary  students,  the  District  provides  transportation  for
all students  who  live  more  than  two  miles  from  their  assigned
school.

43a

Appendix  D

SCHOOL  BOARD  ACTION

52a

Appendix  F

SC

HOOL  BOARD  ACTION

DATE:

November  6,  1996

TO:

School  Board

FROM:

John  Stanford

Introduction:  11/6/96
Action:  | 1/20/96

RE:

1997-98  Amendments  to  the  current  Student
Assignment  Plan,  Controlling  Principles  for
new  Student  Assignment  Plan  to  commence
in  1998-99,  and  1997-2000  Comprehensive
Plan

RECOMMENDATION

I recommend  that  the  following  motion  be  passed:

I move  approval  of  Resolution  1996-17  adopting
the  1997-98  amendments  to  Revised  Student  Action
Plan,  adopting  the  Controlling  Principles  for  a  new
Student  Assignment  Plan  to commence  with  the school
year  1998-99  and  directing  the  Superintendent  to
proceed  with  the  development  of  a  new  plan  in
accordance  with  these  Controlling  Principles  for
presentation  to  the  Board  of  Directors  no  later  than
June  1997,  and  endorsing  the  continued  development
of  the  remaining  components  of  the  1997-2000
Comprehensive  Plan  and  directing  the  Superintendent

53a

Appendix  F

to  ensure  those  other  components  are  implemented
concurrently  with  the  implementation  of  the  new
Student  Assignment  Plan.

DISCUSSION

In  a November  16,  1995  report  to  the  State  Board  of
Education  entitled  “Student  Assignment  Process”,  the Seattle
School  District  indicated  that  the current  student  assignment
plan (the  “Controlled  Choice”  plan)  has  not  achieved  all  the
intended  goals.  The  current  plan has  not:

adequately  contributed  to  an  increase  in  academic
performance  of all  students

*  promoted  parental  involvement

*

*

*

*

provided  equal  access  to  high  quality  programs

significantly  reduced  racial  isolation

contributed  to  equity  in movement  of students

provided  comprehensive  programs  in  small  schools

increased  market  share

Amending  the  current  plan  for the  school  year  1997-98,
adopting  guiding  principles  and  directing  the  staff to develop
a  new  student  assignment  plan  based  on  those  principles,
and  endorsing  the  continued  development  and  phasing  in of
the  Comprehensive  Plan  will  increase  student  achievement

54a

Appendix  F

by promoting  quality  schools  close  to home,  ensuring  equity
of access  to  programs,  and  providing  staffing  flexibility  at
schools.

Staff recommends  that  the Comprehensive  Plan  continue

to  be phased  in to:

implement  the  Education  Plan

allow  time  for families  and  communities  to  provide
input  and  adjust  to  changes

target  resources  in a time  of declining  funds

negotiate  supportive  contracts

permit  periodic  reviews  of the  facilities  master  plan

permit  an  orderly  implementation  of the new  Student
Assignment  Plan

The  staff recommends  that  the  new  Student  Assignment
Plan  be phased  in over  a three-year  period.  In the  first  year,
the  existing  plan  would  be  amended  to  incorporate  the
following  elements:

The  current  elementary  controlled  choice  plan  will
be  retained.

Racial  balance  guidelines  will  be  replaced  by
desegregation  goals.

5Sa

Appendix  F

Students  will  be able to choose  any  elementary  school
within  the  District  (Open  Choice)  but  limitations
will  be  placed  on  transportation  based  on  the
transportation  service  standards.

Desegregation  positive  assignments  will  be the  highest
tie breaker.

The  staff  will  designate  set-aside  seats  as  necessary
to  achieve  overall  Comprehensive  Plan  and
desegregation  goals.

Alternative  schools  and  All-City  options  will  exist.

Selected  program  relocations  will  occur.

The  staff recommends  that  the new  Student  Assignment
Plan  commence  with  the  1998-99  school  year  and  be phased
in over  two  years.  The  principle  elements  of the  new  plan
would  include  the  following:

The  new  nine  cluster  choice  pattern  will  be
implemented.

Desegregation  goals  will  continue.

Open  choice  will  continue  but  limitations  will  be
placed  on  transportation  based  on  the  transportation
service  standards.

Desegregation  will  be  the  highest  tie  breaker.

56a

Appendix  F

The  staff may assign students  to set-aside  seats  where
necessary  to achieve  overall
Comprehensive  Plan  and
desegregation  goals.

Alternative  schools  and  All-City  option.  will  exist.

Selected  program  relocations  and  reconfigurations
may  occur.

Begin  two  years  of grandfathering  (transportation).

57a

Appendix  F

SEATTLE  SCHOOL  DISTRICT  NO.  |

RESOLUTION  1996-17

58a

Appendix  F

SEATTLE  SCHOOL  DISTRICT  NO.  |
RESOLUTION  1996-17

WHEREAS,  the Board  of Directors  of the Seattle  School
District  is  committed  to  phasing  in  a  1997-2000
Comprehensive  Plan  to  consist  of:  an  Education  Plan,
Program  Placement  Process,  1997-98  amendments  to  the
Student  Assignment  Plan,  new  Student  Assignment  Plan  to
commence  in  1998-99,  Facilities  Master  Plan,  Human
Resources  Plan,  and  Weighted  Student  Formula;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of  Directors  is  committed  to
providing  equal  and  high  quality  educational  opportunities
to  all  students  in  a  multi-ethnic,  multi-racial,  and  multi-
cultural  learning  environment;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of Directors  has  concluded  that
all of the  components  of the  Comprehensive  Plan  are  critical
to  advancing  the  Board’s  goals;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of Directors  is committed  to  the
concept  of  promoting  student  academic  achievement  by
providing  quality  schools  close  to  home;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of  Directors  is  committed  to
providing  adequate  time  for  students,  their  families  and
Seattle  school  resources  to  adjust  to  changes  in the  Student
Assignment  Plan;

59a

Appendix  F

NOW,  THEREFORE,  BE  IT  RESOLVED  THAT  THE

BOARD  OF  DIRECTORS:

1.  Adopts  the  1997-98  amendments  to  the  Revised
Student  Assignment  Plan  (the  current  “Controlled  Choice”
plan),  which  are  attached  hereto  as  Exhibit  A;

2.  Adopts  the  Controlling  Principles  for  a  new  Student
Assignment  Plan  to  commence  with  the  school  year  1998-
99,  which  are  attached  hereto  as  Exhibit  B, and  directs  the
Superintendent  to proceed  with  the development  of a detailed
new  Student  Assignment  Plan  in  accordance  with  these
Controlling  Principles  for  presentation  to  the  Board  of
Directors  no  later  than  June  1997;  and

3.  Endorses  the continued  development  of the remaining
components  of  the  1997-2000  Comprehensive  Plan  and
directs  the  Superintendent  to  ensure  those  remaining
components  are  implemented  concurrently  with  the
implementation  of the  new  Student  Assignment  Plan,  and
consistent  with  available  resources.

This  resolution  is in  accordance  with  and  based  upon
the  detailed  Findings  and  Conclusions,  which  are  attached
hereto  as  Exhibit  C and are  incorporated  herein  by reference.

1997-98  AMENDMENTS

TO  THE  CURRENT  “CONTROLLED  CHOICE”

STUDENT  ASSIGNMENT  PLAN  FOR

ELEMENTARY  SCHOOLS

(EXHIBIT  A TO THE  RESOLUTION)

62a

Appendix  F

1997-98  Amendments  to  the  Current

“Controlled

Choice”  Student  Assignment  Plan  for
Elementary  Schools

Background

There  were  seven  component  criteria  that  determined
how  the  new  student  assignment  plan  would  be  developed;
choice,  predictability,  equity,  diversity,  minimal  disruption,
multicultural  learning  environment,  and  transportation  costs.
The  challenge  was  to  meld  the  individual  components  into  a
cohesive  plan/implementation  process  so  that  students  could
be  closer  to  home  and  cross  town  forced  bussing  based  on
race  would  be  reduced  or  eliminated.

Transition  Year  (1997-98)

To  minimize  disruption  and  maintain  predictability,  it  is
recommended  that  the new  student  assignment  plan be phased
in.  The  first  phase  of  the  plan  will  be  accomplished  by
amending  the  existing  plan  for  the  year  1997-98.  The  new
plan  will  commence  in  1998-99  and  will  be  implemented
over  a  two  year  period.

Amendments  To  Existing  Plan

During  the  transition  year  (1997-98),  three  major
amendments  to  the  current  controlled  choice  plan  are
proposed.  These  amendments  focus  on  adopting
desegregation  goals,  open  choice,  and  new  tiebreakers.  The
specific  amendments  to  the  existing  controlled  choice  plan

63a

Appendix  F

are  set  forth  next,  along  with  the  pros  and  cons  of  each
proposed  alternative.

Amendment  No.  1

Desegregation  Goals

K-12  students  shall  be  assigned  to  schoo!  in  a  manner
that  supports  desegregation  as  a goal.  This  would  eliminate
the  use  of current  racial  balance  guidelines  which  include
mandatory  assignments  across  town  in  order  to  support
desegregation.  Goals  will  be set  based  on  20%  points  above
or  below  the  overall  minority  enrollment  percentage.

Pros:
¢  There  will  be  fewer  mandatory  assignments  based  on

race.

¢  More  students  can  be enrolled  in schools  located  closer  to
their  homes.

¢  More  families  will  receive  their  first  choice.

¢  There  will  be greater  equity  in student  movement  between
majority  and  minority  populations.

¢  There  will  be greater  equity  in student  movement  between
north  and  south  populations.

64a

Appendix  F

Cons:
* Some  schools  may  become  more  racially  isolated.

*  Some  schools  (particularly  South,  South-East  schools)  may

—— become  oversubscribed.

¢  Schools  located  North  of the  ship canal  may  become  under
enrolled  if those  schools  currently  receive  students  from  the
South  and  South-East  segments  of the  city.

Amendment  No.  2

Open  Choice

Students  will  have  the opportunity  to  choose  any  school
in the  District.  Transportation  will  be limited  to eligible  sites
based  on  the  existing  transportation  standards.

Pros:

¢  Choice  is  increased.

*  Transportation  costs  remain  relatively  stable.

*  Provides  a  transition  to  the  new  plan.

Cons:
¢  People  with  discretionary  time/resources  benefit  most.

*  Popular  schools  will  have  longer  wait  lists.

65a

Appendix  F

*  Neighborhood  children  who  arrive  late  at  oversubscribed
schools  may  be  wait  listed  because  of previous  assignments
made  to  open  choice  students  at  that  schooi.

Amendment  No.  3 a.

Tiebreakers  For  Half-Day  Kindergarten
(Wales  Amendment)

The  tiebreakers  for  half-day  kindergarten  shall  be:

1.  neighborhood
2.  desegregation
2.  siblings
3.  distance
4.  lottery

Pros:
¢  The  tiebreaker  order  supports  voluntary  racial  transfers.

¢  The  tiebreaker  order  improves  predictability  in placement  for
neighborhood  students.

¢  Neighborhood  students  will  not  be  squeezed  out  by
voluntary  racial  transfers.

Cons:
*
the  first  tiebreaker.

Community  input  suggests  that  parents  favor  “siblings”  as

*  The  tiebreakers  are  complex.

66a

Appendix  F

Amendment  No.  3 b.

Tiebreakers  For  First  Through  Fifth  Grade  and  All-
Day  Kindergarten

The  tiebreakers  for  first  grade  through  fifth  grade  and

all-day  kindergarten  shall  be:

desegregation  positive
neighborhood
siblings
distance
lottery

whd
abt

Pros:
¢  The  new  tiebreakers  support  desegregation  by encouraging
voluntary  racial  transfers.

*  Neighborhood  choice  is  improved.

Cons:
¢  Community  input  suggests  that  parents  favor  “siblings”
as  the  first  tiebreaker.

¢  The  tiebreakers  are  complex.

*  Neighborhood  children  from  over  subscribed  schools  may
be  displaced  in  favor  of voluntary  racial  transfers.

67a

Appendix  F

Amendment  No.  3 c.

Tiebreakers  for  Alternative  Schools  and
Special  Programs

The  tiebreakers  for  alternative  schools  and  special

programs  shall  be:

desegregation
siblings
special  program  preference
PP?
lottery

Pros:
¢  The  tiebreaker  order  supports  voluntary  racial  transfers.

*  These  tiebreakers  are  the  least  complex  of the  tiebreakers.

Cons:
*  In oversubscribed  schools  a younger  siblings  may  be denied
entry  in favor  of a  voluntary  racial  transfer.

68a

Appendix  F

CONTROLLING  PRINCIPLES  OF  THE  NEW  1998-99

ASSIGNMENT  PLAN

(EXHIBIT  B TO THE  RESOLUTION)

69a

Assignment  Plan

70a

Appendix  F

Principle  | —  Voluntary  Desegregation

In  1998-99,  the  District  will  implement  a  K-12
“voluntary”  desegregation  plan.  Strategies  and  features  of
the  plan  that  will  promote  voluntary  desegregation  include:

1.  Setting  goals  based  on  20%  points  above  or  below
the overall  minority  enrollment  percentage;

2.  Giving  assignment  preference  to  applicants  who  help
to  improve  racial  balance  (voluntary  racial  transfers);

3.  Providing  viable  desegregation  positive  school  options
with  strategically  located  “designated/magnet”  schools;

4.  Providing  “open  choice”  to  facilitate  more  options
outside  of the  neighborhood  school;

5.  “Setting  aside”  seats  to  accommodate  voluntary  racial
transfers.

Pro:

*  There  will  be fewer  mandatory  assignments  based  on  race.
*  More  students  can  be enrolled  in schools  located  closer  to
their  homes.
*  More  families  will  receive  their  first  choice.
*  There  will  be  greater  equity  in  student  movement  among
majority  and  minority  populations.

Tla

Appendix  F

Con:

*  Certain  schools  may  become  more  racially  isolated.
*  Certain  schools  (particularly  in  the  South,  South-East
schools)  may  become  over-enrolled.
¢  Schools  located  north  of the ship canal  may  become  under-
enrolled  if the schools  are  currently  receiving  a large number
of students  from  south  of the  ship canal.

Principle  Il —  Open  Choice

Families  may  choose  any  neighborhood  or  alternative
school  in the  district.*  However,  transportation  will  only be
provided  to  schools:

1.  within  the  cluster;
2.  to  designated  desegregation  positive  schools
outside  of their  cluster;
3.  to  selected  alternative  schools;
4.  to selected  regional  or all city programs  including:
Distar,  Spectrum,  alternative  schools.

*  The  open  choice  feature  will  start  during  the  1997-98
school  year.

Pro:

*  Choice  is  increased.
¢  Transportation  costs  remain  relatively  stable.
¢  Provides  a  transition  to  the  new  plan.

72a

Appendix  F

Con:

¢  People  with  discretionary  time/resources  benefit  most.
¢  Popular  schools  will  have  longer  wait  lists.
¢  Local  children  from  oversubscribed  schools  may  he
displaced  by late  arrivals.

Principle  —  III.  Choice  Areas

The  district  will  be  divided  into  nine  geographically
contiguous  Clusters  for elementary  school  choice.  Transportation
will  be  provided  to  “cluster  schools”  and  other  schools  or
programs  based  upon  the  transportation  standards.

Pro:

¢  Each  cluster  consists  of contiguous  school  reference  areas
in the  same  part  of town.
*  Students  would  be closer  to  home  if they  so  choose.

Con:

¢  Some  of the  clusters  in the  neighborhoods  are  not  racially
balanced.
*  Some  clusters  have  perceived  poor  choices.

Principle  IV.  —  Alternative  School  Choice

Every  choice  cluster  will  have  access  (with  transportation
provided)  to at  least  one  alternative  school.  For the purposes
of student  assignment,  an  alternative  school  is a  school  that
does  not  have  a  reference  area.

73a

Appendix  F

Pro:

¢  Everyone  has  an  alternative  school  choice.
*  Distance  is not  a factor-  provides  equal  access.

Con:

¢  All  alternative  schools  are  not  the  same.  Parents  perceive
this  as  inequitable.

Principle  V.  —  Oversubscription  Rules

In the event  that more  students  apply than  there are spaces
available,  assignments  will  be  made  in accordance  with  the
following  priorities.

Tiebreakers:

A.  Neighborhood  Schools  —  (full day K-5)

Desegregation
Neighborhood
Siblings  (outside  of the  neighborhood)
Distance
Lottery

FP Pr

B.  Neighborhood  Schools  (half-day  K -  Wales

Amendment)

Neighborhood
.  Desegregation

Siblings  (outside  of the  neighborhood)
Distance
Lottery
PPPS

74a

Appendix  F

C.  Alternative  Schools  and  Special  Programs

1.  Desegregation
2.  Special  Program  Preference
3.  Siblings
4. Lottery

Principle  VI.  —  Mandatory  Assignments

A.  A student  will  receive  a mandatory  assignment  when
space  is not  available  in  any  of the  schools  chosen  by the
family.

|.  Mandatory  assignments  will  be made  to the next
closest  school  to  the  student’s  “choice”  address
within  the  cluster.

2.  Asa  general  rule,  no  student  will  be mandatorily
assigned  outside  of his  or  her  cluster.

B.  The  District  reserves  the discretion  to address  program
needs  under  the
Comprehensive  Plan  in  any  school.  This  may
result  in enrollment  caps  and/or  limited  mandatory  assignments.

Pro:

¢  Predictability  is high  within  the  clusters.
¢  It supports  the  neighborhood  concept.

Con:

*  Racial  isolation  at  some  schools  could  increase.

75a

Appendix  F

Principle  VII.  —  Remedies  for  Racially  Isolated  Schools

No  student  will  be  required  to  attend  a  racially  isolated

school.

Pro:

1.  For a minority  student,  a school  is racially  isolated
if the  total  minority  enrollment  exceeds  79%.

2.  For a majority  student,  a school  is racially  isolated
if the  total  minority  enrollment  is less  than  39%.

¢  This  feature  promotes  desegregation.

Con:

¢  This  feature  may  contribute  to  student  movement  out  of
the area  even  though  the school  is close  to the student’s  home
and  it has  a quality  program.

Principle  VIII.  Transportation  —  “Grandfather  Clause”

If the  school  is not  a cluster  school,  a designated  school
or  select  alternative  school  under  the  new  assignment  plan,
transportation  will  be provided  for up to two  years  beginning
the  school  year  1998-99.

A student  who  is assigned  to  a controlled  choice  cluster
school  under  the  current  plan may  continue  in that  school  to
the  highest  grade.

Pro:

¢  Supports  continuity  and  stability.

76a

Appendix  F

Con:

¢  Increases  transportation  costs.

Principle  IX.  —  Comprehensive  Plan

The  Student  Assignment  Plan  will  be  implemented
concurrently  with  the  other  elements  of the  Comprehensive
Plan.  The  District  reserves  the  discretion  to  implement  the
Student  Assignment  Plan  consistent  with  all  the  other
elements  of the  Comprehensive  Plan,  and  consistent  with
available  resources.  This  discretion  could  extend  to
temporary  enrollment  caps  or  limited  mandatory  assignments.

Pro:
¢  The  Student  Assignment  Plan  is supported  by and supports
other  plans  to  increase  academic  achievement.

Con:
*  A  plan  with  multiple  elements  is  complex  to  coordinate
and  implement.

77a

Appendix  F

FINDINGS  AND  CONCLUSIONS  ADOPTED

BY  THE  SEATTLE  SCHOOL  BOARD

IN  SUPPORT  OF  RESOLUTION  1996-17

(EXHIBIT  C TO  THE  RESOLUTION)

78a

Appendix  F

FINDINGS  AND  CONCLUSIONS  ADOPTED  BY  THE
SEATTLE  SCHOOL  BOARD  IN  SUPPORT
OF  RESOLUTION  1996-17
1997-2000  COMPREHENSIVE  PLAN  AND  NEW
SCHOOL  STUDENT  ASSIGNMENT  PLAN

I.  GOALS  AND  OBJECTIVES

1.  The Seattle  Public  School  District  is committed  to providing
equal  and  high  quality  educational  opportunities  to  all
students,  in  a  multi-racial,  multi-cultural,  and  multi-ethnic
learning  environment.

2.  The Seattle  Public  School  District  is committed  to pursuing
these fundamental  goals through its Comprehensive  Educational
Plan,  which  includes  an  Education  Plan,  Program  Placement
Process,  Facilities  Plan,  Human  Resources  Plan,  Weighted
Student  Formula,  the  1997-98  Amendments  to  the  Revised
Student  Assignment  Plan,  and  a new  Student  Assignment  Plan
to commence  in the  1998-99  school  year.

3.  The  Seattle  Public  School  District  is  committed  to
developing  an  elementary  school  student  assignment  plan
that  will  further  the  following  objectives:

*

*

Maintain  and  enhance  educational  opportunities  and
achievement  for  all  elementary  school  students;

Promote,  maintain,  and  enhance  racial  balance/
diversity;

79a

Appendix  F

*

*

*

*

Increase  parent/student  opportunity  to be assigned  to
their  first  choice  school;

Increase  predictability  of school  assignments:

Increase  equity  in  the  burdens  and  benefits  of  the
school  assignment  plan;

Minimize  disruption  tarough  transitional  phase-in  of
new  plan;

¢  Maximize  cost  effectiveness:

¢

*

¢

Increase  parental  involvement  in  elementary  school
programs  and  schools;

Reduce  long  distance  bussing;  and

Mi£nimize  disruption  to  those  parents/students  who
are  satisfied  with  their  current  school  assignment.

Il.  HISTORY  AND  IMPACTS  OF  VOLUNTARY
DESEGREGATION  IN  SEATTLE  SCHOOLS

1.  Over  the  course  of the  last  25  years,  the  Seattle  School
District  has  voluntarily  undertaken  a  variety  of programs  in
an  effort  to  desegregate  its  schools.

Since  1963  Seattle  has  had  voluntary  racial  transfer

2.
programs.

80a

Appendix  F

3.
In  197i,  the  School  Board  adopted  a  middle  school
mandatory  assignment  desegregation  plan,  although
implementation  was  delayed  for almost  two  years  by a lawsuit
filed  by a  citizen  group  which  opposed  mandatory  bussing.

In  the  mid-1970’s,  the  District  developed  a  “magnet”
4.
school  desegregation  program  which  was  largely  a voluntary
program.

5.
In  1977,  the  School  Board  adopted  a mandatory  student
assignment  plan  (the  “Seattle  Plan”)  for  purposes  of
achieving  the  District’s  racial  balancing  goals.

6.  The  Seattle  Plan  was  immediately  challenged  by  the
passage  of Initiative  350  which  prohibited  mandatory  bussing
throughout  the  state  of Washington.

7.  The Seattle  School  District  challenged  the constitutionality
of Initiative  350  in a  lawsuit  that  was  ultimately  resolved  on
appeal  to  the  U.S.  Supreme  Court.  In  Washington  v.  Seattle
School  District,  458  U.S.  457,  73  L.Ed.2d  896,  913  n.21,
102  S.Ct.  3187  (1982),  the  Initiative  was  found
unconstitutional.

8.
In  1988,  in  response  to  concerns  that  the  burden  of
mandatory  bussing  fell  disproportionately  on  minority
students,  was  contributing  to  “white  flight”  from  the  public
schools,  and  that  bussing  for racial  balance  left  some  schools
under-enrolled  and  others  over-enrolled,  the  School  Board
adopted  a “controlled  choice”  student  assignment  plan.  The
controlled  choice  plan has generally  been  followed  from  1988
to  the  present.

8la

Appendix  F

9.
Since  the  1970’s,  the  number  of students  enrolled  in the
Seattle  Public  Schools  has  declined  from  approximately
60,000  to  46,000  as  of October  |,  1995.  Enrollment  has,
however,  increased  in four  out  of the  last  five  years.

Based  on  October  1995  demographic  data, white  students
10.
comprised  41.3%  of  the  student  population  and  minorities
58.7%.

11.
Over  9,800  students,  or  22.3%  of  total  enrollment,
have  non-English  speaking  backgrounds.  Bilingual  services
are  provided  to  14.2%  of the  enrolled  students.

Ill.  CURRENT  “CONTROLLED  CHOICE”  PLAN

1.  The revised  controlled  choice  plan in effect  in  1995-1996
emphasizes  parental  and  student  choice  of schools  within  a
racially  integrated  and educationally  enhanced  system  of school
attendance  zones  and  assignment  clusters.  The  clusters  were
configured  in  a  manner  that  would  enable  the  District  to
maintain  racial  balance  in  each  cluster  school.  Because  of
housing  patterns,  it was  impossible  to  fashion  clusters  in  a
geographically  contiguous  manner.  For  example,  North
Seattle  neighborhoods  were  matched  with  South  and  Central
neighborhoods.  West  Seattle  schools  were  matched  with
schools  in parts  of Beacon  Hill  and  Rainier  Beach.

2.  The  controlled  choice  plan  has  not  guaranteed  that
everyone  will  receive  their  first  choice  school,  but does  assure
students  stability  of  assignment.  Students  are  allowed  to
choose  from a cluster  of schools,  some  of which  are  close  to
the  student’s  home,  and  some  of which  are  in  racially  and

82a

Appendix  F

culturally  different  neighborhoods.  The  plan  incorporates
desegregation  guidelines,  encourages  voluntary  racial  transfers,
and, where  necessary  to comply  with  desegregation  guidelines,
requires  mandatory  assignments.

3.  Current  state  and  district  desegregation  guidelines
require  that  a  school  not  exceed  the  District’s  minority
percentage  (58.7%)  by more  than  25%  (83.7%)  or  fall below
the  District’s  minority  percentage  by 25%  (33.7%).

4.
In  1989,  the  Wales  Amendment  exempted  neighborhood
half-day  kindergarten  children  from  the  desegregation
guidelines.

Under  the  controlled  choice  plan,  about  7,400  of
5.
the  district’s  46,225  students  are  subject  to  mandatory
assignments,  and  mandatory  bussing.  More  minority  chi'dren
are  transported  north  than  majority  children  are  transpurted
south.

6.  During  the  1994-1995  school  year,  about  1,900
elementary  school  students  rode  the  bus  between  the  north
and  south  ends  of Seattle.  Some  of these  students  did  so  by
choice.  The burden  of this  cross  town,  desegregation  positive
bussing  falls  disproportionately  on  minority  students.  Only
12%  of the elementary  students  who  are  subject to cross  town
busing  are  white.  Bilingual  students  comprise  34%  of  the
group,  minority  students  comprise  52%,  based  on  the
numbers  of elementary  students  who  are  bused  north  to south
or  south  to north.

83a

Appendix  F

7.  From  1991  through  1995,  the  percentage  of  students
receiving  their  first  school  choice  ranged  between  67%  and
71%.

8.  The  percentage  of  students  receiving  mandatory
assignments  remained  at  about  16%.

9.  Approximately  80%  of white  students  choose  a  schoo]
in  their  geographic  area.  These  percentages  are  higher  for
elementary  school  age  children.

10.
Approximately  67%  of  minority  students  choose  a
school  in their geographic  area.  These  percentages  are  higher
for elementary  school  age  children.

In  1994,  a  staff team  was  charged  with  devising  a plan
11.
that  would  simplify  the student  assignment  plan, reduce  costs,
increase  community  satisfaction  and  improve  academic
achievement.

12.  A  new  concept  was  presented  which  continued  the
controlled  choice  concept,  created  contiguous  “choice  patterns”,
increased  choice,  eliminated  mandatory  assignments  based  on
desegregation  guidelines,  and  maintained  incentives  for
voluntary  desegregation.  No  plan  was  implemented.

13.
In  January  1995,  former  Superintendent  William
Kendrick  recommended  to the School  Board  that  the student
assignment  plan be refined  in order  to  “improve  student  and
parent  satisfaction,  enhance  academic  achievement  efforts,
improve  equity,  maintain  schools  free  of  racial  imbalance,
and  maintain  eligibility  for  federal  and  state  desegregation
related  general  and  capital  funding.”

84a

Appendix  F

14.
Ina  November  16,  1995  report  to  the  State  Board  of
Education  entitled  “Student  Assignment  Process”,  the Seattle
School  District  reported  that  the  controlled  choice  plan  had
not  achieved  all  of the  District’s  intended  goals.  The  plan
had  failed  to “adequately”  increase  in academic  performance,
promote  parental  involvement,  provide  equal  access  to  high
quality  programs,  or  significantly  reduce  racial  isolation.
Furthermore,  the  plan  had  disproportionately  burdened
minority  students.

IV.  ALTERNATIVE  OPTIONS  CONSIDERED  AND
PUBLIC  COMMENT

1.
In  1994,  in  response  to  a  growing  public  demand  to
change  the  student  assignment  plan,  the  School  District  had
begun  studying  and  developing  alternatives  to  the  controlled
choice  student  assignment  plan keeping  in mind three  factors:
choice,  diversity,  and  predictability.

2.
It  was  determined  that  any  changes  in  the  student
assignment  plan should  be guided  by five principles  identified
by  Superintendent  John  Stanford  in  November  1995:  1)
enable  children  to attend  school  close  to  home  if they choose
to  do  so;  2) provide  equal  access  to  quality  programs;  3)
increase  the  percentage  of  families  who  receive  their  first
choice;  4)  maximize  diversity  within  each  school;  and  5)
minimize  mandatory  assignments  based  on  race.

3.
Four  basic  options  were  developed:  |)  the  current
“controlled  choice”  plan; 2) “regional  choice”,  a plan that would
permit  students  to  choose  any  school  in the  region  in  which
they  live;  3) “open  choice”,  a  plan  that  would  enable  students

85a

Appendix  F

to choose  any  school  within  the  Seattle  Public  Schools;  and  4)
“neighborhood  choice”,  a  plan  that  would  allow  students  to
choose  their  neighborhood  school,  alternative  schools,  or
schools  designated  for voluntary  racial  transfers.

4.  The  School  District  recognized  that  not  every  one  of the
five  guiding  principles  could  be  addressed  by each  of  the
options  developed,  and  that  the  demographics  and  racial
composition  of  the  district  would  make  it  difficult  to
maximize  diversity  in  the  schools  while  at  the  same  time
eliminating  mandatory  assignments  and  allowing  families  to
choose  neighborhood  or  regional  schools.

5.  Concurrently,  the  District  conducted  focus  groups  to
discuss  whether  the district  should  replace  the desegregation
guidelines  with  desegregation  goals.  The  effect  of replacing
desegregation  guidelines  with  goals  would  be  to  reduce
mandatory  racial  assignments,  allow  increased  first  choice
and  neighborhood  choice,  and  potentially  result  in increased
racial  concentration  in some  schools.

6.  Over  a four  month  period,  from  June  through  September
1996,  a series  of community  forums  and  focus  group  sessions
were  conducted  to  obtain  input  from  parents,  students,
interested  community  and  staff  organizations  and  business
leaders,  and  to  assist  in determining  which  principles  should
receive  priority  consideration.

7.  These  community  forums  and  focus  groups  reflected  the
fact  that  the  community  placed  a  high  priority  on  allowing
more  children  to attend  school  close  to  home,  increasing  the
percentage  of families  who receive  their first choice,  minimizing
mandatory  racial  assignments,  and  promoting  equity.

86a

Appendix  F

8.
During  the summer  of 1996,  the staff studied  the impacts
of the  preferred  options  order  to  assess  the  ability  of each
option  to  maximize  choice,  diversity  and  predictability.  Each
option  was  evaluated  on  the  basis  of:  |) racial  impacts;  2)
educational  impacts;  3) affordability;  4) program  placement
and  availability;  and  5) resource  allocation.

9.  A  hybrid  “choice  cluster”  plan  was  developed  which
incorporated  elements  of all of the options  under consideration.

10.  On  October  2,  1996,  Superintendent  John  Stanford
presented  a  progress  report  to  the  School  Board  on  staff
recommendations  to  replace  desegregation  guidelines  with
desegregation  goals,  eliminate  mandatory  desegregation
positive  assignments,  create  incentives  for  voluntary
desegregation  positive  transfers,  and  phase  in  a  “choice
cluster”  plan  over  a  three  year  period.

V.  NEW  STUDENT  ASSIGNMENT  PLAN

Seattle  School  Board  Resolution  1996-17  (hereafter  the
1.
“Resolution”)  provides  for  the  adoption  of  a  three  year,
phased-in  revision  to  the  current  controlled  choice  plan  for
elementary  school  student  assignments.

2.  Under  the  Resolution,  the  current  controlled  choice
plan  will  be  amended  for  the  transition  year  1997-98.  The
amendments  will  replace  racial  balance  guidelines  with
desegregation  goals,  permit  students  to  choose  any
elementary  school  in the  district  with  transportation  limited
to  selected  sites,  and  make  desegregation  positive
assignments  the  tie  breakers  with  the  highest  priority.  The

87a

Appendix  F

staff will  retain  discretion  to assign  students  to set  aside  seats
to  achieve  overall  Comprehensive  Plan  and  desegregation
goals.

3.  Under  the  Resolution,  a  new  Student  Assignment  Plan
will  be adopted  to commence  in the  year  19°8-99.  This  new
Student  Assignment  Plan  will  include  the  creation  of
contiguous  clusters,  desegregation  goals,  open  choice  with
limits  on  transportation,  desegregation  as  the highest  priority
tie breaker,  and  two  years  of grandfathering.  Staff discretion
to  achieve  overall  Comprehensive  Plan  and  desegregation
goals  will  be  retained.

Specifica  y, the  new  Student  Plan  to  commence  in  the

4.
year  1998-99  will  contain  the  following  features:

*  Nine  geographically  contiguous  “choice  clusters”  will

be created;

*  Each  cluster  will  include  access  to  an  alternative

school;

*  A  child  can  attend  any  elementary  school  within  the
District,  subject  to  limits  on  transportation,
desegregation  goals,  and  school  capacity;

*  No  child  will  be  mandatorily  assigned  outside  of the

choice  cluster;

*  No  child  will  be  forced  to  attend  a  racially  isolated

school;

88a

Appendix  F

The  order  of precedence  for oversubscription  will  be:
1) desegregation;  2) neighborhood;  3) siblings  outside
the neighborhood;  4) distance;  5) lottery.

Desegregation  will  be  the  highest  priority  tiebreaker
for  oversubscribed  schools.

The  plan  adopts  a  desegregation  goal  which  is  the
District-wide  minority  percentage  plus or  minus  20%.
This  goal  will  relate  to  the  determination  of
desegregation  positive  assignments,  permit  voluntary
racial  transfers  and  exchange  programs,  and  provide
for alternative  schools.

Transportation  will  be  provided,  based  on  state
regulations,  within  the  clusters,  to  multi-cluster
options,  to  all-city  options,  and  for  desegregation-
positive  transfers.

¢  The  student  assignment  plan  will  be  coordinated  with
academic,  facilities,  teacher  and  staff,  and  financial
plans  so  as  to  anticipate  and  mitigate  any  adverse
educational  impacts  related  to increased  racial  isolation.

*  Under the weighted-student  formula  to be adopted  in the
financial  resource  plan,  funding  will  follow  students,
resulting  in  additional  funds  flowing  to  schools  which
also  have  large  minority  student  populations.

*  The  plan  will  provide  for two  years  of grandfathering

starting  with  the  1998-1999  school  year.

89a

Appendix  F

5.
While  the  adoption  of the  Resolution  will  likely  result
in increased  racial  isolation  in some  schools,  the  Resolution
also  seeks  to  ensure  that  the  new  Student  Assignment  Plan
is  implemented  concurrently  with  the  1997-2000
Comprehensive  Plan.  This  is intended,  in part,  to  anticipate
and  mitigate  any  adverse  educational  impacts  of  racial
isolation  by assuring  that  additional  funding  and  resources
can  be allocated  based  on  student  need.

6.  The  1997-2000  Comprehensive  Plan  includes:  an
Education  Plan,  Program  Placement  Process,  1997-98
amendments  to  the  Student  Assignment  Plan,  new  Student
Assignment  Plan  to  commence  in  1998-99,  Facilities  Master
Plan,  Human  Resources  Plan,  and Weighted  Student  Formula.

7.  The  Comprehensive  Plan  is intended  to increase  student
achievement  by promoting  quality  schools  close  to  home,
ensuring  equity  of access  to  programs,  providing  staffing
flexibility  at  schools,  and  mitigating  the  adverse  impacts  of
racial  isolation  by  assuring  that  additional  funding  and
resources  are  allocated  based  on  student  need.

90a

APPENDIX  G —  SCHOOL  BOARD  ACTION
(11/4/98)  (INCLUDING  ATTACHMENT  A —
BASIC  FRAMEWORK)*

SCHOOL  BOARD  ACTION

Date:

October  21,  1998

Introduction:  10/21/98

To:

School  Board

Action:

11/4/98

From:

Joseph  Olchefske,  Acting  Superintendent

Re:

1999-2000  Secondary  Student  Assignment  Plans

RECOMMENDATION

| recommend  that  the  following  motion  be  passed:

I move  approval  of  Resolution  1998-10  adopting  the
basic  framework  for  the  1999-2000  Secondary  Student
Assignment  Plans  and  directing  the  Acting  Superintendent
to  implement  the  Plan  in  accordance  with  the  basic
framework.

DISCUSSION

In  a  November  1995  report  to  the  State  Board  of
Education,  the  Seattle  Schoo)  District  indicated  that  its
previous  student  assignment  plan  (the  “Controlled  Choice
Plan”)  had  not  achieved  all  its  intended  goals.  Specifically,
the previous  plan did not:  adequately  contribute  to an  increase
in  academic  performance  of  all  students;  contribute  to
parental  involvement;  provide  equal  access  to  high  quality
programs;  significantly  reduce  racial  isolation;  contribute  to

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Exhibit  2 to  the  Taylor  Declaration  filed  12/29/00.

la

Appendix  G

equity  in  movement  of  students;  provide  comprehensive
programs  in  small  schools;  or  increase  market  share.

In November  1996,  the  School  Board  took  several  steps
to  implement  a  new  Student  Assignment  Plan  designed  to
increase  student  achievement  by promoting  parental  choice
and  involvement,  quality  schools  close  to  home,  and  equity
of access  to  programs.  For  the  transition  year  1997-98,  the
Board  adopted  amendments  to the existing  Controlled  Choice
Plan  including  the  replacement  of racial  balance  guidelines
with  integration  goals  and  open  choice  for all  schools  within
the  District  with  transportation  limits.  In  November  1997,
the Board  adopted  the basic  framework  for  a new  Elementary
Student  Assignment  Plan  for  implementation  in  September
1998.  Key  elements  of the  new  elementary  plan  included  a
universal  assignment  rule  based  on  open  choice  within  the
District,  transportation  within  the  region  and  preference
within  the cluster;  nine  geographically  contiguous  elementary
clusters;  tiebreakers  based  on  siblings,  cluster,  integration
positive  impact,  distance,  and  lottery.  At  that  time,  it  was
anticipated  that  similar  changes  to  the  Secondary  Student
Assignment  Plans  would  be prepared  for adoption  in the  fall
of  1998.

In  accordance  with  the  Boards  direction,  the  Acting
Superintendent  recommends  the  adoption  of new  Secondary
Student  Assignment  Plans  beginning  with  the  1999-2000
school  year.  The  principal  elements  of the proposed  new  plan
which  are  similar  to  those  in  the  Elementary  Plan  include:

¢  addressing  the  competing  goals  of  maximizing
academic  achievement,  maximizing  racial  integration,

92a

Appendix  G

maximizing  student  enro]lment,  and  minimizing
transportation  costs.

¢  dividing  the  District  into  5  middle  school  regions

containing  middle  schools  in each  region.

*  creating  a  district-wide  high  school  region.

*  a universal  assignment  rule  based  on:  open  choice  to
any  school  in  the  District;  transportation  provided
within  region;  and  region  area  assignment  preference.

*  tiebreakers  based  on  sibling,  region,  integration

positive,  distance,  and  lottery.

*  transportation  for middle  school  will  be provided  for
in-region  choices,  and  for  designated  out-of-region
choices,  with  K-5+  schools  assigned  to  multiple
clusters  for  transportation  purposes,  and  with  a two-
year  “grandfathered”  transportation  commitment  for
current  middle  school  students  who  maintain  their
school  assignment  and  their  service  address.  High
school  transportation  will  continue  to  be  provided
district-wide.

As was  the  case  for the new  Elementary  Student  Assignment
Plans,  the  new  Secondary  Student  Assignment  Plan  is being
implemented  as  part  of  a  comprehensive  Plan  including  an
Educational  Plan,  new  SEA  contract,  Weighted  Student
Formula  and  Facilities  Master  Plan  to  ensure  equitable
educational  opportunities  to all students  regardless  of location
or  racial  or  ethnic  diversity  of the  school.

93a

Appendix  G

ATTACHMENTS

Resolution  1998-10

SEATTLE  SCHOOL  DISTRICT  NO.  1

RESOLUTION  1998-10

WHEREAS,  the Board  of Directors  of the Seattle  School
District  is  committed  to  providing  equal  and  high  quality
educational  opportunities  to  all  students  regardless  of  the
location  or  racial  and  ethnic  enrollment  of the  school;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of  Directors  is  committed  to
providing  high  quality  educational  opportunities  to  all
students  in  a  multi-ethnic,  multi-racial,  and  multi-cultural
learning  environment,  because  it  believes  that  such
opportunities  are  beneficial  to  all  students;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of Directors  is committed  to the
concept  of  promoting  student  academic  achievement  by
providing  the  opportunity  for  students  to  aitend  quality
schools  closer  to  home;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of  Directors  is  committed  to
implementing  a new  Student  Assignment  Plan  in conjunction
with  the  other  components  of the  District's  Comprehensive
Plan  including:  the  Education  Plan,  the  Program  Placement
process,  a  Facilities  Master  Plan,  a Human  Resources  Plan
including  the  new  SEA  agreement,  and  a  Weighted  Student
Formula;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of Directors  began  the  transition
to  this  new  plan  in  1996  by adopting  significant  amendments

94a

Appendix  G

to  the  current  Controlled  Choice  Plan,  adopting  a  new
Elementary  Student  Assignment  Plan  and  directing  the  staff
to  develop  and  implement  new  Secondary  Student
Assignment  Plans  for  1999-2000;  and

WHEREAS,  the  Board  of Directors  adopted  a  detailed
set  of  Findings  and  Conclusions  in  conjunction  with  last
year’s action,  and those  are  incorporated  herein  by reference.

NOW  THEREFORE,  BE  IT  RESOLVED  THAT  THE
BOARD  OF  DIRECTORS:

1.  Adopts  the  basic  framework  of  the  1999-2000
Secondary  Student  Assignment  Plans  (the  “Plans”),
which  is set  forth  in Attachment  A; and

2.  Directs  the  Acting  Superintendent  to  implement  the

Plans  in accordance  with  this  basic  framework.

95a

Appendix  G

BASIC  FRAMEWORK  FOR
1999-2000  SECONDARY  STUDENT
ASSIGNMENT  PLANS

.  GUIDING  PRINCIPLES

.  REGIONAL  CONFIGURATION

.  UNIVERSAL  ASSIGNMENT  RULE

ATTACHMENT  A

97a

Appendix  G

GUIDING  PRINCIPLES

A.  Academic  Achievement

The  Plan  needs  to  be  based  on  universal
principles  that  are  grounded  in  the  Districts
Education  Plan.

These  universal
consistently  applied  to  all  schools.

principles

should

be

The  Plan  must  be  easy  to  understand  and
communicate.

The  Plan  should  increase  the  opportunity  for
children  to choose  schools  closer  to their  homes.

B.

Racial  Integration

Every  student  should  have  the  opportunity  to
attend  a  racially  and  ethnically  diverse  school.

The  District  provides  the  opportunity  for
students  to  attend  a  racially  and  ethnically
diverse  school  because  it believes  that providing
diverse  learning  environments  is  educationally
beneficial  for  all  students.

The  Plan  should  utilize  voluntary,  not
mandatory,  mechanisms  to  promote  diversity.

98a

Appendix  G

C.  Student  Enrollment

The  Plan  should  increase  the  use  of  market-
based  mechanisms  in the  assignment  process.

Individual  school  enrollments  should  be driven
primarily  by  the  capacity  of  each  school
building,  although  the  District  should  manage
the  utilization  of its  facilities  in  such  a  way  as
to provide  an  equitable  educational  program  for
all  schools  throughout  the  District.

The  Plan  should  assign  each  student  according
to  his  or  her  highest  possible  preference.  No
student  should  be penalized  for  seeking  a “first
choice”  assignment  to  a  school  other  than  that
students’  neighborhood  school.  Each  student's
priority  for assignment  will  be determined  by a
ranking  for  each  student  at  each  school,  based
upon  the  universal  tiebreakers.

The  Superintendent  is directed  to implement  this
change  to  the  plans  as  soon  as  possible,  but  no
later  than  the  assignment  period  for  September
2000.  If full  implementation  is  not  possible  by
the  assignment  period  for  September  1999,
alternative  strategies  (i.e.  hold  harmless
provisions)  which  increase  the  number  of highest
possible  preference  choices  should  be  used  as
an  interim  measure.

99a

Appendix  G

D.  Transportation

*  The  plan should  provide  sufficient  transportation
so  that  all  students  will  have  a  reasonable  and
meaningful  number  of  schools  fromwhich  to
choose.

¢  The  Plan  should  not  increase  the  District’s  basic

operating  costs.

¢  The  Plan  must  maintain  the  District’s  promise
of  a  two-year  “grandfather  clause”  regarding
current  middle  school  assignments.

E.  Annual  Review

¢

The  Plan  shall  be reviewed  annually  to determine
that  it operates  in a  fair  and  equitable  manner.

2.  REGIONAL  CONFIGURATION

Middle  Schools

¢  The  District  will  be  geographically  divided  into  5

middle  school  regions.

¢

Each  middle  school  region  consists  of contiguously
grouped  elementary  reference  areas.

The  regions  for  the  secondary  portions  of  K-5+
schools  (African  American  Academy.  AE#2,  AS#I,
Blaine,  Madrona,  Orca,  Pathfinder.  Summit  and

100a

Appendix  G

TOPS)  will  be  the  same  as  the  cluster  designations
for  their  elementary  grades.

The  secondary  alternative  schools  (SASS  South  and
Indian  Heritage)  will  be  all  city draws.

*  NOMS/Coho  will  have  the current  3 cluster  draw  for

grades  K-5  and  all city draw  for grades  6-8.

High  Schools

¢

The  District  will  be one  contiguous  high school  region.

UNIVERSAL  ASSIGNMENT  RULE

The  assignment  of secondary  students  for the  1999-2000
school  year  will  be  predicated  on  the  following:

¢

Universal  school  assignment  rule:

-  Open  choice  to  any  school  in  District.

-  Transportation  provided  outside  the walk  boundary.

-  Resident  region  assignment  preference.

10la

Appendix  G.

4.  TIEBREAK.<RS

If more  students  choose  a school  than  there  are  seats
available  in  that  school,  then  tiebreakers  are  used  to
determine  which  students  receive  assignment  to  that
school.

The  following  tiebreakers  and  order  will  determine
the  priority  under  which  students  are  assigned:

Grades  6-8

1.  Siblings
2.  Region
3.  Integration  Positive
4.  Special  Program  Preference  (for  entering  6"
graders,  applied  through  2001-2002  unless
renewed)
5.  Distance
6.  Lottery

Grades  9-12

Siblings
Region
Integration  Positive
Distance
Lottery
Akhwn—

Students  not receiving  an  assignment  to their first choice
school  are  placed  on  a waitlist  for  later  assignment.

The priority  order of the waitlist  for on-time  applicants
is determined  by the  tiebreakers.  The  waitlist  for  late
applicants  is determined  by time  of sign-up.

102a

Appendix  G

5.  VOLUNTARY  RACIAL  INTEGRATION

A.

District  Commitments

The  District’s  commitment  is  that  no  student
should  be  required  to  attend  a  racially
concentrated  school.

The  District  is  also  committed  to  providing
students  with  the  opportunity  to  choose
voluntarily  a  non-region  school  to  promote
integration.

B.  Voluntary  Integration  Mechanisms  for  Middle

School

In-region  choice.  Students  in  all  five  regions
have  the  opportunity  to  choose  any  school  in
their  region  with  transportation  provided
consistent  with  transportation  standards.  Four
regions  (Northwest,  Northeast,  Southwest  and
Central)  offer  in-region  integrated  choice  for
both  white  and  minority  students.

Out-of-region  choice.  The  resident  student
populations  in  the  Northwest,  Northeast  and
Southeast  regions  are  not  as  racially  or
ethnically  diverse  as  the  resident  population  in
the  Southwest  and  Central  regions.  As  a result,
white  students  in the  Northwest  and  Northeast
regions  and  minority  students  in  the  Southeast
region  will  have  the  additional  opportunity  to

103a

Appendix  G

schools’

out-of-region

choose’
with
transportation  provided  consistent  with
transportation  standards.  These  out-of-region
schools  have  been  chosen  based  on  racial
composition,  capacity,  location,  and  other
school  characteristics  to provide  these  students
with  a  meaningful  opportunity  to  choose  a
school  that  is racially  and  ethnically  diverse  or
that  they  will  help  make  more  racially  and
ethnically  diverse.

Integration  positive  tiebreaker.  “\ntegration
positive”  is the  number  three  tiebreaker.  This
tiebreaker  will  apply  whenever  a  minority
student  is  applying  to  a  school  where  the
percentage  of white  students  in  the  receiving
school  is  more  than  10%  above  the  district
white  average,  or  a white  student  is applying  to
a  school  where  the  percentage  of  minority
students  in  the  receiving  school  is  more  than
10%  above  the  district  minority  average.  (The
current  white  percentage  is 41%,  and the current
minority  percentage  is  59%).  This  tiebreaker
applies  district-wide,  although  transportation  1s
only  provided  to  in-region  and  designated  out-
of-region  schools.

7.  TRANSPORTATION

A.

Basic  Principles  of the  Transportation  Plan

Transportation  eligibility  will  be  based  on  the
Transportation  Service  Standards.

The  current  walk  zones  will  continue  to be in effect.

Middle  Schools

In  general,  students  will  receive  transportation
only  for  in-region  choices,  and  for  designated
out-of-region  integration  positive  choices  for
which  they  are  integration  positive  and  are
outside  the  safe-walk  zones.

All  students  in the  southeast  region,  regardless
of race,  will  be  provided  transportation  to  the
designated  integration  positive  out-of-region
choices.

Transportation  will  be provided  out  of region  to
the  following  programs:
APP  at  Washington
-
Certain  Special  Education  programs
-
Spectrum  at Washington  from  the  Southwest
-
region  for  3 years.

Except  as  noted  above,  Metro  passes  and/or
script  will  be  provided  to  eligible  students
attending  out-of-region  schools.

High  Schools
¢

Yellow  bus  or  Metro  passes  and/or  script  will
be  provided  to  students  meeting  transportation
service  standards.

All-City  and  Multi-Zone  Transportation

¢

*

For  K-5+  schools  (African  American  Academy,
AE#2,  AS#I,  Blaine,  Madrona,  Orca,  Pathfinder,
Summit,  TOPS)  transportation  will  be provided
within  their  assigned  regions

For secondary  alternative  schools  (SASS  South,
Indian  Heritage,  Marshall,  Nova)  metro  passes
and/or  script  will  be  provided.

Grandfather  Commitment  for  Middle  School

*

*

Transportation  will  continue  to  be  provided
through  the grandfaher  period  to those  students
who  are  currently  eligible  for transportation  and
who  maintain  their:

-  1998-99  school  assignment
-  1998-99  service  address.

This  “grandfathered”  transportation  will  be
provided  only for the  1999-2000  and  2000-2001
schoo’  years.

107a

Appendix  G

D.  Equi  [illegible]

The  Plan  will  be  monitored  to  ensure  the
provision  of sufficient  transportation  so  that  all
students  will  have  a reasonable  and  meaningful
number  of schools  from  which  to  choose.

108a

Appendix  G

8.  COMPREHENSIVE  PLAN

*  The  1999-2000  Secondary  Student  Assignment  Plans
will  be implemented  in conjunction  with  the District’s
Education  Plan;  the  Program  Placement  process;  the
Human  Resources  Plan,  including  the  new  SEA
contract;  the  Weighted  Student  Formula;  and  the
Facilities  Master  Plan.

*  Implementing  the  Secondary  Student  Assignment
Plans  in  conjunction  with  the  rest  of the  District’s
Comprehensive  Plan  will  ensure  the  provision  of
equitable  educational  opportunities  to  all  students
regardless  of the location  or racial  and ethnic  diversity
of the  school.

I.

Call  to  Order:

At  11:30  a.m.,  President  Schaad-Lamphere  convened
an  Executive  Session  of  the  Board  with  the
Superintendent  to  discuss  his  annual  performance
evaluation.  At  1:00  p.m.,  there  was  a reception  in honor

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Exhibits  3 and  4 to  the  Taylor  Declaration  filed  12/29/00.

110a

Appendix  H

of Directors  Barnhart  and Roe who are  both stepping down
from  the  Board  after  eight  and  24 years  respectively.  The
regular  Legislative  Session  (public  portion  of the meeting)
was  called to order at 2:00 p.m.  The meeting then continued
with  the  general  announcement  to  the  audience,  the
Pledge  of Allegiance,  a moment  of thought  from  the
Superintendent,  and  roll  call.

At  this  time,  the  Board  President  presented  Directors
Barnhart  and  Roe with  mementos  to serve  as  reminders  of
their years  on  the School  Board.  Each  Director  then took a
moment  to  personally  thank  them  for their  friendship  and
their  service  to  the  children  of Seattle  Public  Schools.

Superintendent’s  Awards  and  Presentations:

Urban  Enterprise  Center:  Corporate  Jobs  Challenge
“Recognition  Award.”  Herman  McKinney,  Vice
President/Executive  Director  of Urban  Affairs,  provided
background  about  the  Corporate  Jobs  Challenge  (CJC)
initiative.  The  CJC  was  issued  in  1995  and  challenged
Seattle  businesses  to  hire  from  the  ranks  of qualified
minority  applicants  residing  in  the  target  communities
(Central  Area,  Rainier  Valley  and  Duwamish  Corridor).
The  challenge  called  for companies  to hire  | ,000 residents
from  these  neighborhoods.  As  of  September  30,  1999,
3,417  individuals  from  the  target  areas  have  been  hired
off  welfare.  This  year  alone,  1,511  individuals  were
placed  in jobs with  an  average  wage  of $17,000  per year.
Over  1,800  companies  participate  in  this  effort,  and
recently,  recognition  awards  were  presented  to  six
employers  for  their  outstanding  performance  in  hiring

llla

Appendix  H

qualified  minority  applicants.  Seattle  School  District  was
one  of those  honored  for  hiring  residents  from  the  CJC
target  communities  and  providing  the  highest  average
salary  overall.  President  Schaad-Lamphere  accepted  this
award  on  behalf  of the  District.

Ill.  Superintendent’s  Reports:

Recruitment/Hiring  Efforts.  This  report  was
postponed  until  the  December  Board  meeting.

Parent/Teacher  Conferences  in  Relation  to
Standards.  The  Board  was  presented  with
information  about  recent  District  activities  aimed  at
providing  a better  understanding  to principals,  staff,
parents,  and  the  community  about  the  standards.
These  activities  included  six  community  meetings
that  focused  primarily  on  the role  of parents  and  the
community  in  relation  to  the  standards,  and  four
workshops  designed  to  articulate  «|  principals  their
leadership  role  in  working  with  standards  in  their
buildings.  The  principal  workshops  have  taken  the
form  of  videos  and  scripts,  with  the  first  video
having  to  do  with  parent  conferences.  At  this  time,
the  Board  was  treated  to  a  preview  of  this  video.
Also,  they  were  provided  with  copies  of the  parent
guidebooks  relative  to  standards.

IV.  Citizen  and  Community  Concerns—<Action  Items:

Nothing  to  report  under  this  agenda  item.
