C.  Non-Routine  Action  Items:

The  Board  President  went  “off agenda”  at  this  time
to  recommend  a  motion  that  would  provide  for  a
4%  increase  in  the  Superintendent’s  annual  salary.
She  also  commented  that  Mr.  Olchefske’s  annual
appraisal  as  it relates  to  the  District  priorities  will
be  released  this  day  (November  17");  however,
overall  the  Board  is  very  pleased  with  the
Superintendent’s  performance  over  the past year and
gave  him  “fairly  glowing”  remarks.  The  motion  to
increase  the  Superintendent’s  annual  salary  was
unanimously  approved  by the  Board  (7 yes).

1.  2000  Legislative  Session—Action  Agenda.  The
motion  to  approve  the  legislative  agenda  was
unanimously  approved  by the  Board  (7 yes).

Ll3a

Appendix  H

2.

3.

Highly  Capable  Program  Services.  The  motion
directing  the  Superintendent  to  review  Highly
Capable  program  services  and  provide  potential
modifications  to  the  Board  for  adoption  and
implementation  in  the  2001-2002  School  Year
was  unanimously  approved  by the Board  (7  yes).

Annual  Review  of the Elementary  and Secondary
Student  Assignment  Plans.  The motion  was  read
recommending  that  the  Seattle  School  Board
make  no  changes  to  the  elementary  and
secondary  student  assignment  plans for the 2001 -
2002  school  year,  with  the following  exceptions:

—  Reaffirm  and  restate  the  diversity  rationale
for  using  the  integration  positive  tiebreaker.

—  Change  definition  of an  integration  positive
school  from  +/—10%  to  +/—20%  of  the
Districtwide  minority/majority  percentage.

~  Implement  integration  positive  tiebreaker

only  at  entering  grade  levels.

This  motion  was  not  approved  by  the  Board.
Discussion  follows:

Dir.  Kumasaka:  Commented  that  she  its
struggling  with  how  an  integrated  school
system  is  possible  in  light  of  Initiative  200
and  other  decisions  that  are  occurring  at  the
federal  level.  Stated  that  southeast  schools  are

ll4a

Appendix  H

becoming  more  segregated  with  enrollment
at some  of these  schools  at 75%-85%  students
of color  and 50%-75%  of the students  eligible
for  the  free  and  reduced  lunch  program.
Further  stated  that  diversity  is an  important
educational  goal  and  that  there  need  to  be as
many  choices  and  options  as  possible
available  to  these  students...not  less.
Widening  the band  from  10%  to 20%  lessens
their  choices.  Also  opposed  to  the  proposal
to  implement  the  integration  positive
tiebreaker  at  entering  grade  levels  only.

Dir.  Preston:  Stated  that  he has  always  felt  that
integration  positive  should  be the number  one
tiebreaker  ...  that  he  has  difficulty  in
supporting  a change  to the integration  positive
tiebreaker  because  it  is  already  narrowly
tailored.  Further  stated  that  he  agrees  with
Director  Kumasaka’s  assessment  of what  is
occurring  at  some  of the  schools  because  of
actions  already  taken  by the  Board  ...
that
the  Board  may  be creating  a  situation  where
it  claims  reaffirmation  of  our  diversity
rationale  while  creating  policy  that  “flies  in
the  face”  of it.

Dir.  Barnhart:  Stated  that  he was  going  to  favor
widening  the  band  because  he  felt  that  this
action  would  not  limit  choice  at  the extremes
of  our  racial  concentrations  and  would
provide  stability.  Felt  that  it  is  incumbent
upon  the  next  Board  to  look  at  the  weighted

115Sa

Appendix  H

student  formula  and  whether  or  not  the
student  assignment  system  should  be  more
flexible...also  to  look  at  the  issue  of
socioeconomic  status.

Dir.  Preston:  Responded  that  he  is opposed  to
this  proposal  for  some  of the  same  reasons
that  Director  Barnhart  supports  it.  That
Director  Barnhart  is correct  that  widening  the
band  will  make  the  assignment  plan easier  to
implement  for  the  staff,  but  not  necessarily
better  for  the  students.  Reiterated  that  if we
are  a  student-focused  District,  then  the  plan
should  have  escape  mechanisms  for  students
...  particularly  in the overcrowded  southend
where  it  is  racially  isolated  with  much
poverty.  Stated  that  even  with  the  weighted
student  formula,  this  proposal  will  create
difficulties  in the  southend  because  widening
the  band  will  make  almost  every  school  race
neutral  and when  you  put the other tiebreakers
in  place,  children  in  the  neighborhood  will
get  into  the  schools  and  those  outside  of the
neighborhood  won't.  Since  portables  will  not
be  used  at  the  northend  schools  (where  there
is Capacity)  to accommodate  students  from  the
southend,  this doesn’t  appear  to be a student-
focused  change  and will  result  in further  racial
isolation.

116a

Appendix  H

Dir.  Barnhart:  Responded  that  the  Board  is
walking  a “fine  line”  between  trying to create
a  system  that  works  for  everyone  and  the
potential  legal  impacts  of  1-200  and  Brown
vs.  Board  of Education.  That  the  Board  and
staff have  created  a system  that  functions  well
without  being  hamstrung  by lawsuits,  while
trying  to  meet  goals  such  as  integration  and
diversity.

Dir.  Preston:  Responded  that  in years  past,  the
School  Board  challenged  a  State-passed
initiative  at the Supreme  Court  level  regarding
the  right  to  determine  what  the  student
assignment  plan  would  look  like...and  the
Court  ruled  in favor of the District.  Therefore,
there  are  two  separate  laws;  one  that  says
separate  educational  facilities  are  inherently
unequal  and  the  new  State  law  1-200.  With
regard  to  1-200,  felt  that  the  District’s
assignment  plan  is already  narrowly  tailored
because  integration  positive  is not  the number
one  tiebreaker  and  the plan allows  for a range
of racial  participation  in each  school  so  if the
plan  were  challenged  in  court,  the  narrow
tailoring  issue  would  not  hold  up.  Stated  that
if the  District  is going  to  be  sued,  it should
be sued  for doing  the  right  thing.  Reiterated,
however.  that  he does  not  believe  that  we  will
be  sued  because  the  assignment  plan  has
already  been  narrowly  tailored.

117a

Appendix  H

Dir.  Roe:  Stated  that  she  would  not  support
changing  the  integration  positive  tiebreaker
from  +/—10%  because  what  we  have  in place
at  this  time  seems  to  be working  well.

Dir.  Nielsen:  Reiterated  that  the  integration  of
society  should  not  fall  on  the  backs  of  the
public  schools,  but  that  public  schools  have
a  significant  role  to  play  in  this  issue.  That
increasing  the  quality  of schools  in  racially
concentrated  neighborhoods  affects  real  estate
values  ...  the  most  important  influence  on
integration.  The  degree  to  which  schools
improve  in racially  concentrated  areas  is the
degree  to  which  neighborhoods  become
increasingly  attractive  places  to  live,  which
impacts  the integration  of the schools.  Further
stated  that  the  bottom  line  to  integrating
society  most  effectively  is  to  effectively
educate  each  child  because  nothing  will
promote  integration  faster  than  providing
children  with  the  opportunity  to  earn  a  very
good living which  then  allows  them  the ability
to purchase  real  estate  anywhere  they wish  to
live.  Stated  that  where  we  are  in our  present
system  is  fine;  however,  believes  that
integration  is  the  wrong  tiebreaker  ..  .  that
society  is  seeing  an  increasing  number  of
racially  mixed  marriages  and  children  and
knowing  what  race  a  person  is  has  become
an  increasingly  complex  judgment.  Felt  that
a  better  tiebreaker  would  be  poverty.  Urged

118a

Appendix  H

the  Superintendent  and  staff  to  replace
the  integration  positive  tiebreaker  with  a
socioeconomic  tiebreaker  and  have  that
presentation  available  to  the  Board  before
the  fall  of  next  year  when  this  issue  is
addressed  again.

Pres.  Schaad-Lamphere:  Hearing  no  further
comments,  stated  that  she  would  entertain  a
motion  to  delete  item  #2  from  the  original
motion...the  item  which  would  change  the
definition  of an  integration  positive  school  by
widening  the  band.

remove  item  #2  from  the  original  motion.

questions  related  to  the  proposed  amendment.

still  leave  item  #3  in the  motion  which  refers
to  implementing  the  integration  positive
tiebreaker  at entering  grade  levels  only.  Stated
that  he  found  that  no  less  objectionable  than

support  this  particular  change.

An  amendment  was  proposed  by  Director

remove  item  #3  from  the  original  motion.  The
Board  President  called  for  any  additional

Pres.  Schaad-Lamphere:  Called  for  a  vote  on
removing  item  #3.  Clarified  that  a vote  of yes

Appendix  H

This  proposed  amendment  was  approved  by the
Board  (4 yes-Barnhart,  Kumasaka,  Preston,  Roe/

which  means  that  item  #3  will  be  deleted  from

An  amendment  was  proposed  by  Director
Nielsen  and seconded  by Director  Roe  to change
the  secondary  student  assignment  plan  so  that
“closest  school”  is adopted  as  a tiebreaker  to  be
utilized  after  integration  positive  and  before
distance.  The  tiebreaker  order  would  then  read:
1)  sibling;  2)  region;  3)  integration  positive;
4) closest  school;  5) distance;  and,  6) lottery.

Dir.  Nielsen:  Stated  that  he  believed  that  this
amendment  would  help to  rectify  a  flaw  that
currently  exists  in  the  secondary  student
assignment  plan  which  created  the  problem  -
that  occurred  at  Eckstein  this  past  year  ...
that  some  households  have a high  priority  of
acceptance  to two  closest  schools  while  others
will  have  no  chance  at  all  of being  accepted
to  their  one  closest  school.

Dir.  Preston:  Complimented  Director  Nielsen
for a very  well-thought-out  presentation.  Felt
that  this  could  not  be decided  at  this  meeting
because  public  comment  was  necessary
.  that  the
before  the  Board  took  any  action.
potential  for unintended  consequences  not  yet
known  was  too  great.

.

I2la

Appendix  H

as  well.  Stated,  however,  that  he  could  not
support  this  amendment  at  this  time  because
the impacts  were  not  known  and  further  study
needed  to  occur  before  the  Board  took  this
action,  and  that  this  could  be  revisited  in the
future.

Dir.  Kumasaka:  Stated  that she felt uncomfortable
supporting  this  because  there  had  been  no
community  input  about  this  change  ..
.  that
public  sentiment  at  the  community  meetings
was  overwhelmingly  in  favor  of leaving  the

for  their  input.

school  year?

Dir.  Preston:  Asked  the  Superintendent  to

respond  to  that  question.

necessary  to improve  the program  at Hamilton

Pres.  Schaad-Lamphere:  Stated  that  there  are

Hearing  no  further  discussion,  the  Board
President  called  for  a  vote  on  the  proposed
amendment  to the secondary  student  assignment
plan  that  would  make  “closest  school”  a
tiebreaker  to be utilized  after  integration  positive
and  before  distance.

123a

Appendix  H

This  proposed  amendment  was  not  approved
by  the  Board  (3  yes-Nielsen,  Roe,  Waldman/
4  no-Barnhart,  Kumasaka,  Preston,  Schaad-
Lamphere).

An  amendment  was  proposed  by  Director
Nielsen  and  seconded  by  Director  Barnhart  to
change  the  secondary  student  assignment  plan
so  that the fourth  tiebreaker  for all schools  would
be “prior SPS  attendance.”  Prior  SPS  attendance
was  defined  as  attendance  in  Seattle  Public
Schools  for the  preceding  year.

Dir.  Nielsen:  Stated  that currently  in every  grade
except  grades  6  and  9,  this  amendment  is
already  in place.  However,  when  our  students
move  from  elementary  to  middle  and  middle
to  high  school,  the  system  is opened  up  and
no  preference  given  to  current  SPS  children.
Believed  this  practice  to  be  wrong  and  that
the  District  should  take  care  of  existing
customers  first.

Dir.  Preston  proposed  a  “friendly  amendment”
that  “closest  school”  be  deleted  from  the
language  of this  motion  since  that  discussion  had
already  taken  place  and  the  Board  did  not  vote
in  favor  of  adding  “closest  school”  as  a
tiebreaker.  Director  Nielsen  accepted  this
“friendly  amendment”  and  closest  school  was
stricken  from  the  language.

124a

Appendix  H

Dir.  Preston:  Noted  that  because  we  have  a
market  share  system  run  by dollars,  it makes
more  sense  to  leave  the  system  wide  open  so
that all taxpayers  have  the same  opportunities
to  use  that  system  whether  they  choose  to  or
not.  Also  felt  that  if this  change  is approved
by  the  majority  of  the  Board,  it  should  be
phased  in  over  a  period  of time  ..  .  not  just
dropped  on  them  “all  at  once  and  out  of
the  blue.”

Dir.  Kumasaka:  Stated  that  there  are  a  variety
of reasons  that  parents  enroll  their  children
in private  schools  whether  it is long-term  or
only  for  a  year  or  so  and  they  should  not
be  penalized  for  making  that  choice.  Also
stated  that  this  amendment  seemed  very
undemocratic  and  that  all  taxpayers  should
have  equal  access  to  public  education  since
their  tax  dollars  contribute  to  it.

Dir.  Waldman:  Commented  that  all  of  the
taxpayers  of  Seattle  are  our  customers
whether  they choose  to use  the services  or  not.
Therefore,  the  challenge  for  the  Board  and
Superintendent  is  to  make  our  services  so
attractive  that  taxpayers  choose  to  use  them.

Pres.  Schaad-Lamphere:  Stated  that  she  is  a
fervent  supporter  of public  schools.  However,
a  number  of  her  friends  and  family  choose
private  schools  for  their  children  for  a  time

125a

Appendix  H

and  the  reasons  vary  greatly  from  family  to
family.  Believed  that  the  District  should
welcome  private  school  students  into  our
system  whenever  they  choose  to  come.

Hearing  no  further  discussion,  the  Board
President  called  for  a  vote  on  the  proposed
amendment  to the secondary  student  assignment
plan  which  was  further  amended  by  striking
“closest  school”  from  the  language  but  would
make  “prior  SPS  attendance”  the  fourth
tiebreaker.

This  proposed  amendment  was  not  approved  by
the  Board  (2  yes-Nielsen,  Roe/5  no-Barnhart,
Kumasaka,
Schaad-Lamphere,
Waldman).

Preston,

An  amendment  was  then  proposed  by Director
Waldman  and  seconded  by  Director  Nielsen  to
change  the  secondary  student  assignment  plan
by  moving  the  easternmost  boundary  of  the
northwest  region  to  include  the  Greenwood
reference  area,  effective  the beginning  of School
Year  2001.

Dir.  Waldman:  Stated  that  this  area  is  within
walking  distance  of Whitman,  is on  the  other
side  of Aurora,  and  seems  like  a  logical  place
to draw  the  line.  Also,  it will  help to  alleviate
some  of the  enrollment  pressure  on  Eckstein.

126a

Appendix  H

J. Vacchiery:  Stated  that  some  of the  impacts  of
moving the Greenwood  reference  area  into the
northwest  region  would  be:

l.  Increase  the  number  of  students  in  the
northwest  region  and  reduce  the  number
of students  in the northeast  region  (talking
about  approximately  145  students,  grades
6-8).

Potential  of  moving  the  problem  in  the
northeast  to  the  northwest  since  students
currently  living  in  the  far  north  and  far
south  portions  of  the  northwest  region
could  be  displaced  by  the  Greenwood
students  through  the  distance  tiebreaker.

Potential  for “grandfathering”  yellow  bus
transportation  for  students  attending
Eckstein  and  Hamilton  because  if these
students  are  moved  out  of their  region,
they  lose  their  yellow  bus  transportation.

Dir.  Barnhart:  Stated  that  this  amendment  may
make  sense,  but  it needs  more  study  before
taking  any  action  because  of  the  potential
impacts  to  other  neighborhoods.

Dir.  Nielsen:  Questioned  Director  Waldman  as
to  whether  or  not  the  principal  or  PTSA
President  at  Greenwood  had  been  contacted
about  this  proposal?

127a

Appendix  H

Dir.  Waldman:  Responded  that  she  had  tried  to
contact  both  of these  individuals  prior to  this
meeting  but  was  unsuccessful.

Directors  Nielsen,  Kumasaka  and  Preston  also
felt  that  this  action  could  not  be taken  without  a
public  process  and  without  further  study  of the

Hearing  no  further  discussion,  the  Board
President  called  for  a  vote  on  the  proposed
amendment  to the secondary  student  assignment
plan that would  change  the easternmost  boundary
of the northwest  region  to include  the Greenwood
reference  area,  effective  the beginning  of School
Year  2001.

This  proposed  amendment  was  not  approved  by
the  Board  (2 yes-Roe,  Waldman/5  no-Barnhart,
Kumasaka,  Nielsen,  Preston,  Schaad-Lamphere).

At  this  time,  President  Schaad-Lamphere
revisited  the original  motion,  as  amended.  It was
necessary  to make  minor  changes  to the language
because  of  the  non-approval  of  much  of  the
original  language  (i.e.,  changing  plural  forms
within  sentence  structure  to  singular)  and  these
changes  were  Board-approved  by  “friendly
amendment.”  Also,  Director  Preston  asked  that
the  attachment  to  the  original  motion  entitled
Board  Statement  Reaffirming  Diversity
Rationale  be  included,  in  its  entirety,  as  a  part

128a

Appendix  H

of these  minutes.  The  original  amended  motion,
and  the  accompanying  attachment  now  read:

Recommendation

I recommend  adoption  of the  following  motion:

| move  that,  based  on  its  annual  review  of the
student  assignment  plans  including  staff reports
and  community  input,  the  Seattle  Schoo!  Board
make  no  changes  to the elementary  or  secondary
student  assignment  plans  for  the  2000-2001
school  year,  and  that  it:

Reaffirm  and  restate  the  diversity  rationale  for
using  the  integration  positive  tiebreaker  (as  set
forth  in the  attached  statement).

Attachment

Board  Statement  Reaffirming  Diversity
Rationale

The  Seattle  School  District  is fortunate  to  have
the  pluralism  brought  by the  African  American,
American  Indian,  Asian  American,  Hispanic,  and
White  communities  and  by  the  multi-ethnic
groups  within  each.  The  school  system's
diversity  reflects  the  increasing  pluralism  of the
U.S.  society  and  emphasizes  the  broader  need
for  international  awareness  and  cooperation.
Diversity  in  the  classroom  increases  the

129a

Appendix  H

likelihood  that  students  will  discuss  racial  or
ethnic  issues  and be more  likely to socialize  with
people  of different  races.  Diversity  is  thus  a
valuable  resource  for  teaching  students  to
become  citizens  in  a  multi-racial/multi-ethnic
world.

Providing  students  the  opportunity  to  attend
schools  with  diverse  student  enrollment  also  has
inherent  educational  value  from  the  standpoint
of education’s  role  in a democratic  society.  The
survival  and  strength  of our  democratic  society
depends  upon  an  educated  citizenry  which  shares
concerns  about  the  welfare  of  society,  its
members,  and  the  democratic  principles  that
govern  it.  Diversity  brings  different  viewpoints
and  experiences  to  classroom  discussions  and
thereby  enhances  the educational  process.  It also
fosters  racial  and  cultural  understanding,  which
is  particularly  important  in  a  racially  and
culturally  diverse  society  such  as  ours.

The  District's  commitment  to the diversity  of its
schools  and  to  the  ability  to  voluntarily  avoid
racially  concentrating  enrollment  patterns  also
helps ensure  that all students  have access  to those
schools,  faculties,  course  offerings,  and
resources  that  will  enable  them  to reach  their  full
potential.

130a

Appendix  H

Based  on  the  foregoing  rationale,  the  Seattle
School  District’s  commitment  is that  no  student
should  be  required  to  attend  a  racially
concentrated  school.  The  District  is  also
committed  to  providing  students  with  the
opportunity  to  voluntarily  choose  to  attend  a
school  to  promote  integration.  The  District
provides  these  opportunities  for  students  to
attend  a  racially  and  ethnically  diverse  school,
and  to  assist  in  the  voluntary  integration  of  a
school,  because  it believes  that  providing  diverse
learning  environments  1s educationally  beneficial
to  all  students.

The  Board  approved  this amended  motion  (6 yes/
1 no-Roe).

Pres.  Schaad-Lamphere:  Thanked  the  staff  for
their  hard  work.  Also,  praised  her colleagues
for the  many  hours  of work  and  thought  that
they put into this particular  issue.  Commented
that,  although  they  do  not  always  agree  on
the  best  way  to  accomplish  it,  every  Board
member  is committed  to the highest  standards
of excellence  and  equity  for  all  of Seattle’s
children.

At  this  time,  Director  Nielsen  proposed  two
additional  motions.  The  first  motion  directs  the
Superintendent  to review  the composition  of the
northeast  and  northwest  middle  school  regions,
within  the  next  six  months,  and  present

i3la

Appendix  H

recommendations  regarding  the placement  of the
Bagley  and  Greenwood  Elementary  reference
areas  in the northwest  region,  and the Northgate
reference  area  in  the  northeast  region.  This
motion  was  seconded  by Director  Barnhart.

The  second  motion  directs  the  Superintendent
to  research  the  possibility  and  implications  of
opening  a  new  middle  school  in North  Seattle,
and  provide  findings  and  recommendations  to
the  Board  by Fall  of 2000.

While  discussing  the  first  proposed  motion,  it
became  apparent  that  both  of these  motion  are
tied  to  one  another.  However,  there  are  other
areas  of the city that  also  must  be studied  before
any  changes  occur  because  the  impacts  may  be
far-reaching.  Also,  some  of the  problems  cited
in the  northeast  and  northwest  regions  may  not
be  specific  to  those  areas  at  some  time  in  the
future  (i.e.,  Aki  Kurose  M.S  in  the  southeast
region).  Director  Nielsen  stated  that  he  had  no
problem  with  withdrawing  both  of these  motions
as  long  as  the  Board  agreed  that  the
Superintendent  and  staff  should  begin  looking
at  these  concerns.  The  Board  felt  that  its wishes
and direction  to the Superintendent  and  staff had
been  clearly  articulated;  and  therefore,  formal
motions  probably  were  not  necessary.  With  that
understanding  among  the  full  Board  and  the
Superintendent,  Director  Nielsen  withdrew  both
of these  motions.

Appendix  H

VII.  New  Business:

5  Value  Engineering  Approvals  for  Bryant,  Coe,
Emerson  and  West  Seattle.  Introduced  for action  at

Resolution  1999-17,  Construction  Projects  will  not
Create  or  Aggravate  Racial  Imbalance.  Introduced
for action  at  the  December  8" meeting.

10"  Grade  WASL  Required  for  High  School
Graduation.  Introduced  for  action  at  the  December
8" meeting.

Pres.  Schaad-Lamphere:  Commented  that
when  graduation  requirements  are  changed,
notification  usually  occurs  four years  prior to
implementation.

J.  Rimmer:  Responded  that  a  high  school
reform  agenda  is currently  being  developed
that  includes  an  increase  in  graduation
requirements.  That  there  is a  plan  to  bring  a
full  proposal  to  the  Board  in  the  spring  but
because  of the timeliness,  it was  necessary  to
bring  this  piece  forward  at  this  time.  Noted
that  in terms  of the  State,  the  Board  has  the
authority  to  make  decisions  that  go  beyond
State  requirements.  In  addition,  there
are  extenuating  circumstances  for  which
procedures  would  need  to  be  developed  in
terms  of how  this  will  be  implemented  and

133a

Appendix  H

under  what  circumstances  students  would  be
exempt  from  taking  the  test  (Superintendent
would  grant  on  a case-by-case  basis).

Pres.  Schaad-Lamphere:  Stated  that  high school
diplomas  are  a  property  right  and  we  may
open  ourselves  up  to  legal  issues  if care  is
not  taken  when  changing  graduation
requirements.

.

J.  Rimmer:  Responded  that  this  motion  only
mandates  that  students  take  the  WASL,  not
pass  it in order  to graduate.

B.  Benzel:  Stated  that  since  the  District  is not
requiring  that  the  WASL  be  passed  in order
to  graduate  .
.  only  taken.  That  the  issue  of
opportunity  is different,  and  the  opportunity
will  be there  for all  students  within  the  target
group  to  meet  the  requirement.

.

J. Rimmer:  Added  that  make-up  tests  and  other
implementation  issues  will  be  included  as  a
part  of the  procedure.

There  were  additional  questions  and  concerns
raised  by  Board  members,  and  they  were
unanimous  in  their  desire  to  see  some  of these
questions  adequately  answered  prior to the vote.
It was  agreed  that  this  will  be an  agenda  item  for
further  discussion  at  the  Policy  and  Legislative
Committee  meeting,  on  November  23rd.

SCHOOL  BOARD  ACTION

Date:

October  4, 2000

To:

School  Board

From:  Joseph  Olchefske

Superintendent

Re:

Annual  Review  of  the  Elementary  and  Secondary
Student  Assignment  Plans

RECOMMENDATION

I recommend  the  following  motion  be adopted:

| move  that,  based  on  its  annual  review  of the  student
assignment  plans,  the  Seattle  School  Board  affirm
high school  region  for tiebreaker  purposes  and make  no
changes  to  the  elementary  or  secondary  student  assignment
plans  for the  2001-2002  school  year,  except  as  follows:

the single  |

1.  Change  the  definition  of  an  integration
positive  school  from  +/—10%  to  +/-2015%  of
the District-wide  minority/majority  percentage;

2.  Implement  the integration  positive  tiebreaker

only  at  entering  grade  levels;  and

138a

Appendix  H

3.  Apply  the  integration  tiebreaker  until  a
school’s  assignments  reach  a  neutral
integration  status.;end

4.  Blew

he  Bamshart/Wald

.

at the heh  school  devel.
drendment

inuing  improvements  in

the

superintendent
i

rd  her

Further,  t
to  fi
schools,  particularly  those  five
board
the
a

school

to

un
ibed
on a regular

sch

high
basis.

DISCUSSION

Concerns  for the secondary  student  assignment  plan include
the  imbalance  in  school  choice  and  school  effectiveness,  and
the uneven  geographic  distribution  of secondary  schools.  There
are  no particular issues of concern  with regard to the elementary
program.  Options to address  secondary  plan concerns  include  a
no-change  option  and  an  o»tion  that  would  create  geographic
regions  with  a high  preference  for between  one  to  three  high
schools.  Community  input  over  the  past  two  years  heavily
favored  the  no-change  option  and  a strong  focus  on  improving
the quality  of all schools.

With  respect  to  the  integration  positive  tiebreaker,
seventy-five  percent  of  all  schools  are  now  outside  the
District’s  current  defined  band  of an  integrated  school  (+/—
10%  from  the  District-wide  average).  After  review  and  2
years  of  experience,  there  was  not  strong  evidence  that
utilizing  a  +/-10%  band  provided  a  materially  better
educational  experience  than  would  a  band  of  +/—20%.
Accordingly,  in  order  to  fulfill  our  narrowly  tailoring

139a

Appendix  H

obligation,  staff  is recommending  a  +/—20%  band.  A wider
band  would  allow  for a more  targeted  focus  on  schools  that
have  significant  racial  concentrations.  Currently,  the
tiebreaker  applies  at  all  grade  levels,  but  it  impacts  few
students  at  the  non-entry  level  grades.  Applying  the
integration  tiebreaker  to entry grades only will give it  a more
targeted  impact.  Finally,  applying  the  integration  tiebreaker
until  a school  reaches  a neutral  integration  status  will  further
ensure  the  purpose  of the  tiebreaker  is met.

Staff  Person:

Mark  Green/John  Vacchiery

Appendix  H

The  Seattle  Public  Schools

SCHOOL  BOARD  ACTION

DATE:

November  11,  2000

TO:

Board  Members

FR:

Don  Nielsen,  Michael  Preston

RE:

High  School  Program  Assignment  Priority

RECOMMENDATION

I recommend  the  following  motion  be adopted:

ae

Tl

——T

2002-03
special  assignment  process  earns for the
year  -for  the  2002-03  assignment  year  for
assignment
high  school  programs  which  are  limited  to one  site  and
for which  students  need  to qualify  and  apply.  Students
that qualify  and are  accepted  into the program  wittcould
be assigned  to the school  where  the program  is located.

Immediate  adoption  is  in  the  best  interests  of  the
District.

l4la

Appendix  H

DISCUSSION

With  programs  that  are  limited  in location  and  require
qualifications  and  applications,  students  who  meet  the
qualifications  and  are  accepted  should  be  assigned  to
the school  where  the program  is located.

Students  that  take  classes  and/or  programs  in  middle
school  that  is in preparation  for  a  special  program  at
high school  should  have  priority  to  those  programs.

The  students  would  be assigned  into  set-aside  seats  at
the  start  of the  assignment  process.  Acceptance  into
such programs  will be limited  to the total available  seats
in the program.

APPENDIX  J —  LETTERS  RECOMMENDING  AND
APPROVING  ANDY  MEEKS  FOR
BALLARD  BIOTECH  PROGRAM*

Celebrating  125  Years
of Putting  Students  First

May  2, 2000

McClure  Middle  School
1915  First  Avenue  West
Seattle,  WA  98119
(206)  378-2700
Fax  (206)  378-2701

Seattle  Schools

The
Seattle  Public  Schools

Student  Assignment  Appeals  Board
Administration  and  Service  Center

~  815  Fourth  Avenue  N.
Seattle,  WA  98109

RE:  ANDY  K.  MEEKS  (Student  1.D.  5300090).  Grade  8

To  Whom  It May Concern,

Andrew  has  been  a  student  at  McClure  Middle  School  for
the  past  three  years.  He  has  been  enrolled  in  the  Honors
program,  which  has  been  very  good  for  him  academically
because  it  has  allowed  him  to  develop  a  focus  on
concentration  skills.  Teachers  in  this  program  use  a  variety
of instructional  strategies  that  include  direct  teaching,  group
work,  and  a  variety  of hands-on  activities.

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Attachment  21  to  Korrell  Declaration  filed  2/12/01.

144a

Appendix  J

Andy  has  been  diagnosed  with  ADHD  and  dyslexia  and  has
been  successful  in overcoming  these  handicaps.  | believe  a
program  such  as the Biotech  Career  Academy  at Ballard  High
School  would  help  Andy  continue  to  be  successful  because
of the  standards  set  within  the  program  and  the  hands-on
activities  built  into  the  program.

Ballard  High School  is also clos > to home,  which  Andy needs
because  of the support  his parents  have to give him. Attending
a  school  farther  away  from  his  home  could  result  in
discontinuity  from  home  and  school  relationships.

Please  feel  free  to  call  me  at  McClure  (206/  378-2700)  if
you  have  any  questions.

Sincerely,

s/  Phil  Brockman
Phil  Brockman
Principal

145a

Appendix  J

To:  Joseph

Fm:  John  V

Ballard  High  School
1418  N.W.  65th  Street
Seattle,  WA 98117-5237
(206)  252-1000
(206)  252-1001  Fax

Seattle  Schools

The
Seattle  Public  Schools

Ballard  High School  offers  a unique educational  opportunity  to
its students  through  the  Biotechnology  Career  Academy.  This
is an  interdisciplinary  program  for students  with  an  interest  in
the biosciences.  Students  must  apply to the program,  including
the completion  of a teacher or counselor  recommendation  form.
Students  are  then  selected  for  the  program  based  on  interest
and  ability  to complete  the program.

This type of program  is not offered  at any other school.  Its unique
aspects  include  the  following:

Science  courses  are  centered  on  biotechnology.

*

¢

The  Biology  course  teaches  students  to  use  the
equipment  used  in  professional  biotechnology
laboratories.  This biology has a mathematics  prerequisite
higher  than  the  standard  biology  courses,  due  to  its
advanced  nature.

The Chemistry  course  in the academy  has a much  larger
focus  on  biochemistry  and  organic  chemistry  than
standard  chemistry  courses.

146a

Appendix  J

*

The Genetics  course  is a science  course  and a vocational
course  in that it is geared to teach  students  the processes
and  the  science  used  in biotechnology  lab  work.  This
class  is designed  in conjunction  with  an  internship  with
a local  biotechnology  company.

The  Academy  uses  a  coordinated,  interdisciplinary  approach.
Math,  Science  and  Language  arts  utilize  integrated  units,  make
connections  across  disciplines  and  adapt  individual  curriculum
to the interest  and needs of the Academy  students.  For example,
Ethics  in Biotechnology  are  studied  in all three  disciplines  in a
coordinated  effort.  Student  read  literature  and  professional
materials  in Language  Arts  while  studying  the  science  and  the
validity  of arguments  in  Biology.  Members  of the  scientific
community  evaluate  a culminating  project

The  Academy  allows  for  occasional  Block  Scheduling  when
needed  for Academy  activities.

The  Academy  is  managed  by an  interdisciplinary  team  of
teachers  dedicated  to the students  in the program.

The  students  arc  scheduled  as  a  cohort,  creating  a  smaller
community  of learners  within  the  context  of a  larger  school.

There  are  other  unique  aspects  of this  program  including  the
mentorships  and  internships  offered  by local  scientists,  field
trips and seminars  by scientists.  This  is a program  that  is unique
in the  Seattle  School  District.

Andy  Meeks  has  been  approved  to  be  admitted  into  the
Biotechnology  Career Academy pending placement  at Ballard
High School.

s/  [illegible]
Biotech  Academy

APPROVE  ASSIGNMENT  PLAN*

For  Introduction:  10/4/00

SCHOOL  BOARD  ACTION

Date:  October  4, 2000

To:

School  Board

From:  Joseph  Olchefske

Superintendent

Re:

Annual  Review  of  the  Elementary  and  Secondary
Student  Assignment  Plans

RECOMMENDATION

I recommend  the  following  motion  be  adopted:

I move  that,  based  on  its  annual  review  of the  student
assignment  plans,  the  Seattle  School  Board  affirm  the single
high  school  region  for  tiebreaker  purposes  and  make  no
changes  to  the  elementary  or  secondary  student  assignment
plans  for  the  2001-2002  school  year,  except  as  follows:

1.  Change  the  definition  of  an  integration  positive
school  from  +/-10%  to  +/2015%  of  the  District-
wide  minority/majority  percentage;

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Attachment  26  to  Korrell  Declaration  filed  2/12/01.

149a

Appendix  L

2.  Implement  the  integration  positive  tiebreaker  only

at entering  grade  levels;  and

3.  Apply  the  integration  tiebreaker  until  a  school’s
assignments  reach  a  neutral  integration  status.;-and

at the heh  scheet  fered,
arrendeent

sch

Furt
the
to  focus  on  continuing
Vv
schools,  particularly  those  five under subscribed  high schools
and  report  to the school  board  on  a regular  basis.

rintendent
t

irects  t
i

her

i

i

ity

DISCUSSION

Concerns  for  the  secondary  student  assignment  plan
include  the  imbalance  in  school  choice  and  school
effectiveness,  and  the  uneven  geographic  distribution  of
secondary  schools.  There  are  no  particular  issues  of concern
with  regard  to  the  elementary  program.  Options  to  address
secondary  plan  concerns  include  a  no-change  option  and  an
option  that  would  create  geographic  regions  with  a  high
preference  for between  one  to three  high schools.  Community
input  over  the  past  two  years  heavily  favored  the  no-change
option  and  a  strong  focus  on  improving  the  quality  of all
schools.

With  respect  to  the  integration  positive  tiebreaker,
seventy-five  percent  of  all  schools  are  now  outside  the
District’s  current  defined  band  of  an  integrated  school
(+/-10%  from  the  District-wide  average).  After  review  and

150a

Appendix  L

2 years  of experience,  there  was  not  strong  evidence  that
utilizing  a  +/-10%  band  provided  a  materially  better
educational  experience  than  would  a  band  of  +/-20%.
Accordingly,  in  order  to  fulfill  our  narrowly  tailoring
obligation,  staff  is recommending  a  +/-20%  band.  A  wider
band  would  allow  for a more  targeted  focus  on  schools  that
have  significant  racial  concentrations.  Currently,  the
tiebreaker  applies  at  all  grade  levels,  but  it  impacts  few
students  at  the  non-entry  level  grades.  Applying  the
integration  tiebreaker  to entry  grades only will  give it a more
targeted  impact.  Finally,  applying  the  integration  tiebreaker
until  a school  reaches  a neutral  integration  status  will  further
ensure  the  purpose  of the  tiebreaker  is met.

APPENDIX  N  —  PLAINTIFF’S  FIRST  REQUESTS
FOR  ADMISSION  AND  DEFENDANTS’  RESPONSES
(EXCERPTS)*

REQUEST  FOR  ADMISSION

Pursuant  to  Rule  36  of  the  Federal  Rules  of  Civil

Procedure,  please  admit  the  following:

REQUEST  NO  1:  Admit  that  Andy  Meeks,  son  of Jill
Kurfirst,  would  have  been  allowed  to  attend  Ballard  High
School  for the  2000-01  school  year  had his racial  status  been
non-White  rather  than  White.

RESPONSE:

Defendants  object  to this  request  as  posing  an  improper,  and
impossible,  hypothetical.  Without  waiver  of the  objection,
and  with  the  understanding  that  the  response  would  not
necessarily  be  the  same  under  a  “race  neutral”  scenario,
Admit.

REQUEST  NO.  2:  Admit  that  Andy  Meeks,  son  of Jill
Kurfirst,  would  have  been  allowed  to  attend  Nathan  Hale
High  School  for the  2000-01  school  year  had  his racial  status
been  non-White  rather  than  White.

RESPONSE:

APPENDIX  O —  DECLARATION  OF JILL  KURFIRST
IN  SUPPORT  OF  PICS’  MOTION  FOR  PARTIAL
SUMMARY  JUDGMENT  ON  STATE  LAW  CLAIMS
DATED  FEBRUARY  12, 2001*

IN  THE  UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
FOR  THE  WESTERN  DISTRICT  OF  WASHINGTON
AT  SEATTLE

No.  C00-1205R

PARENTS  INVOLVED  IN  COMMUNITY  SCHOOLS,
a  Washington  nonprofit corporation,

Plaintiff,

v.

SEATTLE  SCHOOL  DISTRICT  NO.  1, a political
subdivision  of the  State  of Washington,  et  al.,

Defendant.

DECLARATION  OF  JILL  KURFIRST  IN  SUPPORT  OF
PICS’  MOTION  FOR  PARTIAL  SUMMARY  JUDGMENT
ON  STATE  LAW  CLAIMS

1, Jill  Kurfirst,  declare:

1.

I am  a  member  of the  plaintiff  organization,
Parents  Involved  in  Community  Schools  (“PICS”).  | am
competent  to  testify  in  this  matter  and  make  the  following
declaration  based  upon  personal  knowledge.

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Attachment  9  to  Korrell  Declaration  filed  2/12/01.

1S6a

Appendix  O

2.

| am  the  mother  of  Andy  Meeks.  Andy  is
presently  a  freshman  at  Northwest  Yeshiva  High  School  on
Mercer  Island.

3.

In the  spring  of 2000,  Andy  applied  to  attend
Ballard  High  School,  hoping  to  enroll  in  its  Biotechnology
Career  Academy.  While  he  was  accepted  into  this  biotech
program,  the District's  assignment  plan denied  him placement
at  Ballard  High  School  on  the  basis  of his race  and,  instead,
assigned  him  at  Ingraham  High  School  (a school  that  was
not  among  his top three  choices).  As a result  of his failure  to
be admitted  to  Ballard,  he was  not  permitted  to enroll  in the
biotech  program  in the  fall  of 2000.

4.

| appealed  the District’s  decision  to deny Andy
a  spot  at  Ballard.  That  appeal  was  based  upon  two  grounds,
one  medical,  the  other  educational:  Andy  has  Attention
Deficit  Hyperactivity  Disorder  and  dyslexia,  conditions  that
Ballard  was  better  suited  to  handle  than  Ingraham  (because
of Ballard’s  six-period  day and  proximity  to  the  house,  and
the  biotech  program  because  of  its  hands-on  integrated
learning).  Also, the District’s  refusal  to allow  Andy the chance
to  enroll  in  Ballard’s  biotech  program  denied  him  an
educational  opportunity  that  no  other  school  in the  district
could  replicate.  After  the  first  appeal  was  rejected  by  a
District  employee,  | appealed  that  rejection.  A  two-person
appeals  board  then  rejyected  my  appeal  for a  second  time.

5.

Winnie  Bachwitz  is a  PICS  member.  Like  my
son,  her  daughter  was  accepted  into  Ballard  High  School’s
biotech  program  but  was  denied  the  chance  to  enroll  at  that
school  because  of her race.  She  was  assigned  to  Ingraham,  a

157a

Appendix  O

school  that  was  not  among  her  top  choices.  Ms.  Bachwitz
appealed  her  daughter’s  assignment  and  that  appeal  was
rejected  by the  District.

6.

Integrated  Math  | is a  math  course  typically
taken  by ninth  graders  in the Seattle  School  District.  Students
must  have  passed  Integrated  Math  I as  eighth  graders,  be
recommended  by  their  math  teacher  for  promotion  into
Integrated  Math  II, have  received  letters  of recommendation
from  their  middle  school  teachers,  and  write  an  essay
demonstrating  their  interest  in the program  in order  to qualify
for the Ballard  Biotechnology  Career  Academy.  Applications
for this program  were  due  in advance  of District  high school
assignments  and  were  reviewed  for  acceptance  into  the
program  by Biotechnology  Career  Academy  staff.

7.

I and  other  parents  use  a  variety  of objective
criteria  to  evaluate  the  quality  of District  schools.  Among
those  criteria  are  standardized  test  scores,  the availability  of
advanced  placement  courses,  the  number  of students  taking
advanced  placement  courses,  results  of  AP  tests  and  SAT
tests,  the number  of students  going on  to college,  the number
of college  preparatory  courses  offered,  the  Seattle  Times
college  preparedness  rankings,  the  University  of
Washington’s  rankings  of  high  schools,  and  disciplinary
statistics.

8.

PICS  members  believe  that  the  academic,
extracurricular,  and  other  special  programs  at  five  of  the
District’s  high schools  (Ballard,  Franklin,  Garfield,  Hale,  and
Roosevelt)  are  better  organized,  better  supported,  and  more
effective  than  those  at the remaining  schools.  My knowledge

158a

Appendix  O

of the disparities  among  District  high schools  is based  upon
review  of school  guides,  tours  of the  schools,  discussions
with  District  employees,  discussions  with  students  currently
in the  high  schools  and  with  parents  of Seattle  high  school
students,  classroom  visitations,  and  other  observations.

9.

Attending  school  close  to  home  has  many
advantages  for  students  and  parents  alike.  Reducing
transportation  time  is the  most  obvious  benefit.  If students
must  travel  long  distances  to  school  on  Metro  busses,  and
transfer  busses  en  route,  they  lose  a  significant  part  of the
day at  a  stage  in their  development  when  time  is especially
scarce  (and scarce  for their parents  too). There  are  also  safety
concerns  when  girls and boys must  spend significant  amounts
of time  waiting  at bus  stops  early  in the  morning  and  late  at
night.  Living  a  longer  distance  from  school  makes  it more
difficult  for both  students  and parents  to participate  in after-
school  and extra-curricular  activities.  Assignment  to a school
far  from  home  also  makes  it difficult  for  students  to  form
friendships  with  classmates  whom  they  meet  at  school  and
to  preserve  friendships  with  peers  they  have  known  from
childhood.  Yet,  cultivating  and  nurturing  friendships  is
especially  important  during  adolescence.

10.

—_‘ In my  experience,  parental  involvement  in my
children’s  schools  has  had  a  positive  effect  on  the  priority
that  my  children  place  on  school  work  and  on  the  quality
and  frequency  of communication  between  their  teachers  and
me.  Having  my  children  assigned  to  a  school  far  from  my
home  would  make  it more  difficult  for me  to  be  involved  in
their  schools,  and  other  PICS  parents  have  similar  concerns.

159a

Appendix  O

11.

At the high school  level,  District  high schools
offer  several  academic  offerings  for high-achieving  students,
including  honors  courses  and  advanced  placement  (“AP”)
courses.  AP courses  are  open  to students  with  the ability and
willingness  to handle  college-level  coursework,  work  that  is
a  good  deal  more  challenging  than  that  in  a  typical  high
school  class.  At the end  of an  AP course,  a student  may  take
a  test  that,  if passed,  may  allow  him  or  her  to  place  out  of
classes  in  college  or  receive  college  credit.  AP  classes  are
appealing  to  many  students  who  want  to  be  challenged  and
who  want  to  get a head  start  in their  work  toward  a college
degree.  In  making  admissions  decisions,  colleges  —
particularly  the  most  selective  ones  -  look  favorably  upon
students  who  have  challenged  themselves  in such  classes.

12.

ATTACHMENT  19 of the materials  submitted
in  support  of PICS  Motion  for  Partial  Summary  Judgment
on  Plaintiff's  State  Law  Claims  is a copy  of a pamphlet  that
was  distributed  by  defendants  to  students  and  parents
interested  in  the  Ballard  biotech  program  during  tours  and
information  sessions  held  at  the  school  during  January  and
February  of 2000.

13.

ATTACHMENT  21  of the materials  submitted
in  support  of PICS  Motion  for  Partial  Summary  Judgment
on  Plaintiff's  State  Law  Claims  is a copy  of letters  | received
from  defendants  regarding  my son’s  application  to attend  the
Ballard  Biotech  program.

160a

Appendix  O

the State of Washington,  that the foregoing  is true  and correct.

day of February,  2001.

s/  Jill  Kurfirst

Appendix  P

I, Morgan  Lewis,  declare:

1.  I am  the Manager  of Enrollmer : Planning,  Technical
Support  and  Demographics  for  Seattle  School  District  No.
1.  My  office  is  located  at  4141  -  4" Ave.  S.,  Seattle,  WA
98134.  | recerved  my master’s  degree  in economics  from  the
University  of  Washington,  which  included  specialized
training  in statistics,  projections,  demographic  analysis,  and
forecasting.  | am  competent  to testify  in this action  and make
this  declaration  on  personal  knowledge  and  the  official
records  and  files  of the  Seattle  School  District.

2.  Following  the  receipt  of official  enrollment  data  for
fall,  2000,  | analyzed  what  happened  to  the  approximately
307  students  whose  assignments  were  affected  by  the
integration  tie-breaker  for the 2000-01  school  year.  Of these
students,  | have  been  able  track  to  the  enrollment  stams  of
293  as  of October,  2000.  Of this  group,  209  were  assigned
to  a  school  that  was  one  of their  choices  (what  we  called  a
“non-mandatory  assignment”).  Of  these  non-mandatory
assignments,  87  got  the  same  school  that  they  would  have
received  without  the integration  tiebreaker.  93 additional  non-
_  mandatory  assignments  were  to  one  of the  over-subscribed
schools  (Ballard,  Hale,  Franklin,  Roosevelt,  or Garfield)  due
to  waitlist  movement  and  capacity  adjustments.  29 received
assignments  to another  school  that  was  one  of their  choices.
84 students  were  assigned  to schools  that they did not  choose
(“mandatory  assignments”):  29 of the mandatory  assignment
group  ultymately  were  assigned  to  the  same  school  as  they
would  have  received  without  the  integration  tie-breaker;
3 were  able  to  move  to  one  of the  over-subcribed  schools
due  to  waitlist  movements  and  capacity  adjustments.  The

163a

Appendix  P

remaining  52  students  received  mandatory  assignments  to
the closest  school  with  space  available.  However,  30 of these
students  did  not  enroll  in the  Seattle  Public  Schools  for  fall
2000.  Of  these  30,  16  were  not  enrolled  in  Seattle  Public
Schools  at  8" graders,  which  suggests  that  they  might  not
have  attended  in any  event.

3.  For  the  2000-01  assignment  cycle,  for  any  white
student  in the District,  if they had listed  Franklin  High School
as  one  of their  choices  (and  there  is no  limit  on  the  number
of choices),  they  could  have  been  assigned  to  that  school.
Conversely,  for  any  minority  student,  including  those
“bumped”  from  Franklin  due  to  the  integration  tie-breaker,
they could  have  been  assigned  to Ballard,  Hale, or  Roosevelt,
if they  made  those  schools  one  of their  choices.

4.  The  Board  of Directors  of the  Seattle  School  District
made  several  changes  to the  high school  assignment  plan for
2001-02,  which  will  reduce  the  impact  of  the  racial  tie-
breaker  in  the  future.  First,  the  tie-breaker  will  apply  only
at  the  entering  grades.  Therefore,  no  transfers  will  be
determined  by the integration  tie-breaker.  Second,  the  Board
changed  the definition  of “racial  balance”  from  plus or minus
10%  of the  district’s  overall  racial  demographic  to  plus  or
minus  15%.  This  has  the  effect  of rendering  Roosevelt  High
School  neutral  for desegregation  purposes,  so  that  race  will
not  be  a  factor  in  assignments  to  that  school  for  next  year.
However,  if the  same  set  of schools  is oversubscribed  and
the  past  year’s  choice  patterns  remain  the  same,  the  set  of
conditions  described  in  paragraph  3, above,  will  remain  in
place:  any  student,  regardless  of  race,  will  be  able  to  be
assigned  to  one  of the  over-subscribed  high  schools  if they

164a

Appendix  P

so  choose.  Third,  the  Board  directed  that  a “thermostat”  be
developed,  so  that  if the  integration  tie-breaker  results  in an
entering  high  school  class  coming  into  balance,  the  tie-
breaker  will  be  “turned-off.”  This  will  have  the  effect  of
reducing  the  number  of 9" grade  assignments  resulting  from
the  integration  tiebreaker.

5.  As  a  part  of  the  assignment  process,  the  School
District  calculates  the  distauce  from  a  student’s  home  to  a
school.  The  straight  line  distance  between  the  PICS’
representatives  homes,  as  identified  on  their  enrollment
records,  and  the  identified  high  schools  is as  follows:

APPENDIX  Q —  SCHOOL  BOARD ACTION  (11/17/99)
RE:  REVIEW  OF ASSIGNMENT  PLANS*

SCHOOL  BOARD  ACTION

DATE:

October  27,  1999

TO:

School  Board

FROM:  Joseph  Olchefske

Superintendent

Introduction:
Action:

11/5/99
11/17/99

RE:

Annual  Review  of the  Elementary  and  Secondary
Student  Assignment  Plans

RECOMMENDATION

| recommend  adoption  of the  following  motion:

I move  that,  based  on  its  annual  review  of the  student
assignment  plans  including  staff  reports  and  community
input,  the  Seattle  School  Board  make  no  changes  to  the
elementary  or  secondary  student  assignment  plans  for  the
2001-2002  school  year,  except  as  follows:

1.  Reaffirm  and  restate  the diversity  rationale  for
using the  integration  positive  tiebreaker  (as set
forth  in the  attached  statement);

2.  Change  the  definition  of  an  integration
positive  school  from  +/—  10%  to  +/—  20%  of

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Attachment  |7  to  Korrell  Declaration  filed  2/16/01.

166a

Appendix  Q

the  District-wide  minority/majority  percentage;
and

3.  Implement  the  integration  positive  tiebreaker

only  at  entering  grade  levels.

DISCUSSION

Concerns  for  the  secondary  student  assignment  plan
include  the  imbalance  in  school  choice  and  school
effectiveness,  the  uneven  geographic  distribution  of
secondary  schools,  and  set-aside  seats  for  programs.  There
are  no  particular  issues  of  concern  with  regard  to  the
elementary  program.  Options  to  address  secondary  plan
concerns  include  a no-change  option and an  option that would
create  geographic  regions  with  a high preference  for a single
school.  Community  input  heavily  favored  the  no-change
option  and  a  strong  focus  on  improving  the  quality  of all
schools.

With  respect  to  the  integration  positive  tiebreaker,
seventy-five  percent  of  all  schools  are  now  outside  the
district’s  current  defined  band  of  an  integrated  school
(+/—  10%  from  the  District-side  average).  If the  band  is not
changed,  more  schools  will  be subject  to the  tiebreaker  over
time.  A wider  band  would  allow  for a more  targeted  focus  on
schools  that have  significant  racial  concentrations.  Currently,
the  tiebreaker  applies  at  all  grade  levels,  but  it impacts  few
students  at  the  non-entry  level  grades.  Applying  it to  entry
grades  only  will  give  it a  more  targeted  impact.

167a

Appendix  Q

ATTACHMENTS

Board  Statement  Reaffirming  Diversity  Rationale

Staff:  /s/  MSG
Mark  Green,  John  Vacchiery

168a

Appendix  Q

DRAFT

Board  Statement  Reaffirming  Diversity  Rationale

The  Seattle  School  District  is  fortunate  to  have  the
pluralism  brought by the African  American,  American  Indian,
Asian  American,  Hispanic,  and  White  communities  and  by
the  multi-ethnic  groups  within  each.  The  school  system’s
diversity  reflects  the  increasing  pluralism  of the  U.S.  society
and emphasizes  the broader  need  for international  awareness
and  cooperation.  Diversity  in  the  classroom  increases  the
likelihood  that  students  will  discuss  racial  or  ethnic  issues
and be more  likely  to socialize  with  people of different  races.
Diversity  is thus  a valuable  resource  for teaching  students  to
become  citizens  in a  multi-racial/multi-ethnic  world.

Providing  students  the opportunity  to attend  schools  with
diverse  student  enrollment  also  has  inherent  educational
value  from  the standpoint  of education’s  role  in a democratic
society.  The  survival  and  strength  of our  democratic  society
depends  upon  an  educated  citizenry  which  shares  concerns
about  the welfare  of society,  its members,  and the democratic
principles  that  govern  it. Diversity  brings different  viewpoints
and  experiences  to  classroom  discussions  and  thereby
enhances  the  educational  process.  It also  fosters  racial  and
cultural  understanding,  which  is particularly  important  in  a
racially  and  culturally  diverse  society  such  as  ours.

The  District's  commitment  to the diversity  of its schools
and  to  the  ability  to  voluntarily  avoid  racially  concentrating
enrollment  patterns  also  helps  ensure  that  all  students  have
access  to  those  schools,  faculties,  course  offerings,  and
resources  that  will  enable  them  to  reach  their  full  potential.

169a

Appendix  Q

Based  on  the  foregoing  rationale,  the  Seattle  School
District’s  commitment  is that  no  student  should  be required
to attend  a racially  concentrated  school.  The  District  is also
committed  to  providing  students  with  the  opportunity  to
voluntarily  choose  to attend  a school  to promote  integration.
The  District  provides  these  opportunities  for  students  to
attend a racially  and  ethnically  diverse  school,  and  to  assist
in the  voluntary  integration  of a  school,  because  it believes
that providing  diverse  learning  environments  is educationally
beneficial  for  all  students.

Adopted  November  1999

170a

this  declaration  on  personal  knowledge  and  the  official
records  and  files  of the  Seattle  School  District.

2.  Exhibit  A to  my  declaration  of December  21,  2000  is a
map  depicting  the  racial  make-up  of the  neighborhoods  and
the  races  of  those  students  whose  2000-01  high  school
assignments  were  initially  determined  by  the  integration
tiebreaker.  Exhibit  A-1  is a  further  refinement  of that  map,
showing  the racial  concentration  of Seattle’s  neighborhoods
along  a  north-sound  boundary  roughly  north  of downtown
Seattle,  following  the  southern  boundary  of the  John  Hay,
Stevens,  and  McGilvra  Elementary  School  reference  areas.
Based  on  October  2000  enrollment,  58.3%  of the  District’s
total enrollment  lives  south  of this boundary.  However,  75.2%
of the  District’s  total  minority  enrollment  lives  in  this  area.
Within  each  ethnic  group,  the  following  percentages  live  in
the  southern  part  of the  City:

4.  I have  also  superimposed  circles  on  Exhibit  A-]  showing
what  the  “distance”  tiebreaker  would  have  produced  for
Ballard,  Nathan  Hale,  Roosevelt,  and  Franklin  high  schools
for  2000-01,  in  the  absence  of the  integration  tiebreaker.
In other words,  if the integration  tiebreaker  were  not  in effect,
student  living  within  these  circles  would  have  been  assigned
for  2000-01.  Students  living  outside  the  circles  would  not
have  been  assigned.

5.  In the fall of 2000,  in conjunction  with  the School  Board’s
review  of the  assignment  plan,  District  staff  developed
depictions  of “ten  region”  and  “five  region”  high  school
assignment  plans,  as  proposed  by  Directors  Petersen  and
Nielsen,  respectively.  Exhibit  B  to  this  declaration  depicts
the “ten  region”  plan, whereby  each  high school  would  have
had  its own  region  area.  Exhibit  C depicts  the  “five  region”
plan,  whereby  students  within  the  defined  area  would  be
assured  of assignment  to  one  of at  least  two  high  schools
specified  for each  region.

6.  | have  examined  the  Ninth  Grade  assignments  to  the
Biotechnology  Program  at  Ballard  High  School  for  2000-01.
Thirty-one  students  pre-qualified  for this  program,  of which
11  were  racial  minorities.  However,  only  17  (6  whites/11
minorities)  were  assigned  to  Ballard  under  the plan.  Of these

174a

Appendix  R

17,  12  are  currently  enrolled  (5 whites/7  minorities).  The
remaining  seats  in  the  program  were  filled  by  students
assigned  to  Ballard,  who  also  met  the  prerequisites  for  the
Biotech  program.  Of the Ninth  Graders  currently  participating
in  the  Biotech  program,  17  are  white  and  10  are  racial
minorities.

bal

Q.  Tell  me  why  you  found  Ballard  the  most  attractive.

A.  Ballard  was  the top of the list for a variety of reasons.
While  Andy  was  in middle  school  there  was  a similar  sort  of
push on  within  the neighborhood  to try and get neighborhood
families  who  had  previously  applied  to  Roosevelt,  Franklin
and  Garfield  to  take  more  interest  in  Ballard,  because  we
could  make  it our own  local  school.  And we  had been  pleased
with  the  way  in which  McClure  had  succeeded  in becoming
our  school  and  in educating  our  kids.

So  there  was a lot  of rallying  the  troops  at  [23]  P.T.A.
meetings  and  at  McClure  functions  where  Ballard  kids  or
Ballard  parents  would  come,  and  anyone  who  had  older
siblings  who  had  already  kind  of  taken  the  risk  and  gone
there  were  there  saying  good  things.  And  letters  came  out
from  coaches  saying,  you  know,  come  see  the  school.  And
we  would  get  invited  to  go  see  band  performances.  Andy
was  in  the  band,  so  there  was  that  affiliation  and  they  had
our  names.

So  Ballard,  over  the  course  of two  or  three  years,  went
from  being  a  schoo!  that  you  might  give consideration  to  to
the  school  that  the  kids  at  least  were  talking  from  the  very
beginning  of eighth  grade  as,  oh,  yeah,  that’s  where  we  are
going.  | mean,  to  the  extent  that  trying  to  explain  that  you
have  to  fill out  these  forms  and; yes,  we  really  have  to go see
these  other  schools;  and  yes,  you  have  to  have  at  least  three

178a

Appendix  §

choices  listed  was  me  as  an  adult  explaining  to  a  teenager
who  thought  that,  oh,  I’m  going  to  Ballard,  that’s  where
everyone  went,  and  that’s  where  everyone  wants  to  go.  So
there  was  that.

Ballard  is truly the closest  school  to our  house.  So that’s
the second  reason.  Ballard  is close  to the house.  Very easy  to
get to, harder  to get home  because  it’s getting back  up Queen
Anne  Hill  if you  were  to  bike  it, which  we  do.  It’s  much
harder  than  the  coasting  down  and  then  riding  a  bike  to
Ballard,  which  is actually  very  doable  [24]  from  where  we
were  on  Queen  Anne  Hill.

*

x

*

[28]  *

x

*

Q.  Why  wasn’t  Garfield  one  of your  choices?

A.  The  day we  drove  by Garfield  was  not  a  good  day
for Garfield.  I’m  trying  to think  of the  most  diplomatic  way
to  put  this.  And  I’ve  since  come  to  learn  why  sometimes  it
doesn’t  look  so  good  in  the  neighborhood  immediately
around  Garfield.

Which  is that,  as  odd  as  this  may  sound,  apparently  they
choose  a corner  very  near  Garfield  when  they release  people
from jail.  Adults,  I’m  talking  about  adults.  And  so  there  was
—  when  we  drove  to  Garfield  that  day,  there  were  two  or
three  blocks  surrounding  Garfield  where  there  were  police
cars,  there  were  seedy  characters,  there  were  people,  you

179a

Appendix  §

know,  doing whatever  it is called  where  you are  being frisked
against  the  car.  | mean,  you  know,  it was  not  a good  scene.

x

*

other  two,  for  instance,  that  you just asked  me  about.

decision.  That’s  even  something  that he speaks of. So  | won’t
have  to  force  him  to  leave  the  house,  thank  goodness.

Q.  You  hope.

A.  Aren’t  you  supposed  to move  while  they’re  gone  and
then  not  tell  them?  So  because  he’s  college  bound,  a  strong
academic  program  is really  what  we  were  looking  for.

really  excellent.  They  have  people  from  the community  who
[36]  have  partnered  with  the  school.

academy  where  they make  sure  each  student  has  a particular

teacher  assigned  to them  that  they meet  with  quite regularly,

Do  you  know,  is her daughter  attending  Ingraham  High

Q.  And  have  you  discussed  with  Ms.  Bachwitz,  or  her

A.  In general  terms.

Q.  Okay.  Generally,  what  do you  know?

Ingraham  for those  same  two  reasons.

Q.  Do you know  what  the particular quality  factors  were

for  her  in mak:  \g that  decision?

A.  Again,  I know  her  child  is college-bound,  and  there
was  —  and  | know  that Winnie  felt that the test  scores  or the

(10lj)*

Q. Have  you encountered  situations  where  a PICS  parent
has  indicated,  you  know,  | wanted  to  get my  child  into  this
particular  school  because,  for  instance,  they  have  a  great
basketball  program  or  track  or  any  other  sport?

A.  It’s  really  —  there  are  different  sports,  but  in part  it
has  to do with  how  close  the  school  is to  the  home.  And  this

186a

Appendix  S

was  another  one  of my famous  presentations  before  the school
board.

Because  if you  have  to  ride  three  Metro  buses  home  as
you  do  from  Ingraham  to  get  to  either  Magnolia  or  Queen
Anne  or  Ballard,  it  makes  it very  difficult  for  your  child
student  to  participate  in any  after  school  sport.  Because  by
the  time  they’re  home,  it’s  another  hour  and  45  minutes  to
two  hours  and  five  minutes,  and  then  they’re  starving  and
they  have  to  eat  because  they  haven’t  eaten  [102]  dinner,
and  then  they  have  to  do homework  and  they are  exhausted
so  they  are  not  doing  as  good  a job.  And  then  they  have  to
get  ready  to  get back  on  the  school  bus  to  get  to  school  on
time  the  next  morning.

So when  you  take  these  different  schools  into  effect,  you
want  a school  that  your  kid can  both  have  a good education,
participate  in the after  school  activities  and actually  get home
at  a  reasonable  hour  without  relying  on  them  each  having
their  own  car  or  being transported  by other  students  that  can
be,  you  know,  questionable  at best.

And  you  want  them  to  be  able  to  go  to  the  games,  and
you  want  them  to be able  to get to those  games  with  ease  and
get  home  again  safely.

Q.  I'd  like  to  know  the  percentage  and,  if you  know  it,
the gross  number  of assignment  decisions  that are  made  based
on  the  operation  of these  particular  tiebreakers.

A.  Well,  at high school  | believe  there  were  slightly  over
300  students  whose  assignment  was  a  direct  result  of  a
diversity  tiebreaker,  integration  tiebreaker.  In other  words,
without  that  tiebreaker  [32]  they  would  not  have  received
the  assignment  they  have.

contro]  what  parents  do,  in  terms  of their  choices,  and  that

sibling,  and  if we’re  using that estimate  of about  ten  percent,
then,  in essence,  virtually  all  of the  rest  of the  assignments
would  be done by distance.  And  the reason  for that is distance
is calculated  to one-hundredth  of a mile.  So you  would  have
to  have  students  with  the  exact  same  sibling,  diversity  and
distance  to have  a lottery  tiebreaker  actually  be the deciding
factor.

So lottery  happens  more  often  at schools  where  distance
isn’t  calculated.  So at regular program  high schools  distance
is calculated.  So  virtually  all  of the  remaining  assignments
will  be  done  by distance.

[39]  *x

*

*

Q.  (By  Mr.  Korrell)  I’m  sorry,  | am  asking  a  more
specific  question  than  I have  asked  before.  You have generally
described  for  me  the  Open  Choice  plan  and  the  order  of the
tiebreakers.  And  I’m  curious  to know  now  specifically  when
does  a  race  tiebreaker  come  into  play?

A.  The  race  tiebreaker  would  come  into  play  at  high
school  where,  based  on  the —  if we  think  of two  years  —  for
example,  if the  October  1999  enrollment  at  a  high  school
was  outside  of the definition  of diversity,  so  the district-wide
enrollment  was  59.8,  and  if the  school  was  plus  or  minus
more  than  ten-percent  minority  from  that  level,  the  school
would  receive  an  integration  status  that  was  positive  for the
underrepresented  group.

19la

Appendix  T

Every  student  who  chooses  that  school  in  the  choice
process  would,  as part of their calculation  of tiebreakers,  have
their ethnicity  compared  to the integration  status  of the school
to  decide  whether  or  not  their  presence  would  positively
contribute  to the diversity  of that school.  And  if [40] so,  they
would  receive  that  tiebreaker  advantage.

Q.  So  if 1 understand  this  right,  when  the  —  when  the
school’s  population  deviates  in  2000-2001  assignments,
where  the  school’s  population  deviated  by  more  than  ten
percentage  points  from  the  definition  of  diversity,  the
integration  tiebreaker  or  race-based  tiebreaker  kicks  in,
correct?

A.  It’s  —  I wouldn’t  say  “kicks  in.”  And  again,  I’m  —
there  is  a —  October | is the  official  enrollment  period  for
state  reporting.  And based  on  the demographic  composition,
racial  composition  of  a  school  October  1,  the  school’s
integration  status  for the following  year’s student  assignments
will  be  established.

Once  established  —  under  the  old  policy,  once
established,  it would  stay  that  way  for  the  entire  processing
period  for the  next  year.  So  if a high  school  was  integration
positive  for  whites  and  white  students  were  choosing  that
school  in  the  on-time  period,  they  would  receive  the
tiebreaker.  The tiebreaker  would  affect  assignments,  possibly,
if the  school  was  oversubscribed,  as  well.

Q.  And  just so  we're  clear,  under  the  2000-2001  plan,
that  happened  at  schools  where  the  racial  composition  was
more  than  ten percentage  [41] points  from  the school  district’s
established  balance?

192a

Appendix  T

A.  We  had  high school  —  we  had  regular  program  high
schools  who  went  into  that  2000-2001  processing  being
designated  as  integration  positive  for  whites  or  minorities,
and  we  also  had  those  schools  be  oversubscribed.  And
therefore,  that  tiebreaker’s  impact  would  be felt.

a

Q.  What  determines  the  capacity  of  the  individual

schools?

A.  Principals,  school  site  councils,  the  superintendent.

[44]  From  my  perspective,  I see  it as  a  collaborative
process.  Schools  request  a certain  capacity  that they feel they
can  serve  in  their  building  for  physical  reasons,  academic
reasons,  programmatic  reasons.  And  that’s  the capacity  I run
off of, in terms  of trying  to process  students  into  those  seats.

Q.  Am I right,  then,  that  a  school’s  physical  capacity
could  exceed  the established  capacity  for the year  but would
be determined  by other  factors,  such  as program  and academic
goals?

A.  Capacity  is —  it’s  a  tough  number  to  pin down.  An
example,  30 years  ago there  were  100,000  students  in Seattle
and  high schools  ran  at 3,000  students  and  ran  two  shifts.  So
it’s, —  1 mean  capacity  is  — I mean  it’s what  it is, in terms  of
the  way  we  choose  to  run  our  buildings,  but,  at  the  same
time,  the deference  to me  is to the academics  of the program

193a

Appendix  T

managers,  the  principals  to  say  this  is how  many  students
we  can  serve  comfortably  or  safely  or  whatever.

Q.  And  you  are  talking  to  someone  who  is uninitiated
in the  ways  of education  policies,  so  I am  trying  to learn  the
terminology.

My  understanding  is that  there  is  a —  it [45]  sounds
like  there  is a difference  between  what  the district  determines
—  this  collaborative  process  as  the  school’s  capacity  for  a
year,  that’s  different  from  a simple  calculation  of the school’s
physical  capacity,  is that  correct?

A.  It’s  very  possible  that  the  physical  calculation,  the
number  of classrooms  times  the number  of students  per,  you
know,  those  kind  of calculations,  could  be different  than  the
capacity  that  the  school  feels  they  can  comfortably  serve.
And  there  could  be dozens  of reasons  why  those  differ.

Q.  Sure.  I’m just trying  to understand  the  terms.

For  the  district  to  be  able  to  operate  a  race-based
tiebreaker,  it is necessary,  of course,  for the  district  to  know
the  race  of the  student  at issue,  correct?

A.  Correct.

Q.  And  it’s my understanding  that that  is something  that
a parent  does  when  registering  for attendance  at Seattle  Public
Schools,  is indicate  the  race  of his  or  her  child,  correct?

A.  There  is a  space  on  that  registration  form  for  race,

yes.

194a

Appendix  T

[46]  Q.  Do  you  know  what  happens  to  a  child’s  —  let

me  step  back.

How  is a child  classified  for purposes  of the  operation
of these  tiebreakers  if his parents  refuse  to identify  the child’s
race?

A.  My  understanding  —  the  enrollment  center,  the
enrollment  processing  is really  not  my area  of expertise.  But
my  understanding  is  that  the  enrollment  center  specialists
will make  a determination  based  on  the ethnicity  of the parent
who  is present.

Q.  A person,  a parent  will  fill out  a form,  [47] refuse  to
mark  race.  Is it your  understanding  that the district  employee
receiving  this registration  form  will mark  a race  for that child
based  on  his  or  her  perception  of the  parent?

A.  That’s  my  understanding.

Q.  Are  these  forms  always  submitted  in  person,  or  is

there  a mechanism  for  mailing  them?

A.  The  only  time  you  would  fill  one  of these  forms  out
is if you’re new  to the district.  And a new  person  to the district
is  required  to  physically  show  up  somewhere  to  establish
whatever.  I mean  you  can’t  be  a  new  person  and  just  show
up  through  the  mail  or  something.

195a

Appendix  T

Q.  Do  you  know  what  happens  —  do you  know  how  a
student  is classified  for  purposes  of the  operation  of these
tiebreakers  if the  parent  indicates  more  than  one  racial.
category?

A.  I do not.  There  are  racial  categories  that  reflect  that
particular  designation.  For  example,  Hispanic  black  is  a
category  that a parent  could  choose  and could  indicate.  But  I
don’t  know  how  they  would  do something  that  isn’t  part  of
the group.

Q.  Am  | correct  there  is no  category  that  would  allow  a

parent  to  mark  both  Caucasian  and  25  black,  for  instance?

[48] A.  There  is not.

[57] Q.  Let  me  step  back.  You  explained  to me  the kind

would  be in balance,  which  schools  would  be out  of balance,
depending  on  which  —  on  what  the  definition  of balance
was,  correct?

A.  Right.

Q.  Do  you  remember  being  asked  to do any  research  or
run  any studies  or tests  to determine  the effect  on  the district's
racial  composition  of  other  approaches  to  student
assignments?

196a

Appendix  T

A.  Not  with  respect  to  the  diversity  of the  school,  no.

Q.  With  respect  to other  aspects,  do you  recall  anything

like  that?

A.  Well,  there  were  —  both  Director  Peterson  and
Director  Nielsen  proposed  regional  plans  that  were
considered  at  the  time  that  they  adopted  the  plans  in
November.

Q.  Were  you  asked  to do anything  with  respect  to those

plans?

A.  I provided  capacity  and  resident  head  counts  of the
proposed  regional  plans  from  the  perspective  of were  there
enough  seats  within  a  region  to  meet  the  residents  if every
student  chose  [58]  to  go to that  school.

Q.  Did the district  do any analysis  of the effect  that these
plans  would  have  had  on  the  racial  balance  of the  district’s
high  schools?

A.  I can’t  say  about  the  racial  balance  in  the  high
schools.  We,  in  the  regional  plans,  we  looked  at  the  racial
composition  of the  resident  students.  But  that  was  mainly
from  the  perspective  of if  | draw  a  region  around  a  school
that  has  1,000  high school  students,  is there  a high school  in
that  region  that  can  serve  1,000,  and  what  are  the
demographics  of those  students  who  live  there?  It doesn’t
address  whether  or  not  they  will  choose  that  school  or  not.

197a

Appendix  T

So what  the school  actually would  look  like  is something
I can’t  answer.  You can  make  the assumption  that  if everyone
chooses  it this  is what  it would  look  like.

district  staff to estimate  what  those  choices  may  be and what
the  consequent  racial  balance  would  be?

A.  Not  in  choices,  just  in  resident  populations  of the

underlying  geographic  regions.

considered  by the  board,  prior  to  the  November  change  in

that  involved  free  and  reduced  lunch  price  status.

A.  Other  than  counting  kids  who  were  eligible  for  free
and  reduced  lunch  by grade.  That  particular  proposal  never
went  anywhere,  in  terms  of  assessing  how  you  would
implement  it,  so  that  I could  say  to  him  this  is  how  many
students  would  or  would  not  be  eligible  for  your  proposed
tiebreaker.

Q.  What  do  you  mean,  it “never  went  anywhere”?

A.  Director  Nielsen  never  asked  for  more  information.

kids  who  are  free  lunch  kids  and things  like  that, but  I don’t

[61]  Q.  Do  you  know  off the top of your  head?

tiebreaker  level  than  it currently  is used?

A.  The  only  discussion  about  lottery  was  your  first
comment,  what  would  a  pure  lottery  look  like.  The  answer
is it would  look  like  the  district  average  at  every  school.

I mean  if the lottery  was  perfectly  random,  then  we’d  be
—  we'd  be —  every  school  would  be the district  average,  at
least  with  respect  to the kids  that we  process  in the, you  know,
the  on-time  period,  because  that’s  the  only one  it applies  to.

You  testified  there  was  some  discussion  of what  a  pure
