Q.  And  is that the same  source  for your  information  here
about  the population  of school-aged  children  being about  50
percent  white?

A.  No.  That  is a  combination  of what  we  know  about
Seattle  public school  children  and specifically  what  we  know
about  private  school  students  in this  area.  It doesn’t  —  I mean
there  would  be  homeless  —  there  would  be  some  students
that  wouldn’t  be  in  that  percentage.  But  basically  the  50
percent  white  reflects  a combination  of public  and  private  in
the  year  that  we  actually  have  data  reported.

Q.  So  this  50  percent  white  figure  for  school-aged
children  is  taken  from  the  data  submitted  to  the
Superintendent  of Public  Instruction?

A.  Yes.

20la

Appendix  T

Q.  Do you  have  any  information  about  the —  about  any
trends  in  the  racial  demographic  of the  City  of  Seattle
subsequent  to the  1990  census  data that you  referred  to here?

A.  No  significant  trends  that  I’m  aware  of.

Q.  What  do  you  do,  if anything,  to  keep  yourself

apprised  of that  kind  of information?

A.  Well,  unfortunately,  sometimes  city-wide  [70]  data
is hard  to obtain,  other  than  census  data.  It’s  one  of the only
times  where  we  count  heads.  But  Puget  Sound  Regional
Council  provides  regional  forecast  information,  not  always
by minority/majority  categories.  We  can  look  at  migration
data  from  the  state,  primarily  driver’s  license  information
about  in  and  out  migration.  There  are  a  few  forecasting
agencies  in  the  city  that  try  to  project  employment  and
growth.

And  other  than  that,  we  have  the  luxury  of  knowing
exactly  what  our  individual  students  are  each  year.  So that’s
not  so  much  as  a projection  as  an  actual  counting.

Q.  With  respect  to the city’s overall  racial  demographic,
have  you  done  any  research  to determine  whether  there  have
been  changes  to this  75/25  breakdown  since  the  1990  census?

A.  No.

Q.  Do  you  know  if anyone  at the  district  has  attempted

to do its own  update  of that  1990  data?

A.  No.

[75]  And  the  final  question  on  this  paragraph,  it’s  the

Q.  The  next  paragraph,  paragraph  eight,  you  say  that,
in  the  second  sentence,  “Seattle  has  had  and  continues  to
have  racially  concentrated  neighborhoods.”

What  do  you  mean  by  “racially  concentrated

neighborhoods”?

A.  Areas  of the city where  the underlying  population  of
either  public  school  districts  or  residents  is primarily  white
or  minority.

Q.  Now,  you’ve  mentioned  two  different  measures,  the
population  of public  school  districts  and  overall  population.
To  which  do  you  refer  here  when  you  refer  to  “racially
concentrated  neighborhoods”?

A.  | think  both.

Q.  And  do  you  have  a  cutoff  in  mind  of  when  a
neighborhood  constitutes  a racially  concentrated  neighborhood
as  opposed  to one  that  is not  racially  [76] concentrated?

204a

Appendix  T

A.  No.  | mean  I guess  it’s —  it’s a statistical  comparison
of saying  what  the  average  in the  city is and  then  looking  at
places  where  that’s  above  or  below  that  average.  In  other
words,  if the city before  was  75 percent  white  and  25 percent
minority,  if there  were  areas  of the  city  that  were,  say,  60
percent  minority,  | would  consider  that to be concentrated  as
comparison  to  the  city average.

Q. Is that  what  you  mean  by racially  concentrated,  then,
that  the  population  differs  substantially  from  the  city-wide
breakdown?

A.  The  city  is  a —  the  city  doesn’t  distinguish,  it’s  the
entire  city’s  average  within  that  city.  If you  break  it up  into,
say,  reference  areas  that  we  provide,  you  may  find  reference
areas  that are  dramatically  different  composition  than  the city
average.  So that’s  what  I’m  referring  to.

Q.  So the city average  is approximately  75 percent  white,
25  percent  minority  based  on  your  —  based  on  the  census
data  you  referred  to, correct?

A.  Uh-huh.

Q.  So  if a  neighborhood  is  75  percent  white  and  25
percent  minority,  that  is  not  a  racially  [77]  concentrated
neighborhood  the  way  you're  using  this  term  because  it
reflects  the  city  ave-age?

A.  Well,  | guess  that’s  kind  of a “Yes”  and “No.”  I mean
it is —  it is the  city  average,  but  one  group  as  compared  to
the other  is significantly  overrepresented,  in terms  of to each

20Sa

Appendix  T

other,  compared  to  the  district  average,  that  that  looks  like
the  district  average.  The  average  is not  50/50,  is where  I’m
at on  that.

Q.  I understand  that.  And  what  I’m  trying  to  figure  out
is  you  refer  to  something  that  you  are  calling  racially
concentrated  neighborhoods.

A.  Right.

Q.  And  I’m just  curious,  when  you  were  thinking  of a
racially  concentrated  neighborhood,  what  are  you  referring
to?  Are  you  referring  to  one  that  is  —  has  a  —  that  is
significantly  different  from  the racial  breakdown  of the whole
city,  or  if  a —  just  55  percent  white  and  then  45  percent
minority,  does  that  constitute  racial  concentration?

A.  In the  context  of this  map,  I think  it’s  —  it’s  fair  to
say  that  it’s  looking  at  whether  or  not  one  specific  minority
or  majority  group  is the  predominant  group  in that  area.  In
other words,  an  area  that was  80 percent  white  and 20 percent
[78]  minority  would  have  a  high  concentration  of  white
residents.  It might be the district  average  in the example,  but
it still  has  a  high  concentration  of white  residents.

Q.  That  much  I understand.

A.  Okay.

Q.  What  I’m  trying  to  understand  is  is  that  what  you
mean  by a racially  concentrated  neighborhood?  We'll  look
at the  map  that  you’ve  attached  as  Exhibit  A.  I'd  like  to  talk

206a

Appendix  T

about  the  specifics  on  that  in  a  second.  But  a  hypothetical
neighborhood  of 75 percent  white,  25 percent  minority,  is that a
racially  concentrated  neighborhood?

A.  Yes.

Q.  So it’s not just a deviation  from the district  average  that

A.  Again,  I know  the characteristics  of the district  students,
in  terms  of those  kids.  And  I’m  not  sure  —  maybe  I’m  not
answering  your  question.

Q.  Yeah, and I’m not trying  to have an argument with you,
I’m  just  trying  to  figure  out  what  you  mean  by “racially
concentrated  neighborhoods”  [79]  when  you  say,  “Seattle
has  had  and  continues  to  have  racially  concentrated
neighborhoods.”

A.  The generic —  the general —  the general  interpretation
is —  or  my  general  message  in  that  is that  we  don’t  have  a
homogeneous  district,  it doesn’t  look  the same  from  one  end to
the other.  There  are  areas  of the city that are — like | said, if the
average  is 75/25,  there  are  areas  that  look  like  that,  but  that  is
still not what  I call homogenous.  It might be the higher average.
There  are  also  areas  that  are  95 percent  white  and  five  percent
minority  and  95  percent  minority  and  five  percent  white.

The  concentrations  that  I’m  referring  to,  it’s  at  least  my
intent  to  show  the  district  is not just one  smooth  demographic
from  the entire  end.  And  that’s  really  all I’m  trying  to show  or
trying  to 1llustrate.

to  “racially  concentrated

A.  Yes,  yes.

school  students  that  I do know  more  about, more  so than census

district  is getting  more  racially  concentrated  in  area,  but  the

A.  Adding  on  to  that  —  I mean  the  private  school
enrollment  is another  factor,  which  is —  in some  sense  it’s  the
remainder  of who  is left of school-aged  children,  more  or  less.
And  that  demographic  is not  changing,  in  terms  of minority/
majority  in a dramatic  —  or  in a trendable  way.

So  combining  those  two,  | would  —  my  best  estimate
would  be no.  But  the  scenario  you  illustrated  a moment  ago  is
possible,  I mean  where  [83]  the  resident  population  is doing
one  thing  and  the  student  enrollment  is doing  something  else.
That  is possible.

Q.  Why  has  that  —  the  idea  of magnet  programs  not

in our  various  schools.

tourism  academy  at  West  Seattle,  we  have  a  technology
immersion  program  at  Cleveland.  We  just  put  in  the
International  Baccalaureate  at  Ingraham.  We  started  a
program  under  Stanford’s  leadership  at Meany  Middle  School
that didn’t  work  and  I think  was  —  he put the wrong  principal

to  that  school?

we  go  forward.

Q.  And  why  is that?

213a

Appendix  U

A.  I think we’re  doing a better job, I think we  have better
leadership,  and  I think  we  have  better  programs  that  we’re
putting  in place.

Q.  Did  you  ever  discuss  using  race  for  voluntary

transfers  only?

A.  What  do you  mean  by that?

Q.  And  when  I’m  asking  these  questions  I’m  referring
in  the  context  of  when  you  were  moving  away  from
Controlled  Choice.  There  has  been  notes  in  the  literature
about  having  voluntary  racial  transfers,  where  you  wouldn’t
—  you  wouldn’t  deny  anyone  admission  to  a  school  on  the
basis  of race  or force  anyone  to go to a school  on  the basis  of
race,  but  you  would  allow  somebody  to  voluntarily  —  they
would  get preference  to voluntarily  transfer  to another  school
based  on  the race  and the racial  makeup  of that other  school,
as  | understand  it.

A.  That’s  been  discussed.

*

*

*

[33]  *

*

*

Q.  Why  is that?

A.  Well,  the  statistics  are  pretty  —  the  research  is very
clear  that  poverty  is  a  much  bigger  factor  in  academic
achievement  than  is  race.  And  the  other  reason  is  the
definition  of race  is becoming  more  and  more  cloudy  as  our
society  becomes  more  and  more  integrated.

214a

Appendix  U

At  what  percent  of blood  line  is a  person  an  African-
American?  | don’t  know.  But  we  have  —  we  have  people
with  ten  percent,  20  percent,  30  percent,  40  percent,  50
percent,  90 percent,  100  percent.  At  what  point  is someone
an  African-American?

Q.  Why  is  that  issue  problematic  for  the  assignment

plan?

A.  Well,  you  have  to  mark  down  your  race  on  [34]  the
sheet.  And  I think  race  is becoming  less  and  less  of an  issue.

Q.  Are you  saying that  it —  it opens  the door  for people

to  abuse  what  race  they can  mark,  based  on  —

A.  Oh, obviously  it does  that. And some  people do abuse

various  races.

*

*

Q.  Do you  think  that  the Seattle  School  District  has been
—  the  Seattle  School  District  high  schools  have  been
segregated  at any time  in the past  [37] 20 years,  as  you define
the  word?

A.  Have  they been  segregated  in the  last  20 years?  No.

Q.  Do you  know  why the school  district  uses  the 60/40

number  that  they  use  for  their  racial  balance  guideline?

21Sa

Appendix  U

A.  Well,  I think  they  believe,  “they”  being  my
colleagues,  that we  should  use  the racial  makeup of the city’s
school  system  as  the  basis  to  determine  racial  balance.  |
happen  to disagree  with  that.

Q.  What  do you  think  should  be used?

A.  I don’t  think  we  should  use  race  at all.

Q.  Has  the  board  ever  looked  at  using  the  makeup  of
the  high  school  aged  children  in Seattle,  private  and  public
school?

A.  No,  I don’t  think  that —  not  in my  time.

Q.  What  about  the  city as  a whole,  the  racial  makeup

of the  City of Seattle  as  a whole?

A.  Well, if you were  going to use  race,  which  I wouldn’t
do,  but  if you  were,  I would  certainly  use  that  percentage
before  I would  use  the one  we  use.

Q.  And  why is that?

A.  To  me  it makes  more  sense.

One  of the reasons  we  have  60 percent  minorities  in the
school  system  is not  because  we  are  [38] so  attractive  to the
minority  population,  it’s  because  we  have  been  unattractive
to the  majority  population.

*

*

*

216a

Appendix  U

Q.  So  in your  mind,  you  would  just  —  you  would  not
use  race  as  a tiebreaker  and  let schools  go to their  natural  —
just  natural  choice  patterns?

A.  That  would  be my  preference,  but  I think  in order  to
gain  support  of  my  colleagues  I would  have  to  have  the
economic  factor  involved  to  get  anything  passed.  | would
not  be  successful  in  not  having  some  criteria  that  would
diversify  our  schools.

[39]  Q.  Do  you  know  why  diversity  1s so  important  to
your  colleagues?  I’ve  talked a little  bit  about  them  thinking
it has  educational  value.  Any  other  reasons?

A.  Well,  | think  the  history  has  been  that  minority-
impacted  schools  have  traditionally  been  less  effective  in the
education  of  children  than  majority  schools.  And  so  the
feeling  is that  in order  to effectively  educate  minority  children
we  must  get  them  into  schools  that  have  a  large  percentage
of majority.  That’s  the  feeling.  But  the  fact,  in my  mind,  the
fact  that  minority-impacted  schools  are  not  effective
educational  learning  environments  has  nothing  to  do  with
race  and  everything  to  do  with  the  educational  program  and
quality  and  capabilities  of the  adults  in  that  building.

*

Q.  The  bottom  paragraph  of this  page  says,  “Based  on
the  foregoing  rationale,  the  Seattle  School  District's
commitment  is that  no  student  should  be required  to attend  a

226a

Appendix  V

A.  Uh-huh.

Q.  What  is a  racially  concentrated  school?

A.  A  racially  concentrated  school  is a  school  in which
the student  body is overwhelmingly  one  [58] particular  racial
group.

Q.  What  does  “overwhelmingly”  mean?  Is there  a cutoff
beyond  which  a school  is concentrated  and  before  which  it’s
not  concentrated?

A.  Well,  | don’t  think  —-  the  real  life  answer  to  your
question  is no,  of course  it’s  not.  Ultimately,  it’s  in the  eye
of the  beholder.  But  you  know,  our  sense  here  was  there  has
been  a  history  in  our  country  of,  you  know,  getting  back  to
the  south  in  the  50s,  of forcing  black  kids  to  go  to  schools
with  only  black  kids  and  white  kids  to  go  to  school  with
only  white  kids,  and  that’s  the  way  It 1s.

percent  white  student  population  as  opposed  to  overall  city

use,  | suppose,  the  measure  of  the  school-aged  children
aggregate  measure,  you  could  use  the  city-wide  population

student-based  school-aged  population  itself versus  aggregate

adult  population  is a far more  relevant  reality  for my daughter

or  as  precise  as  we're  going  to  get  measure,  is school-aged
population  that  chooses  to  come  to  Seattle  Public  Schools.
And  —  period.

Q.  How  is  it that  the  district’s  goals,  as  articulated  in
the  rationale  that  include  its  being  prepared  to  be  a  good
citizen  in a multi-cultural  community,  how  ts that  goal  better
served  by  using  60/40  rather  than  the  broader  community
ratio  of approximately  25 percent  minority/75  percent  white?

A.  Let’s  think  about  the  world  in  which  [69]  they’re
going  to  be citizens.  The  world  in which  they’re  going  to  be
citizens  is the  population  that  is their  contemporaries.  And
their  contemporaries  are  school-aged  children.  And  my
mother,  who  is  84  years  old,  lived  in  an  overwhelmingly
white  world  because  the  world  was  overwhelmingly  white
in her  age  group.  My daughter,  who  is six  years  old,  lives  in
a  very  diverse  world  because  her  age  group  1s significantly
more  diverse  than  my  84-year-old  mother.

So  that  is a  society  they're  going  to  inhabit.  You  know,
we  shouldn't  be  preparing  kids  to  be  blacksmiths,  because

236a

Appendix  V

that’s  not  the  world  they’re  going  to  inhabit.  We  should  be
preparing  kids  to  live  in a  technologically,  information  rich
environment,  because  that’s  the  world  they’re  going  to
inhabit.  They’re  also  going to inhabit  a world  that  is far more
racially  and  culturally  diverse  than  you  will  ever  inhabit  in
your  life  or  | will  in my  life.

Q.  Can  you  explain  how  the  district’s  goals  are  better

in a  school  like  Ballard  from  37  percent  to  44  percent  or  in
Roosevelt  in moving  the minority  percentage  from  45 percent
to  [70]  49  percent?  How  do  those  marginal  changes  in
minority  representation  in  those  schools  help  the  district
affect  its  goals?

A.  Ultimately,  the  tiebreaker  or  a  student  assignment
plan  is  a  blunt  instrument.  And  I accept  it  as  a  blunt
instrument;  that’s  what  it is.  It’s  not  —  you  know,  you  —
you  don’t  have  surgical  instruments  in this  world.

Is  it  moving  the  right  direction?  Is  it  creating
opportunities  for kids  from  diverse  populations  to attend  that
school?  Is it —  is it an  instrument,  blunt  though  it may  be, to
serve  —  to  serve  that  purpose?  My answer  to  the  question  is
yes.  It’s  —- it’s the best  instrument  we  have,  and  it —  I believe
it works  to  serve  that  end.

Q.  Can  you  —

A.  On  the  margin,  | mean  is  there  something  magic
between  37 and 44?  Again,  I don’t  —-  you  know,  it’s  begging
imprecision  that  doesn’t  exist  in this  world.

academy  at  Cleveland,  the  performing  arts  program  at  West
Seattle,  leadership  changes  at  West  Seattle  High  School  and
Sealth  and  Rainier  Beach,  dramatically  changing  our
leadership  profile  of those  schools,  commitment  of additional
energy  and  resources  around  high schools.  Went  out  and  got
the  Gates  Grant  to  do  school  transformation  all  throughout
our  district,  particularly  focusing  on  high  schools.

The  programmatic  aspects,  | mean  clearly  the choice  plan
at  the  end  of the  day gives  the  superintendent  and  gives  the
school  district  immediate  feedback  from  parents  on  a  year-
to-year  basis  about  what  they  find  attractive  and  what  they
don’t  find  attractive.  And  we  use  that  information  to  drive
our  reform  and change  efforts  at individual  schools.  And there
is —  I can  tell  you,  as  a  result  of the  changing  dynamics  of
choice,  I think  we've  learned  that  the things  Ballard  has done
in the  last four years  parents  have  responded  to, and  the things
Rainier  Beach  has  done  in  the  last  four  years  parents  [77]
have  not  responded  to.  And  guess  what?  We  learn  from  that
and  we  try  to  replicate.

240a

Appendix  V

So  your  question  implied  nothing  else  is going  on.  And
in  fact,  in the  overall  scope  of things,  | spend  far more  time
on  those  things  than  | would  ever  spend  on  student
assignment,  because  I consider  it a  blunt  instrument.  The
brain  surgery  of all  this  exists  in programmatic  changes  that
are  tailored  to  the  evolving  and  constantly  changing  interest
and  needs  of parents.

Q.  What  does  the  district  do to  advertise  to  parents  the

programmatic  differences  in the  various  high  schools?

A.  To  answer  that  question  I got  to  change  a  word  in
the  question.  I think  the  word  “advertise”  is a bad  word  or  a
narrowly  defining  word.  | think  if the intent  of your  question
is Communicate  to  parents  —  am I taking  that  as  the spirit  of
your  question?

Q.  Yes.

A.  Okay.  Then  | think  —  I think  we  have  a  very  broad
program  that  we  —  each  year  I think  we've  gotten  better  at.
I think  we’ve  come  from  a  place  of not  being  very  strong  at
it.  The  strongest  place  is what  I call  back  fence  marketing,
it’s  the  [78]  parent-to-parent  contact.  And  the  more  you  can
create  happy  customers  at  one  school,  they  talk  about  their
happiness.

| mean  I think  Ballard  is  a good example.  I think  McClure
Middle  School  is another  great  example.  No  one  wanted  to
go there  four  years  ago,  and  | think  the change  in leadership
and  change  in  program  today,  it’s  becoming  a  far  more
popular  school  than  it was  three  years  ago.  And  so  I think  at

24la

Appendix  V

the end of the day, the core  resources  —  the  core  resource  of
that  communication  effort  is parent  satisfaction  and  having
parents  be advocates  to other  parents.

Clearly,  my  work  and John  Stanford’s  work  before  me,  |
mean  he  sort  of invented  this  game  plan  around  the  high
visibility  superintendency  and  the need  to communicate  very
broadly  all around  the  community  of the  role  of our  schools
and  the  sort  of cheerleading  function  of the  superintendent
and  the  board.  I  clearly  focus  my  energy  and  my
communicating  efforts  around  those  schools.  We've
developed  in  the  last  couple  of years  | think  a  much  more
sophisticated  communications  department.  And  | work  very
closely  to  develop,  for  lack  of  a  better  term,  marketing
materials  on  a  school-by-school  [79]  basis.

So  this  is a  fairly  —  and  again,  in a choice  district,  this
is  what  you've  got  to  do.  | mean  you  move  from  a  Soviet
organizational  structure  to much  more  of a capitalist,  if | can
use  that  shorthand  for the  moment.  And  you've  got to  make
a  commitment  to  be  attractive  to  parents  in  their  ongoing
changing  and  evolving  interests.

Q.  Why  did  you  propose  changing  the  trigger  for  the
operation  of the tiebreaker  from  a ten-percent  point deviation
from  the 60/40  racial  balance  to a 20-percent  point deviation?

242a

Appendix  V

A.  | guess  there  is three  things  I need  to  say.  First  is I
very  much  wanted  to,  and  | think  we  collectively  wanted  to,
to  maintain  a  diversity  tiebreaker  or  some  element  into  the
plan  that  had  a  very  clear  direction  around  diversity.

Second,  I wanted  to  soften  ‘ts  impact.  | felt  and  we
collectively  felt  through  our  —  this  iterative  process  that
some  softening  was  important.  And  the  broadening  of the
band  was  one  part of that  softening.  The  entering  grade  issue
was  another  one,  and the thermostat  as  opposed  to the on/off
switch  was  the  third  piece  of that.

And  so  I certainly  felt

Q.  Let  me  interrupt  you  one  second.  Was  Barnhart/
Waldman,  the  elimination  of  Barnhart/Waldman  also  a
softening  effort?

A.  No.  At  least  I didn’t  think  of it that  way.

Q.  I’m  sorry,  | interrupted  you.

A.  So  my  first  point  was  maintain  the  [82]  component.
Second,  soften  its impact.  Third,  you've  got to remember,  as
superintendent,  | am  an  employee  and  under  contract  to seven
board  members.  And  this  issue  is fundamentally  their  issue,
it’s  their  policy  issue.  And  my job is to  bring  to  the  table,  as
a result  of this  iterative  process  that  I’ve  been  talking  about,
a  package,  a  proposal  that  gets  on  the  table  for  them  to
discuss.

243a

Appendix  V

So  you  know,  | think  I need  to  soften  the  personal
ownership  to  the  degree  not  that  I’m  walking  away  from  it,
I’m  just  saying  part  of my  job  is to  get  these  topics  on  the
table.

[85]  *

*

A.  Correct.

Q.  The  added  text  under  four,  the  school  board  directs
you  to  focus  on  continuing  improvement  and  quality  to  all
high schools,  particularly  those  five  undersubscribed  schools,
and  report  to  the  school  board  on  a  regular  basis.  What  1s
your  understanding  of why you  were  to  focus  particularly  on
the  five  undersubscribed  schools?

A.  At  the  end  of the  day,  the  evil  in  the  middle  of the
difficulties  that  we’ve  had  this  year  regarding  high  school
student  assignments,  or  the  middle  school  the  year  before
that,  various  elementaries  at  various  times  along  the  way  —
| mean  this  is  simply  this  year’s  installment  of an  ongoing
issue  of how  you  be  a  choice  district.  But  clearly  the  evil  at

244a

Appendix  V

the  root  of it all  is ultimately  the  parent  choice  results  that
clearly  indicated  parents  in larger  numbers  felt  some  of our
schools  were  very  attractive  and  other  schools  weren't
attractive.  Larger  numbers  over  here,  smaller  numbers  over
here.  And  that,  as  a  result,  we  had  long  waitlists  over  here.
Regardless  of what  we  did  about  student  [87]  assignment  at
the  end  of the  day,  that  was  just  going  to  —  if the  choice
patterns  stayed  the  same,  the  only  issue  we  were  going  to
resolve  with  student  assignment  was  who  was  going  to  be
angry.

The  only way  we  could  get at a net  increase  in happiness
is by increasing  the parental  attractiveness,  which  would  then
have  the  effect  of  lowering  the  waitlists  and  hopefully
eliminating  the  waitlists  at  the  five  more  currently  more
popular  schools.

So  | think  it  was  an  acknowledgement  that  student
assignment  was  really  only  one  piece  of how  you  resolve
this  issue.

Q.  This  sentence  we're  discussing  here  does  not  refer
to  improving  parent  perception  of high  schools,  it refers  to
improving  quality  at  the  five  undersubscribed  schools.

Is  it your  understanding  that  there  is a  quality  problem

at  the  five  undersubscribed  schools?

A.  I think  there  is clearly  an  attractiveness  problem.  And
attractiveness,  In my  mind,  is a  measure  that  gets  at what  do
parents,  in numbers,  feel  is quality.  And  as  I said  earlier,  that
is  ultimately  a  fluid,  a  changing,  a  dynamic  phenomenon.

24Sa

Appendix  V

And  so  there  clearly  is an  issue  —  and  part  of that  [88]  is
what  is the substance  of the program.  And  then  second,  what
is —  how  is that  substance  communicated.  And  if parents,
by going  through  the  choice  process,  are responding  to both
what  they  know  —  the  substance  and  what  they know  about
it, ultimately  it clearly  identifies  attractiveness  issues.

And  so  | think  quality  —  I struggle  when  you  ask  me  the
question  the  way  you  did,  because  ultimately  there  are  many
people  that  chose  Ingraham,  there  are  many  people  that  chose
West  Seattle,  there  are  many  people  that chose  Sealth,  Rainier
Beach,  Cleveland.  All  of those  schools  have  been  chosen  by
parents.  So obviously  they,  those  particular  parents  see  them
as  quality  choices,  because  they  had  other  choices.  There  is
a question  of is there  broad  enough  numbers  and  ts the nature
of that  program  perceived  as  quality  broadly.

Q.  And  I guess  the  reason  |  focused  on  the  word
“quality”  is that  the  direction  to  you  1s for  you  to  focus  on
quality  improvements  —

A.  Sure.

Q.  —  not  just  perception  improvements.

A.  And  I guess  when  I say  the  word  “attractiveness,”  |

think  it captures  both  of those  [89]  premises.

*

*

*

The  second  sentence  here  says,  “After  review  and  2 years
experience,  there  was  not  strong  evidence  that  utilizing  a

246a

Appendix  V

10%  ten-percent  band  provided  a  materially  better

that  the  plus  or  minus  ten-percent  band  did  not  provide  a

of 20  percent?

A.  Well,  | think  that  gets  at  the  sort  of blunt  instrument

any  student  assignment  process  you’re  balancing  a  number
of competing  interests;  diversity  clearly  being  one  of them,
first  choice  being  another,  and  close  to  home  being  another,
et cetera,  et cetera.

And  so I think  this  gets  at  that  idea,  that  widening  the
band,  what  | called  softening  earlier,  could  get  at  improved
—  could  —  we  could  make  that  [90]  change  without  having
a  negative  impact,  given  the  other  competing  interests  that
are  involved.

{Q.]  The  very  last bullet  here  under  “Controlled  Choice”
on  the  second  page  says,  “No  more  than  58%  enrollment  of
any  ethnic  minority  group.”

What  does  that  refer  to?

A.  Under  the  Seattle  plan  and  under  most  of  the
Controlled  Choice  years,  you  not  only had  the  plus or  minus
20 percent  or  25 percent,  if this  document  is correct,  but you
also had a caveat  for any single ethnic  group.  That  was  usually
meant  to  be  ethnic  minority  group.  And  so  if a  school  were
in  compliance  with  the  definition  of  plus  or  minus  [34]
25  percent  but  100  percent  of their  minorities  were  of one
ethnic  group,  then  they  still  would  be  out  of  compliance.
That’s  sort  of what  it meant.

So  it was  —  the  definitions  that  have  been  used  by the
courts  and by school  districts  are,  in my opinion,  sort  of racist.
And  the  reason  that  they  are  is because  you  sort  of make  it
minorities  lumped  together  versus  whites  lumped  together.
And  that  has  the  assumption  that  Alan  Sugiyama  and  myself
have  more  in common  than  Don  Nielsen  and  myself,  or  Don
Nielsen  and  Alan.

Q.  You  think  that  assumption  is problematic?

A.  I think  it’s  asinine,  it’s  not  just  problematic.

250a

Appendix  W

Q.  That assumption  appears  to underlie  the current  district

plan,  doesn’t  it?

A.  Well,  | don’t  know.

Q.  Does  the  current  district  plan make  any  differentiation

in assignment  among  various  minority  groups?

A.  Well,  let’s just say that the assumptions  of commonality
among  minorities  is still  prevalent  in this  country.  That’s  why
we  have  the  terminology  “minorities.”  And  the  similarities
between  the  Chinese  New  Year  and  the  Juneteenth  are  pretty
close,  [35]  right?

Q.  Why,  then,  doesn’t  the  district  track  and  attempt  to
accomplish  some  sort  of racial  balance  that considers  the various
ethnic  minorities  that  are  currently  considered  just minority?

A.  I don’t  know.

Q.  Are  those  numbers  —

A.  It’s  one  of things  that,  even  though  I’ve  been  harping
on  it periodically  for almost  20 years  now,  that  most  of the time
people  can’t  hear  it.  It’s  just  that  group  think  takes  you  nght
back  to minorities  and  whites.  And  even  in the Asian  category,
Chinese,  Japanese,  Korean,  Filipino,  Hawaiian,  Samoan,
although  they’re  called  Pacific  Islanders  now,  they  sort  of
Cambodian,  Vietnamese,
separated  out a little  bit,  but  Laotian,
all  Asian,  right?  Same  culture?  No.

That  speaks  for  itself,  for people  who  can  think.

goals  that  the  district  has  for the  high  school?

A.  No.

you  can  get  100  different  —  because  of probabilities,  the
law  of probabilities.

Q.  How  does  the  school  board  go  about  evaluating  the

quality  of the  education  available  at  its  high  schools?

A.  We have  a number  of different  quality  indicators  that

our  staff collect  for us.

Q.  What  are  those?

A.  Well,  | can’t  give  you  an  exhaustive  list,  but  I will
give  you,  as  I understand  it, some  of the  things  we  look  at,
which  include  test  scores  on  standardized  norm  referenced
tests,  test  scores  on  criterion  referenced  tests.

Q.  What’s  the  difference  between  those  two  kinds  of

tests?

A.  I'd  have  to  send  you  to  school  to  learn  that.

Q.  Can  you  give  me  a  quick  thumbnail  sketch?

262a

Appendix  X

A.  In  a  norm  referenced  standardized  test,  the  reason
we  call  it “norm  referenced”  is kids’  scores  are  forced  into  a
bell  curve,  so  that  there  are  always  winners  and  losers,  or
tails  of the  bell.

Q.  Much  like  my  law  school  exams.

A.  There  you go, you  would  ve familiar  with  [137]  those.

In  criterion  referenced  tests,  we  ask  kids  often  more
open-ended,  often  not  multiple  choice  questions  that  we  try
to  determine  what  they —  what  they  know  and  what  they’re
able  to do.  So  you  could  have  everyone  with  scores  that  are
passing.  Wouldn’t  that  be a  dream  in  law  school?

Q.  So  two  kinds  of test  scores.

A.  So  test  scores,  dropout  data,  attendance  data,
discipline  data,  GPA  average.  | think  our  administrators  use
seven  to  nine  different  indicators  when  they  look  at  schools.

Q.  Do  you  look  at  GPA  first  year  in  college?

A.  That’s  not  typically  something  that  we  look  at,  no,

but  it is available  in  published  —  typically  in  newspapers.

Q.  Is  that  something  parents  look  at,  to  your

understanding”?

A.  | don’t  imagine  that  that  particular  data  is  widely

enough  available  that  parents  would  usc  it.

Q.  So  is  there  a  certain  percentage  that  you  would
venture  to  say,  a range  of percentage  that  would  be diverse?

A.  Well,  diversity  is diversity.  | mean  diversity  captures
the presence  of multiple  categories.  The  meaning  of diversity
that  we  most  wanted  to  capture,  in  terms  of an  effective
learning  environment,  is one  in which  students  are  not  made
to  feel  peculiar  or  excluded  as  a  consequence  of  the
differences  that  they  represent.

1 think  in  a  prior  case,  it  may  very  well  have  been  in
Wessmann,  when  asked  about  what  kinds  of numbers  make
a difference,  | didn’t  see  a —  I did not  cite a particular  number
and  I do not  cite  particular  numbers.  | don’t  think  anyone  in
the  literature  cites  a specific  number  to capture  diversity,  but
rather  we  talked  about  the  quality  of the  environment  as  it
pertains  to  learning.  And | think  the  phrase  I use,  we  would
have  sufficient  numbers  so  as  to  avoid  students  feeling  any
kind  of specter  of exceptionality.  I think  the  thing that  makes
students  uncomfortable  in the  learning  environment  is when
there  is the  kind  of exceptionality  that’s  a distraction  to  their
learning.

[50]  Q.  So  for  instance,  in the  kind  of hypothetical  that
I posed  earlier  of the school  that was  20 percent  white,  would
you  —  would  you  say  that  anyone  in that  school  would  have
felt  that  exceptionality?

A.  There  is no  way  of knowing.  I mean  one  would  have
to  examine  and  explore  and  understand  how  that  context

277a

Appendix  Y

operates.  You  could  have  two  different  educational  facilities
identically  composed,  but  the  nature  and  quality  of  the
learning  experience  within  the  two  would  depend  on  how
the  educators  and  adults  in  those  two  contexts  crafted  the
educational  experience  so  as  to take advantage  of the diversity
that  was  present.  I mean  it’s  not  simply  a matter  of numbers.

*

*

*

[54]  *

*

*

A.  Well,  again,  when  you  —  when  you  look  at  the
tradition  in terms  of the desegregation  work  that’s  been  done,
you  go back  to the 50s  and  60s  and  the  work  in this  area,  and
you  see  that  traditionally,  the  courts  have  responded  to
schools  being  racially  identifiable  and  high  minority
concentration  when  they’re  in excess  of 50 percent  black  or
50  percent  Hispanic.

Now,  that  is a  tradition  not  of which  we  come,  in terms
of the racial  composition  of the school,  which  tends  to suggest
that  it will  not  benefit  from  the access  to quality  that  majority
schools,  majority  white  schools  typically  enjoy  in  terms  of
resource  allocation,  teacher  quality,  as  indicated  by several
different  measures,  and  so  on,  the  availability  of courses,
those  kind  of [55]  experiences,  when  schools  tend  to  exceed
the greater  than  50 percent  black,  not  necessarily  greater  than
50  percent  minority.

That  can  be misleading,  especially  when  you're  looking
at places  like  either  Seattle  or  San  Francisco.  There  you  have
a  situation  where  the  tradition  with  regard  to  specific  Asian

278a

Appendix  Y

populations,  as  well  as  whites,  you  have  a  context  that  still
tends  to work  like  most  majority  white  schools  operate  even
though  in some  schools  you  may  have  a majority  —  you  may
not  have  a  majority  of  —  a  S50  percent  plurality  of any
particular  category  of students  but you  may  have  schools  that
may  be 30 percent  Japanese  and  25  percent  white,  and  it still
has  that  majority  context  feel  to  it  or  performance
characteristic  to  it in terms  of its  benefit  from  the  resource
allocation  decisions  of the  school  board  and  so  on.

So you  would  —  you  would  be concerned  about  schools
that  get to  the  point  of being  80 percent  minority,  depending
on  the  composition  of that  minority,  but  | do think  you  have
to  look  at  the  composition  of  that  minority  in  terms  of
understanding  the  extent  to  which  that  school  is likely  to  be
beneficiary  of the  kinds  of educational  [56]  resources  and  to
be  —  and  to  have  —  to  be  in  possession  of the  indices  of
quality  that  we  would  expect  —  that  we  would  aspire  to our
schools  having  for  all  students.

Q.  So it sounds  like a lot of it depends  on  almost  a case-

by-case  analysis.  -

A.  Well,  | mean  because  of —  because  minority  is not  a
monolithic  thing,  one  doesn’t  benefit  from  simply  saying
minority,  because  a  place  that’s  80  percent  minority  and  the
place  next  door  is  80  percent  minority,  they  can  be  vastly
different  places  depending  on  the composition  of that  student
body  when  you  begin  to  talk  about  specific  race  ethnic
categories.

279a

Appendix  Y

Q.  And  so  they  can  have  vastly  different  effects  on  the

educational  experience  and  benefits?

A.  They can  have  different  educational  outcomes  for its

students,  yes.

[94]  *

*

*

Q.  (By Ms.  Loppnow)  We were  talking  right before  we
took  a  break  about  whether  or  not  there  were  studies  that
you  knew  of that  had  said  that  desegregation  didn’t  increase
or  had  a  negative  effect  on  test  scores.  And  you  said  it was
important  that  diversity  be  done  right  and  desegregation  be
done  right.

A.  That  desegregation  be done  well.

Q.  Can  you  explain  —

A.  That  we  be concerned  about  the  process.

Q.  Can  you  explain  what  you  mean  by that?

A.  Yes.  We  have  demonstrated  that  we  can  desegregate
—  early  on,  | said  that  early  on  the  research  indicated  that  it
was  —  some  of the  benefits  of desegregated  schooling  are
greater,  are  more  likely  to  be  achieved  when  you  take  into
consideration  the socioeconomic  mix  of the  school,  also,  that
mixing  poor  minority  with  more  white  children  did  not
necessarily  increase  the  likelihood  that  the  school  was  going

280a

Appendix  Y¥

to  secure  better  access  to  [95]  resources,  better  access  to
better  teachers  and  so  on,  so  that  it is important  to  take  into
consideration  the  economic  mix  of  the  school  as  you
desegregate.

Secondly,  when  we  desegregate  we  also  have  to  be
concerned  that  we  are  not  satisfied  simply  to  desegregate
the building.  There  are  resegregation  processes  that take place
such  that  within  a  school  you  could  still  get  racially
identifiable  classes  such  that  your  less  prestigious  courses
are  still  predominantly  minority  and  your  more  highly
prestigious,  highly-valued  courses  remain  predominantly
white  and/or  Japanese  or  Chinese,  depending  on  the  school
district  that  you’re  in.  So  you  do  have  to  watch  for
resegregation  factors.

You  also  have  to prepare  the receiving  schools.  Actually,
you  prepare  the adults  in the educational  setting  to engage  in
the  process  of desegregation.  Some  of the  research  clearly
shows  that professional  development  in the form  of equipping
teachers  with  ways  to  become  more  multi-culturally
competent  are  important.  As a matter  of fact,  one  of the  local
not  local,  but  one  of your  resident  scholars  there  in  the
state  of Washington  is James  Spikes,  who  is internationally
[96] known  for his research  on  multi-cultural  educations  and
the  preparation  of teachers  for cross-cultural  competency.

So  those  are  important  things  to  do  such  that  teachers
are  given  assistance  with  preparing  themselves  to  be able  to
engage  in much  more  diverse  client  population,  particularly
as  the  schools  become  even  more  diverse  or  as  the  school
district  is about  to  undergo  desegregation.

28la

Appendix  Y

So at least  those  things  are  things  that  | think  need  to be

taken  into  consideration  in terms  of doing  it well.

Q.  Some  of  the  studies  that  you  provided  talk  about
creating  a learning  environment  where  there  is status  leveling.

A.  Yes.

Q.  Can  you  explain  what  that  1s?

A.  Well,  the status  leveling  isn’t  intended  to suggest  that
we  want  to  bring  anyone  else’s  status  down,  but  what  the
research  really  talks  about  is  preparing  the  adults  in  the
context,  the  authorities  in  the  context  that  you're
desegregating  to  treat  all  of the  students  with  equal  status
and  to  behave  in a way  that  communicates  that  they endorse
and  approve  the  desegregation  or  the  increasing  diversity  of
the  [97]  school.  Those  are  important  things  to  do.

Students  like  young  children  are  very  sensitive  to  adult
approval.  And  when  the  adults  1n the situation  act  in ways  to
suggest  either  that  they  favor  one  group  of  students  over
another  or  that  they  do  not  support  the  impending  or  the
growing  amount  of diversity  or  desegregation,  then  it delivers
a  message  that  suggests  that  they  are  not  likely  to  treat
students  fairly.

Recent  research,  a study  that  was  published,  | forget  the
authors  of the  study,  “What  Matters  Most  in Teaching,”  the
report  indicated  that  one  of the  most  important  attributes  of
classroom  treatment  is that  students  be —  students  perceive
themselves  as  being  treated  fairly  and  evenhandedly,  that  that
has  to  be  a  characteristic  of  a good  learning  environment.

282a

Appendix  Y

Some  of the  evidence  on  the  contrary  to  that  is that  we
see  that sometimes,  when  we  look  at the reasons  why students
are  suspended  or  disciplined,  that  we  —  we  see  two  things.
We see  action  taken  by race  and ethnicity,  and we  occasionally
see  that  the  reasons  why —  we  have  seen  in the  research  the
reasons  why  students  of color  or  minority  students,  black
and  Latino,  are  disciplined  [98]  are  sometimes  for  more
subjective  reasons,  and  that  the  penalties  tend  to  be  more
harsh  for black  and  Latino  students  compared  to  their  white
fellow  students.

Those  are  conditions  that  are  unfair  and  do  not  suggest

that  the  students  are  perceived  as  having  equal  status.

Q.  So  it’s  —  it’s  not  enough  to  get  the  —  some  of the
benefits  that  you’ve  listed  in  your  report  and  talked  about
here  just to  put  all  the  students  in one  school  together,  you
have  to  make  these  preparations  with  teachers  and  the
learning  environment,  is that  right?

A.  If you  —  yes.  It’s  not  simply  sufficient  to  rearrange
the  furniture,  you  have  to,  in fact,  prepare  people  and  treat  it
in a way  that  gives  you  the  greatest  likelihood  of reaping  the
rewards  that  can  come  from  having  a  richly  diverse  student
population  in the  school  or  in a  classroom.

293a

APPENDIX  BB  —  DECLARATION  OF
DAVID  J. ARMOR,  PH.D.
DATED  MARCH  §, 2001*

IN  THE  UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT
FOR  THE  WESTERN  DISTRICT  OF  WASHINGTON
AT  SEATTLE

No.  C00-1205R

PARENTS  INVOLVED  IN  COMMUNITY  SCHOOLS,
a  Washington  nonprofit  corporation,

Plaintiff,

VS.

SEATTLE  SCHOOL  DISTRICT  NO.  1, a political
subdivision  of the  State  of Washington;  JOSEPH
OLCHEFSKE,  in his  official  capacity  as  superintendent;
BARBARA  SCHAAD-LAMPHERE,  in  her  official
capacity  as  President  of the  Board  of Directors  of Seattle
Public  Schools;  and  DONALD  NIELSON,  in  his  official
capacity  as  Vice  President  of the  Board  of Directors  of
Seattle  Public  Schools;  STEVEN  BROWN,  JAN
KUMASAKA;  MICHAEL  PRESTON,  BARBARA
PETERSON;  and  NANCY  WALDMAN,  in their  official
capacities  as  members  of the  Board  of Directors,

Defendants.

DECLARATION  OF  DAVID  J. ARMOR,  Ph.D.

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Attachment  11  to  Korrell  Declaration  filed  3/5/01.

294a

Appendix  BB

1, David  J. Armor,  declare:

| am  a Research  Professor  at George  Mason  University’s
School  of Public  Policy.  A true  and  correct  copy  of my
curriculum  vitae  is  attached  as  Exhibit  1.  As  that
document  indicates,  | have  conducted  research  and
authored  numerous  works  on  issues  involving  race,
education,  school  choice,  and desegregation.  I have  served
as  an  expert  on  such  subjects  in a number  of court  cases.

Parents  Involved  in  Community  Schools  has  asked  me
to  rebut  the  report  of Dr.  William  T. Trent  submitted  by
the  Seattle  School  District  in this  case.

The  Trent  report  aims  to  describe  the  benefits  of
attending  desegregated  or  racially  diverse  schools.  |
understand  that  one  reason  for  this  expert  study  is that
the  Seattle  School  Board  has  a school  assignment  policy
that  mandates  some  decisions  be  made  on  the  basis  of
race.  Since  consideration  of  race  by  a  governmental
agency  is  constrained  by  the  U.S.  Constitution,  the
District  Court  must  evaluate  whether  the  benefits  of
desegregation  or  racial  diversity  are  sufficient  to  meet
constitutional  requirements  as  established  by  the  U.S.
Supreme  Court.  Thus  the  nature,  the  consistency,  and
the  degree  of benefits  may  be  critical  in  deciding  this
issue.  That  is,  if the  benefits  prove  to  be  irrelevant  to
the  governmental  agency’s  mission,  or  sporadic  and
inconsistent,  or  weak  and  uncertain,  then  such  results
may  have  bearing  on  the  Court's  decision.

295a

Appendix  BB

One  of the  benefits  of desegregation  Dr.  Trent  describes
is  “opportunity  and  achievement.”  He  concludes,  and
cites  various  studies  in  support,  that  attending  a
desegregated  public  school  is more  likely to lead  to more
desegregation  experiences  later  in life  —  at  colleges,  in
employment,  and  in  residential  choices.  Even  if the
studies  he cites  showed  significant  and consistent  effects
in this  regard,  it is a tautological  benefit:  it is tantamount
to saying  that  “racial  diversity  promotes  racial  diversity”
or  “desegregation  promotes  desegregation.”

Dr.  Trent  describes  a second  benefit  of diversity  as  being
learning  and  “achievement  outcomes.”  He  cites  expert
studies  that  he  has  done  in  other  cases  to  support  his
conclusion  of  “better  academic  achievement...for
[minority]  students  in those  schools  that  have  a greater
proportion  of non-minority  students.”  In those  cases  with
which  I am  familiar  (Prince  Georges  County,  Charlotte-
Mecklenburg,  and  Kansas  City),  it  is  my  opinion  that
his  studies  did  not  establish  a  positive  relationship
between  the  percent  non-minority  students  in  a  school
and  significantly  higher  achievement.  In fact,  I did  both
original  and  rebuttal  studies  in these  cases  that  showed
virtually  no  relationship  between  academic  achievement
(as measured  by test  scores)  and the percent  non-minority
in a  school.

Another  benefit  Dr.  Trent  identifies  is improved  teaching
and  learning,  a  conclusion  that  is supposedly  supported
by a survey  of 11" grade students  in Louisville,  Kentucky
(Kurlaender  and  Yun,  2000).  This  study  consists  entirely
of self-reports  by students  as  to  various  experiences  in

296a

Appendix  BB

school.  There  are  no  objective  measures  of benefits  (such
test  scores,  course  grades,  etc.)  in this  study,  and  there
is  no  analysis  whatsoever  that  compares  educational
benefits  (self-reported  or  otherwise)  for  students  in
schools  or  classrooms  with  differing  amounts  of racial
diversity.  Thus  the  study  offers  no  evidence  about  the
effects  of desegregation  or racial  diversity  on  educational
benefits.

The  final  benefit  of desegregation  cited  by Dr.  Trent  is
“civic  values,”  which  includes  improved  race  relations,
reductions  in  racial  prejudice,  and  improved  attitudes
toward  inclusiveness.  He  cites  a  number  of studies  in
support  of this  conclusion,  not  all  of which  | have  been
able  to  review.  Those  | have  reviewed,  however,  do not
support  a  conclusion  of  significant  and  consistent
benefits  arising  from  desegregation  and  racial  diversity.
I present  several  quotations  from  two  of  the  more
important  references  cited:  Slavin  (1995)  and  a
comprehensive  literature  review  by Schofield  (1995).

a.

Slavin  says,  “Desegregation  must  be  seen  as  an
opportunity  for improvement  of intergroup  relations,
not  as  a  solution  itself.”  Also,  “In  traditionally
organized  schools,  desegregation  rarely  fulfills  the
conditions  outlined  by  Alport”  for  improved  race
relations.  In  other  words,  desegregation  or  racial
diversity  by themselves  do not  benefit  race  relations.
Benefits  are  contingent  on  other  changes  being made
in  a  school  program,  such  as  having  a  special
curriculum  designed  by Slavin  called  “cooperative
learning.”

297a

Appendix  BB

b.  The  Schofield  study  is  a comprehensive  review  of
research  on  the  impact  of desegregation  on  many
possible  benefits  including  academic  achievement,
postsecondary  education,  self-esteem,  and  race
relations.  In  most  of these  areas,  she  concludes  the
evidence  of  benefits  is  weak,  inconsistent,  or
conditional.  (1) “.  .
.  not  all instances  of desegregation
will  lead to academic  gains.” (2) “the academic  ‘effects
of desegregation  are  almost  completely  restricted  to
the early primary  grades.’”  (3) “although  desegregation
has  no  measurable  impact  on  college  attainment  of
Southern  blacks,  it appears  to have  a positive,  though
weak  effect  for  those  living  in  the  North.”  (4)  “The
major  reviews  of school  desegregation  and  African
American  self-concept  or  self-esteem  generally
conclude  that desegregation  has no  clear-cut  consistent
impact.”  (5)  “Thus,  the  evidence  taken  as  a  whole
suggests  that  desegregation  has  no  clearly  predictable
impact  on  student  intergroup  attitudes.”

Dr.  Trent  has  not  based  his  conclusions  on  any  review
or  analysis  of data  pertaining  directly  to benefits  of racial
diversity  in Seattle  schools  for  Seattle  students.

In  authoring  my  rebuttal  of  Dr.  Trent’s  report  and  in
making  this  declaration,  | am  relying  on  my  own  expert
studies  done  in other  school  desegregation  studies,  the
studies  cited  above  which  were  included  in  Dr.  Trent’s
citations,  other  research  studies  in  the  field  of
desegregation  benefits,  including  my  book  Forced
Justice:  School  Desegregation  and the Law,  which  cites
many  of these  other  studies.

298a

Appendix  BB

| declare  under  penalty  of perjury  under  the  laws  of the

State  of Washington  that  the  foregoing  is true  and  correct.

Dated  this  Sth  day of March,  2001.

s/  David  J. Armor,  Ph.D.
David  J. Armor,  Ph.D.

299a

APPENDIX  CC —  DEFENDANT  SCHOOL  DISTRICT’S
FIRST REQUEST  FOR PRODUCTION AND  PLAINTIFF’S
RESPONSES  THERETO  (EXCERPTS)*

INTERROGATORY  NO.  1:  Please  identify:

(a)  all  PICS  members

(b)  the school-aged  children  (K-12)  of all  PICS  members

(c)  all PICS  members  and  their  children  who  claim  that,
for  the  2000-2001  school  year,  their  child  or  children  were
assigned  to  a school,  other  than  their  first  choice  school,  by
reason  of their  race.

ANSWER:

Objection.  This  request  is overbroad  and  not  calculated
to  lead  to  the  discovery  of  admissible  evidence.  It  seeks
information  beyond  the  scope  of permissible  discovery  and
protected  by the  free  speech  and  free  association  provisions
of  the  United  States  and  Washington  State  constitutions.
Complete  information  about  which  children  were  denied
admission  to  which  schools  as  a consequence  of defendants’
race-balancing  policies  is  in  defendants’  possession.  The
identity  of all  PICS  members  is not  germane  to  any  issue  in
this case.  The  central  question  is whether  defendants  illegally
use  race  as  a  factor  in  deciding  which  students  to  admit  to
particular  schools,  and  defendants  have  raised  a  question
about  PICS’  standing  to  bring  this  suit.  All  that  is necessary

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Attachment  33  to  Korrell  Declaration  filed  2/12/01.

300a

Appendix  CC

for this action  to proceed is that one  PICS  member  have standing
to  bring  this  action,  and  without  waiving  these  specific  or  any
other  general  objection  articulated  above,  PICS  provides  the
following  information:

Jill  Kurfirst  is  a  PICS  member;  her  son  is Andy  Meeks.
She  and  Andy  timely  applied  for  his  admission,  for  the  2000-
2001  school  year,  to  Ballard  High  School  as  their  first  choice,
Nathan  Hale  High School  as their second  choice,  and Roosevelt
High  School  as  their  third  choice.  Because  of Andy’s  race,  he
was  denied  admission  to those  schools  and assigned  to Ingraham
High  School.

Winnie  Bachwitz  is a PICS  member.  She  and  her daughter
timely  applied  for admission,  for  the  2000-20001  school  year,
to  Ballard  High  School  as  their  first  choice,  Roosevelt  High
School  as  their second  choice,  and Nathan  Hale  High School  as
their third choice.  Because  of her daughter’s  race,  she was  denied
admission  to  those  schools  and  assigned  to  Ingraham  High
School.

Rick  and  Barbara  Hack  are  PICS  members;  their  children
are  Joseph  (entering  the  seventh  grade),  Daniel  (entering  the
sixth  grade),  and  Heidi  (entering  the  fourth  grade).  Because  of
the race  of their adopted  son  Joseph  (and the consideration  given
to  siblings  of a current  student  under  the  current  policies),  all
three  children  will  likely  be  able  to  attend  Eckstein  Middle
School  and  Roosevelt  High School,  the schools  they prefer.  The
defendants’  policy  of using  race  in  admissions  decisions  and
their  goal  of  racial  balancing  has  affected  them  and  the
educational  opportunities  of  their  children  by changing  the
neighborhood-nature  of their  public  schools,  by  breaking  up

30la

Appendix  CC

the  community  that  has  developed  among  the  students  and
parents  in the schools,  and by making  it more  difficult  for them
to meet,  know, and participate  in the education  of their children
with  the parents  of other  students  in the school.

John  and  June  Miller  are  PICS  members;  their  son  is Rip.
Rip  is a  seventh  grade  student  who  will  be  applying  for  high
school  for the 2002-2003  school  year.  They expect  to apply for
his  admission  to  Ballard  High  School  as  their  first  choice.
Because  of defendants’  race  balancing policy, they are  concerned
that  he will  be  denied  admission  to  Ballard  High  School  and
assigned  to another  school  because  of his race.

In addition  to  the  immediate  and  obvious  harm  to  those
parents  identified  herein,  every  parent  with  a child  in the Seattle
public  high  schools  who  desires  to  attend  one  of the  popular
high schools,  including  PICS  members  not specifically  identified
herein,  has  been  or  will  be  harmed  by the  defendants’  race
balancing  policy.  Whether  or not  the district’s  illegal use  of race
resulted  in the denial  of a seat  to a particular  student  seeking  to
attend  a  particular  school,  all  students  and  their  parents  who
applied  to attend  one  of the  popular  schools  have  been  forced
to  compete  for  seats  in  those  schools  in  a  system  that
discriminates  on  the  basis  of race.

DATED  this  __  day of July,  2000.

BENNETT  BIGELOW  & LEEDOM,  P.S.

ORAL  EX AMINATION  OF JOHN  VACCHIERY
DATED  JANUARY  25, 2001*

IN  THE  UNITED  STATES  DISTRICT  COURT  FOR
THE  WESTERN  DISTRICT  OF  WASHINGTON
AT  SEATTLE

NO.  C00-1205  P

PARENTS  INVOLVED  IN  COMMUNITY  SCHOOLS,
a Washington  nonprofit  corporation,

Plaintiff,

vs.

SEATTLE  SCHOOL  DISTRICT  NO.  1,
a  political  subdivision  of the  State  of Washington,  et  al.,

Defendants.

DEPOSITION  UPON  ORAL  EXAMINATION
OF
JOHN  VACCHIERY

9:10  a.m.
January  25,  2001
1501  Fourth  Avenue,  Suite  2700
Seattle,  Washington

*  This  document  was  located  in  the  district  court  record  as

Attachment  &  to  Korrell  Declaration  filed  3/8/01.

303a

Appendix  DD

[Commencing  at  page  57]

*

*

*

Q.  How  is  race  determined  for  purposes  of the  racial

tiebreaker?

A.  At the time of application  to enrollment  in the district,
parents  are  asked  to indicate  one  of the  five ethnic  —  federal
ethnic  categories.

Q.  Does  the  district  have  any  criteria  for [58] assessing
whether  or  not  the  person  is the  race  that  they  self-identify
as?

A.  The  people  are  asked  to self-identify,  and  we  do not

do  anything  to  verify  that.

|

Q.  What  happens  if a  person  identifies  more  than  one

race?

A.  The application  will  not be accepted  and, if necessary,
the  enrollment  service  person  taking  the  application  will
indicate  one  box.  In other  words,  you  can’t  have  more  than
—  you  can’t  have  multiple  ethnic  categories.

Q.  How  does  the  enrollment  service  person  make  that

decision?

A.  They  have  to  use  their  best  judgment  at  the  time.
They  normally  don’t  —  they'll  do  everything  they  can  to
make  sure  that  the  people  that  are  in  front  of them  putting
this  application  in  self-identify.

304a

Appendix  DD

Q.  Do  you  have  any  criteria  for what  they should  do  in

that  situation?

A.  Not  that  I'm  aware  of,  other  than  to  use  their  best

judgment.

Q.  And  by “their  best judgment,”  what  do  you  mean?

A.  That’s  up to  the  individual.  They  have  to  [59]  assess
the  situation  at  the  time  and  make  the  best  determination
they  can.  Again,  that’s  not  something  that  they’re  going  to
do easily.  They will  do everything  they can  to assure  that  the
person  has  the  opportunity  to  self-identify.

Q.  What  does  the  —  what  should  that  person  do  if

someone  refuses  to  mark  a race?

A.  They  will  go  ahead  and  do  it.

Q.  And  how  do they  do  that?

A.  Again,  they use  their  best judgment  to what  category

or  what  racial  category  a  person  should  be  in.

Q.  And  is that  by looking  at  the  person  or  —

A.  That  is  basically  they  have  —  only  with  the
information  they  have  in  front  of them,  which  is the  person
and  hopefully  a  student  that  is  there  in  person  with  the
enrollment  service  person  at  the  time  of the  application.

30Sa

Appendix  DD

Q.  Are  you  aware  of any  problems  with  people  lying

about  their  race?

A.  I’m  not  —  specifically  when  you  say  “lying  about
their  race,”  misidentifying?  Since  the  process  is  that  we
accept  their definition  of their racial  category,  that’s  what  —
it is what  it is.  They  can  —  you  know,  they’re  allowed  to
choose  their  own  [60]  ethnic  category  at  the  time  of
application,  self-identify.

So  if they  self-identify  something  other  than  what  their

race  is, yeah,  that  1s possible.

Q.  But the district  doesn’t  make  any efforts  to determine

if anyone  has  done  that,  is that  correct?

A.  In  the  category  of racial  balance,  no.  Again,  the
parent,  in conjunction  with  their  student,  is allowed  to  self-
identify.

Q.  Can  people  currently  change  their  race?

A.  No.

Q.  Did  they  used  to  be  able  to  change  their  race  every

year?

A.  There  have  been  policies  in  the  past  that  allowed

multiple  —  one  or  more  changes  of race,  that  is correct.

Q. And  when  did  that  policy  change?

306a

Appendix  DD

A.  The  policy  changed  with  the  implementation  of the

current  student  assignment  plan.

Q. Do  you  know  why that  policy  changed?

A.  It was  based  on  the  assumption  that  race  does  not

change.

[139}*

*

*

Q.  Was  there  a time  when  the  Urban  League  presented

the  school  district  with  a  plan  last  fall?

A.  The  Urban  League  did present  a report  to the school

board,  yes.

Q.  And  was  that  plan  considered  by the  school  board?

A.  That,  to my knowledge,  that  specific  plan [140]  was
not  considered.  Some  of the  elements  of the  plan  were  and
in the  other  proposals  before  the  board.

Q.  Were  you  ever  asked  to  look  at  that  plan and  do any

impact  studies  under  that  plan?

A.  Not  on  that  specific  plan,  no.

*

*

*

307a

Appendix  DD

{[143]}*

*

*

Q.  (By Ms.  Loppnow)  During  the time  when  the school
district  was  making  the  change  from  Open  Choice  one  to
Open Choice  two,  we’ve  talked  about  a number  of plans that
you  remeniber  the  board  considering.

A.  Uh-huh.

Q.  Do  you  ever  recall  the  board  considering  any  race

ne

| plans?

MR.  MADDEN:  Object  to  the  form  of the  question.

You  can  answer.

A.  No.

QUESTIONS  PRESENTED

(1)  How  are  the  Equal  Protection  rights  of public  high
school  students  affected  by the  jurisprudence  of Grutter  v.
Bollinger,  539  U.S.  306  (2003),  and  Gratz  v.  Bollinger,  539
U.S.  244  (2003)?

(2)  Is  racial  diversity  a  compelling  interest  that  can
justify  the  use  of race  in selecting  students  for admission  to
public high schools?

(3)  May a school  district  that  is not  racially  segregated
and  that  normally  permits  a student  to attend  any high school
of her  choosing  deny  a child  admission  to  her chosen  school
solely  because  of her  race  in an  effort  to  achieve  a  desired
_  racial  balance  between  whites  and  nonwhites  in  particular
schools,  or  does  such  racial  balancing  violate  the  Equal
Protection  Clause  of the Fourteenth  Amendment?

ii

PARTIES  TO  THE  PROCEEDINGS

Parents

The  petitioner,

in  Community
Seattle
Schools,  is  a  Washington  nonprofit  corporation.
School  District  No.  1, one  of  the  defendants  below,  is  a
political  subdivision  of the State  of Washington.

Involved

In  addition  to  the  parties  listed  in  the  caption,  the
following  individuals  were  named  as  defendants  in  all  the
proceedings  below:
Joseph  Olchefske,  in  his  official
capacity  as  Superintendent;  Barbara  Schaad-Lamphere,  in
her official  capacity  as  President  of the Board  of Directors  of
Seattle  Public  Schools;  Donald  Neilson,  in  his  official
capacity  as  Vice  President  of  the  Board  of  Directors  of
Seattle  Public  Schools;  and  Steven  Brown;  Jan  Kumasaka;
Michael  Preston;  and  Nancy  Waldman,  in  their  official
capacities  as  members  of the Board  of Directors.

On  February  6,  2006,  pursuant  to  S.  Ct.  R. 35.3,
Petitioner  requested  the  substitution  of subsequently  elected
school  board  members  as  follows:

Respondent  Joseph  Olchefske,  the  Superintendent  of
Schools,  has been  replaced  as  Superintendent  by Raj Manhas.
Respondent  Barbara  Schaad-Lamphere,  President  of  the
Board  of  Directors  of  Seattle  Public  Schools,  has  been
Respondent
replaced  as  President  by  Brita  Butler-Wall.
Donald  Neilson,  Vice-President  of the  Board  of Directors  of
Seattle  Public  Schools,  has  been  replaced  as  Vice  President
Respondents,  Steven  Brown,  Jan
by  Cheryl  Chow.
Kumasaka,  Michael  Preston,  Barbara  Peterson  and  Nancy
Waldman,  members  of the  Board  of Directors  of the  Seattle
Public  Schools,  have  been  replaced  as  members  of the  Board
of  Directors  by  Sally  Soriano,  Darlene  Flynn,  Michael
DeBell,  Mary  Bass,  and  Irene  Stewart.

PETITIONER’S  BRIEF

The  petitioner,  Parents  Involved  in Community  Schools
(“Parents”),  respectfully  requests  the  Court  to  reverse  the
court  of appeals  and  hold  that  the  Seattle  School  District’s
race-based  student  admission  plan is unconstitutional.

OPINIONS  BELOW

The  initial  opinion  herein  was  that  of the  district  court,
reported  at  137  F.  Supp.  2d  1224  (W.D.  Wash.  2001)  and
submitted  in the  appendix  to  Parents’  petition  (“Pet.  App.”)
at  269-303.  On  appeal  to  the  court  of appeals  for the Ninth
Circuit,  the  three-judge  panel  rendered  opinions  reported  at
285  F.3d  1236  (9th Cir.  2002),  addressing  Parents’  state  law
claims.
Those  opinions  were  withdrawn,  294  F.3d  1084
(9th Cir.  2002),  and  state  law  issues  were  certified,  294  F.3d
1085  (9th  Cir.  2002),  to  the  Washington  Supreme  Court,
whose  opinions  are  reported  at  72  P.3d  151,  149  Wash.2d
The  subsequent  opinions  of  the  three-judge
660  (2003).
panel  of the  court  of appeals  deciding  Parents’  federal  law
claims  are  reported  at  377  F.3d  949  (9thCir.  2004)  and
submitted  at Pet.  App.  129-268.  The court  of appeals  granted
rehearing  en  banc,  395  F.3d  1168  (2005),  and  its  opinions,
issued  on  October  20,  2005,  are  reported  at  426  F.3d  1162
(9th Cir.  2005)  and  submitted  at Pet.  App.  1-128.

JURISDICTION

The  judgment  of the  court  of appeals  was  entered  on
October  20,  2005.  A  timely  petition  for  a  writ  of certiorari
was  filed  on  January  18, 2006,  and  granted  on  June  5, 2006.
This  Court  has jurisdiction  under  28 U.S.C.  § 1254(1).

2

CONSTITUTIONAL  AND  STATUTORY  PROVISIONS
INVOLVED

1.

The  Equal  Protection  Clause  of  the  Fourteenth
Amendment  to  the  United  States  Constitution  provides  in
relevant  part:  “No  State  shall  ...
deny to  any  person  within
its jurisdiction  the equal protection  of the laws.”  U.S.  Const.,
amend.  XIV,  § 1.

2.

42 U.S.C.  § 1983  provides  in relevant  part:

Every  person  who,  under  color  of  any
statute,  ordinance,  regulation,  custom,  or
usage,  of any  State  ...  subjects,  or  causes
to  be  subjected,  any  citizen  of the  United
States  ...  to  the  deprivation  of any  rights,
privileges,  or  immunities  secured  by  the
Constitution  and  laws,  shall  be  liable  to
the party  injured  in an  action  at law,  suit in
equity,  or  other  proper  proceeding  for
redress.

Title  VI  of the  Civil  Rights  Act,  42  U.S.C.

3.
§ 2000d,  provides in  relevant  part:

No  person  in  the  United  States  shall,  on
the  ground  of  race,  color,  or  national
origin,  be  excluded  from  participation  in,
be  denied  the  benefits  of, or  be  subjected
to  discrimination  under  any  program  or

assistance.

STATEMENT  OF THE  CASE

I,

The  District  Denies  Children  Admission  to  Their
Chosen  Schools  Because  of Their  Race.

Wishing  to create  a predetermined  racial  balance  in its
better  and  more  popular  high schools,  Seattle  School  District
No.  1 (“the  District’”)  uses  a  race-based  student  admissions
plan.  Although  children  may  choose  to  attend  any  of ten
high  schools  in  the  city  regardless  of where  they  live,  five
The  District
schools  have  more  applicants  than  openings.
uses  a racial  classification  to determine  who  will  be admitted
to “oversubscribed”  schools  that  it believes  are  insufficiently
racially  balanced,  admitting  students  who  will  move  such  a
school  toward  the  District’s  desired  racial  balance  in
preference  to  students  whose  admission  would  not.
Joint
Appendix  (“JA”)  38-42.

For  the  2000-01  school  year,  the  District  denied  over
300  students  admission  to  their  chosen  schools  solely
because  of race.
Eighty-nine  students  were  initially  denied
admission  to  their  first  choice  school  because  they  were  not
white,  and  216  were  denied  admission  to  any  of  their
preferred  schools  because  they  were  white.  These  numbers
were  reduced  slightly  by  adjustments  made  before  school
started,  but  many  students,  because  of  race,  were  denied
admission  to  schools  they  had  selected.  JA  39-41,  162-63,
188.  About  30 of these  students  left  the  District  rather  then
attend  the schools  to which  they were  assigned.  JA  163.

Il.

The  Schools  Vary  Widely  in  Quality  and  Program
Offerings.

It
The  District  operates  ten  regular  high  schools.
assigns  students  pursuant  to an  “Open  Choice”  plan  whereby
students  can  elect  to  attend  any  school  in  the  District
regardless  of  where  they  live,  provided  there  is  space
Excerpts  of  Record,  Cir.  Ct.  Docket  at  7/24/01
available.

(“ER”)  580.

The  schools  vary  widely  in quality,  program  offerings,
and  popularity.  This  variation  is confirmed  by objective  data
as  well  as  testimony  from  parents  and school  board  members.
For the 2000-01  school  year,  82%  of all students  selected  one
of five  high  schools  as  their  first  choice:  Ballard,  Franklin,
Garfield,  Hale,  or  Roosevelt.  The  other  five  schools  (Chief
Sealth,  Cleveland,  Ingraham,  Rainier  Beach,  and  West
Seattle)  were  selected  by 18%.  JA  37-38.

Families  and  the  school  board  use  various  objective
scores  on  standardized
criteria  to  evaluate  school  quality:
and  Advanced
tests,  numbers  of  college  preparatory
Placement  (“AP”)  courses  offered,  percentage  of  students
who  take  AP  courses  and  SAT  tests,  percentage  of graduates
who  attend  college,  Seattle  Times  college-preparedness
and
rankings,
University
disciplinary  statistics.
The
oversubscribed  schools  score  better  than  the  others on these
measures  of quality.  JA  147,  151,  243-46,  ER  514,  556-58."
The  schools  also  vary  in the  special  academic  programs  they
offer.  For  example,  Ballard  offers  a  Biotech  Academy,  with
separate  admissions  prerequisites,  and  Hale  offers  a  “Ninth
Grade  Academy”  and  an  “Integrated  Studies”  program.  JA
145-46,  180-81.

of  Washington
rankings,
JA  142,  157,  185,  261-65.

Owing  to  these  differences,  five of the ten  high schools
are  oversubscribed.
Three  of  these  (Ballard,  Hale,  and
Roosevelt)  are  located  north  of  downtown,  where  the
Two
majority  of  District  students  are  white.  JA  161.

'  Comparisons  were  submitted  tc the District  Court,  ER  126, 228, 261,
435-37,  based  on  data at ER  108-25,  182-220,  226-27,  256-608.  That
court  agreed  that  the oversubscribed  schools  were  more  prestigious,
“competition  for assignment  to those  schools  is keen,”  and “students
denied  their choice  of schools  are  deprived  of curriculum  advantages  not
necessarily  available  at other  schools.”  Pet.  App.  271a,  283a  n.7.

5

oversubscribed  schools  (Franklin  and  Garfield)  are  located
south  of  downtown,  where  the  majority  of  students  are
nonwhite.  JA  161.
Of  the  five  undersubscribed  schools,
three  are  located  south  of  downtown  (Cleveland,  Rainier
Beach,  and  Chief Sealth),  one  is north  (Ingraham),  and  one  is
west  (West  Seattle).  JA 310.

Some

schools

of  the

oversubscribed

a
predominantly  white  student  population  and  some  have  a
predominantly  nonwhite  population.  At  Ballard,  Hale,  and
Roosevelt,  white  students  compose  between  54%  and  62%  of
the  population.  Garfield  is 53%  nonwhite.  Franklin  is also
predominantly  nonwhite,  approximately  80%.  JA 284-87.  7

have

Ill.  The  District’s  Assignment  Plan.

The District  adopted  the current  high school  assignment
plan in  1997  for implementation  in the  1998-99  school  year.
Under  this  plan,  students  may  select  any  school  in  the
district.  They  are  assigned,  where  possible,  to  the  school
they  list  as  their  first  choice.  If a  student  is not  admitted  to
her  first-choice  school  because  it is full,  the  District  tries  to
assign  her  to  her  second-choice  school,  and  so  on.
If a
student  is not  admitted  to any of her chosen  schools,  she will
receive  a  mandatory  assignment  to  a  school  with  available
space.  Once  a  student  is admitted  to  a  school,  she  need  not
reapply  in subsequent  years.  JA  163.

If a high school  has  fewer  openings  than  the number  of
students  who  select  it as  a first  choice,  the District  uses  a set
of preferences  —  what  it calls  “tiebreakers”  —  to  determine
who  may  attend.  JA  91-92,  101.  The  first preference  favors
students  with  a  sibling  already  attending  a  school  and
accounts  for about  10%  of assignments.  JA  189-90.

? Of the less popular  schools,  Cleveland  and  Rainier  Beach  have  white
populations  of 10%  and  8%; the other  three  are  “balanced”  by the
District’s  definition.  ER 622.

6

The  next  tiebreaker  gives  preference  to  race.

JA  38,
101.
The  racial  balance  of  students  enrolled  in  all  of
Seattle’s  public  schools  is  about  40%  white  and  60%
nonwhite,  JA  379,  and  the  schoo]  board  has  decided  that  the
student  body  at  each  of  the  popular  high  schools  should
reflect  that  balance.  JA  214-15.
If the  population  of an
oversubscribed  school  deviates  by more  than  a set  number  of
percentage  points  from  the  desired  ratio,  then  a  student
whose  race  will  move  a  school  closer  to  the  desired  racial
balance  will  be  admitted  in  preference  to  students  of  a
different  race.  JA 38; ER  134,  583.

For  purpuses  of this  preference,  a student  is deemed  to
be  of the  race  specified  in her  registration  materials.  When
initially  registering  for Seattle’s  public  schools,  parents  must
identify  the race  of each  child  by using codes  on  a form.  ER
If a  parent  declines  to  specify  a  race,  the  child  is
170-71.
assigned  a race  by the  District,  based  on  a  visual  inspection
of the parent  or the student.  JA  194; ER 623.

From  1998  to  2001,  the  race  preference  was  triggered
by a deviation  of 10 percentage  points  from  the  40:60  ratio.
During  that  period,  if the  race  preference  was  triggered  with
respect  to  a  school,  it  determined  all  admissions  of  new
students  to that  school  in that  year.  (After  this  suit  was  filed
the  permissible  deviation  from  the  40:60  ratio  was  expanded
Infra
and  the  operation  of  the  preference  was  modified.
p.  10.)  Once  all  applicants  in  the  preferred  racial  category
are  admitted,  any  remaining  seats  are  allocated  based  on
distance  from  the  school.  A  final  tiebreaker,  a  lottery,  is
rarely  used, because  distance  is calculated  to  1/100  of a mile.
ER  134, 621.

* Until  1997,  a student  could  change  her racial  category  for admissions
purposes  once  a year.  ER 262-65  (correspondence  from  parents
complaining  about  the new  prohibition  on  changing  races).  The District
appears  to again allow  students  to change  racial  categories.

IV.  Denying  Children  Admission  to  Their  Chosen

Schools  Because  of Race  Harms  Families.

The  plight  of two  families  illustrates  the  consequences
of  the  District’s  racial  classification  scheme  for  many
Jill  Kurfirst  and  Winnie  Bachwitz  are
families  in  Seattle.
members  of  Parents.
Each  has  a  child  who  entered  high
school  in  the  2000-01  school  year,  with  plans  to  attend
college.  JA  179,  185.  Like  many  parents,  it was  important
to them  that  their  children  attend  a school  close  to home  that
offered  a  variety  of college  preparatory  courses,  graduated
students  with  high scores  on  standardized  tests,  and  provided
an  environment  conducive  to learning.  JA  157-59,  185.

programs,

statistics,
